topics.title: Ideal and Real Gas Laws: Gas Constant, Calculations of Pressure, Volume and Temperature Using Ideal Gas Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT302 - FOOD THERMODYNAMICS AND PROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ideal and Real Gas Laws: Gas Constant, Calculations of Pressure, Volume and Temperature Using Ideal Gas Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Ideal Gas** is defined as a hypothetical gas whose molecules occupy negligible space and have no intermolecular forces of attraction or repulsion, and which obeys the **Ideal Gas Law** (also called the **Equation of State** for ideal gases) under all conditions of temperature and pressure.

The **Ideal Gas Law** is mathematically expressed as:

$$PV = nRT$$

where:
- **P** = Absolute pressure of the gas (Pa, atm, bar, or mmHg)
- **V** = Volume of the gas (m³, L, or cm³)
- **n** = Number of moles of the gas (mol or kgmol)
- **R** = Universal Gas Constant
- **T** = Absolute temperature (K or °R)

A **Real Gas** is a gas that does not obey the ideal gas law under all conditions, especially at **high pressure** and **low temperature**, where intermolecular forces and the finite volume of molecules become significant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The course PCFTT302 explicitly requires students to master:
> 1. The concept of ideal and real gases.
> 2. The universal and individual gas constants and their numerical values in **SI, CGS, and Engineering units**.
> 3. Solving numerical problems for P, V, T, n, and R using the ideal gas equation.
> 4. Understanding the deviation of real gases using the **Compressibility Factor (Z)** and the **Van der Waals Equation**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bumper Car" Analogy:**
> 
> Imagine a room full of people in **bumper cars** at a huge amusement park.
> - **Ideal Gas Analogy:** The bumper cars are infinitely small (point-sized), they never bump into each other, and there is no friction between them. They zoom around independently. This is what an **ideal gas** looks like at the molecular level.
> - **Real Gas Analogy:** Now, imagine the bumper cars have **actual size** and the drivers are **talking to each other** through walkie-talkies. Sometimes the cars stick together briefly (attraction) or push each other away (repulsion). This extra "talking and size" changes their behavior. This is what a **real gas** behaves like.
> 
> At **low pressure** and **high temperature**, molecules are far apart and move fast — behaving almost like bumper cars in a giant empty hall (ideal gas). At **high pressure** and **low temperature**, molecules crowd together and the walkie-talkie effect dominates (real gas).

### 1.3 Universal Gas Constant (R) — Standard Values

The **Universal Gas Constant** is one of the most fundamental physical constants in thermodynamics. It connects the macroscopic properties of a gas (P, V, T) to the microscopic number of particles (n).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Gas Constant (R) in Multiple Units (Mandatory for KTU Numerical Solving):**
> - **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**  (SI Units)
> - **R = 0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K)**  (CGS-Volume Units)
> - **R = 1.987 cal/(mol·K)**
> - **R = 8.314 kPa·m³/(kmol·K)**
> - **R = 10.73 psia·ft³/(lbmol·°R)**  (American Engineering Units)
> - **R = 0.7302 ft³·atm/(lbmol·°R)**
> - **R = 82.06 cm³·atm/(mol·K)**

### 1.4 Individual / Specific Gas Constant (R_specific)

Unlike the universal gas constant (R), the **Individual Gas Constant** depends on the molecular weight (M) of the gas:

$$R_{specific} = \frac{R_{universal}}{M}$$

where **M** is the molecular weight in kg/kmol or g/mol.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does R_specific matter in Food Engineering?**
> In food processing units like retorts, dryers, and cryogenic freezers, we deal with specific gases like **CO₂, O₂, N₂, NH₃, and water vapor**. Using R_specific makes calculations faster without converting mass to moles repeatedly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Postulates of the Kinetic Theory of an Ideal Gas

The ideal gas model is built on five fundamental postulates. Any deviation from these postulates results in **non-ideal (real) gas behavior**:

1. The gas consists of a **very large number of identical tiny particles** (molecules).
2. The gas molecules are in **continuous random motion** with high velocities.
3. The volume occupied by the molecules themselves is **negligible** compared to the total volume of the gas.
4. There are **no intermolecular forces** (attraction or repulsion) between molecules, except during instantaneous elastic collisions.
5. All collisions between molecules and with the container walls are **perfectly elastic** (no net loss of kinetic energy).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Postulates:**
> These postulates are simplifications. In reality, every molecule has size, and gases do have weak intermolecular forces (Van der Waals forces). However, when the **mean free path** (average distance between collisions) is much larger than molecular size, the gas behaves ideally.

### 2.2 Derivation of the Ideal Gas Law from the Combined Gas Law

The ideal gas law is the unified form of three classical gas laws (Boyle's, Charles's, and Avogadro's Laws):

**Boyle's Law (P-V relationship at constant T):**
$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$$

**Charles's Law (V-T relationship at constant P):**
$$\frac{V_1}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2}$$

**Avogadro's Law (V-n relationship at constant P, T):**
$$\frac{V_1}{n_1} = \frac{V_2}{n_2}$$

Combining all three: $\dfrac{PV}{nT} = \text{constant} = R$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the most-tested formula set in KTU Module 1. Memorize all entries.**

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ideal Gas Law (Moles) | $PV = nRT$ | P[Pa], V[m³], n[mol], R[8.314 J/mol·K], T[K] | Standard form using moles |
| Ideal Gas Law (Mass) | $PV = mR_{specific}T$ | m[kg], R_specific[J/kg·K] | Direct mass-based problems |
| Universal to Specific R | $R_{specific} = R/M$ | M[kg/kmol] | Convert R for specific gases |
| Moles from Mass | $n = m/M$ | m[kg], M[kg/kmol] | Required in P-V-n-T problems |
| Standard Molar Volume | $V_m = 22.414$ L/mol at STP | At 273.15 K, 1 atm | Quick volume conversion at STP |
| STP Conditions | T = 273.15 K, P = 101.325 kPa | Standard reference | Reference for IGL problems |
| NTP Conditions | T = 293.15 K, P = 101.325 kPa | Slight variation | Some textbooks use NTP |
| Compressibility Factor | $Z = PV/(nRT)$ | Z = 1 (ideal), Z ≠ 1 (real) | Measure of deviation |
| Van der Waals Eq. | $(P + a n^2/V^2)(V - nb) = nRT$ | a, b are gas-specific | Real gas correction |
| Partial Pressure (Dalton) | $P_{total} = \sum P_i$ | P_i = partial pressure | Gas mixture problems |
| Partial Volume (Amagat) | $V_{total} = \sum V_i$ | V_i = partial volume | Volume-based mixture |
| Mole Fraction | $y_i = n_i/n_{total}$ | y_i in component i | Required in gas mixtures |
| Ideal Gas Mixture | $PV = n_{total} R T$ | Use total moles | Mixture P-V-T |

### 2.4 Real Gas Laws — When Ideal Gas Law Fails

The **Compressibility Factor (Z)** is the most important concept for real gases:

$$Z = \frac{PV}{nRT}$$

- **Z = 1** → Ideal gas behavior.
- **Z < 1** → Attractive forces dominate (e.g., at low temperature, gases like O₂, N₂). Molecules pull each other closer, reducing volume.
- **Z > 1** → Repulsive forces dominate (e.g., at high pressure, gases like H₂, He). Molecular volume becomes significant.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility of Z:**
> In the food industry, gases like **CO₂ (used in Modified Atmosphere Packaging)** and **NH₃ (used in refrigeration)** deviate significantly from ideal behavior. The compressibility factor allows accurate design of storage tanks, pipelines, and pressure vessels.

### 2.5 Van der Waals Equation (Real Gas)

For a real gas, Van der Waals introduced two corrections to the ideal gas law:

$$\left(P + \frac{a n^2}{V^2}\right)(V - nb) = nRT$$

where:
- **a** = constant accounting for **intermolecular attractive forces** (Pa·m⁶/mol²).
- **b** = constant accounting for the **finite volume** of molecules (m³/mol).
- The term $a n^2/V^2$ corrects for attractive forces, **increasing effective pressure**.
- The term $nb$ corrects for molecular volume, **reducing available volume**.

### 2.6 Dalton's Law and Amagat's Law (Gas Mixtures)

For a **mixture of ideal gases**, two equivalent formulations exist:

**Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures:**
$$P_{total} = P_A + P_B + P_C + ... = \sum_i P_i$$

**Amagat's Law of Partial Volumes:**
$$V_{total} = V_A + V_B + V_C + ... = \sum_i V_i$$

Both lead to the **ideal gas mixture equation**:

$$PV_{total} = n_{total} R T$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Boyle's, Charles's, and Avogadro's Laws from Kinetic Theory

The kinetic theory of gases derives the ideal gas law by considering molecules colliding with container walls. The pressure exerted by a gas is the rate of change of momentum per unit area of the wall.

**Step 1:** The average kinetic energy of one molecule is given by:
$$KE_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} m \bar{v^2}$$

where $m$ is the mass of one molecule and $\bar{v^2}$ is the mean square velocity.

**Step 2:** The total kinetic energy of $N$ molecules is:
$$KE_{total} = N \cdot KE_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} N m \bar{v^2}$$

**Step 3:** From kinetic theory, the pressure is related to the kinetic energy by:
$$PV = \frac{1}{3} N m \bar{v^2}$$

**Step 4:** Combining with the total kinetic energy expression:
$$PV = \frac{2}{3} \cdot KE_{total}$$

**Step 5:** Since temperature is directly proportional to average kinetic energy:
$$KE_{total} = \frac{3}{2} N k_B T$$

where $k_B$ is the **Boltzmann Constant** = $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.

**Step 6:** Substituting back:
$$PV = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{2} N k_B T = N k_B T$$

**Step 7:** Since $N = n \cdot N_A$ (where $N_A$ is Avogadro's number) and $k_B \cdot N_A = R$:
$$PV = n R N_A k_B \frac{T}{N_A N_A} = nRT$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the rigorous kinetic-theory derivation of the ideal gas law from first principles.

### 3.2 Worked Derivations: Computing P, V, T Using Ideal Gas Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Numerical Solving:**
> **ALWAYS** convert to **absolute units** before plugging into $PV = nRT$.
> - Pressure: Convert to **Pa (or kPa)** or **atm**.
> - Volume: Convert to **m³ (or L)**.
> - Temperature: Convert to **Kelvin** (T_K = T_°C + 273.15).

**Worked Example 1: Finding Pressure**

A food packaging chamber contains 0.5 mol of N₂ at 27 °C in a 0.01 m³ vessel. Calculate the pressure in kPa.

**Solution:**

$$\text{Step 1: Convert temperature to Kelvin}$$
$$T = 27 + 273.15 = 300.15 \, \text{K}$$

$$\text{Step 2: Write the ideal gas law in terms of P}$$
$$P = \frac{nRT}{V}$$

$$\text{Step 3: Substitute values (R = 8.314 J/mol·K = 8.314 kPa·m³/kmol·K)}$$

$$P = \frac{(0.5 \, \text{mol}) \times (8.314 \, \text{J/mol·K}) \times (300.15 \, \text{K})}{0.01 \, \text{m}^3}$$

$$P = \frac{0.5 \times 8.314 \times 300.15}{0.01}$$

$$P = \frac{1247.6}{0.01}$$

$$P = 124763.55 \, \text{Pa} = 124.76 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Worked Example 2: Finding Volume**

Calculate the volume occupied by 2 kg of CO₂ at 50 °C and 200 kPa. (M_CO₂ = 44 kg/kmol)

**Solution:**

$$\text{Step 1: Calculate moles}$$
$$n = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{2 \, \text{kg}}{44 \, \text{kg/kmol}} = 0.04545 \, \text{kmol}$$

$$\text{Step 2: Convert temperature}$$
$$T = 50 + 273.15 = 323.15 \, \text{K}$$

$$\text{Step 3: Solve for V}$$
$$V = \frac{nRT}{P} = \frac{(0.04545 \, \text{kmol}) \times (8.314 \, \text{kPa·m}^3/\text{kmol·K}) \times (323.15 \, \text{K})}{200 \, \text{kPa}}$$

$$V = \frac{122.13}{200} = 0.6107 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Worked Example 3: Finding Temperature**

A 0.05 m³ cylinder contains 1.2 mol of O₂ at a pressure of 500 kPa. Find the temperature in °C. (M_O₂ = 32 kg/kmol)

**Solution:**

$$\text{Step 1: Solve for T}$$
$$T = \frac{PV}{nR}$$

$$\text{Step 2: Substitute values}$$
$$T = \frac{(500 \times 10^3 \, \text{Pa}) \times (0.05 \, \text{m}^3)}{(1.2 \, \text{mol}) \times (8.314 \, \text{J/mol·K})}$$

$$T = \frac{25000}{9.9768} = 2505.85 \, \text{K}$$

$$\text{Step 3: Convert to Celsius}$$
$$T_{°C} = 2505.85 - 273.15 = 2232.7 \, °C$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Numerical Problem Categories

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following matrix maps problem types to formulas and pitfalls.**

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Formula | Common KTU Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Find P | n, V, T | P | $P = nRT/V$ | Forgetting to convert T to Kelvin |
| Find V | n, P, T | V | $V = nRT/P$ | Mixing L and m³ units |
| Find T | n, P, V | T | $T = PV/nR$ | Returning T in °C instead of K |
| Find n | P, V, T | n | $n = PV/RT$ | Using wrong R value for the units |
| Gas Mixture | n_A, n_B, P, T | P_total | $P = (n_A + n_B)RT/V$ | Forgetting Dalton's Law |
| Real Gas | P, V, T, a, b | n | Solve Van der Waals | Iterative solving required |

### 3.4 Detailed Derivation: Compressibility Factor from Van der Waals

Starting from Van der Waals equation:
$$\left(P + \frac{a}{V_m^2}\right)(V_m - b) = RT$$

Rearranging for $P$:
$$P = \frac{RT}{V_m - b} - \frac{a}{V_m^2}$$

Substituting into the definition of $Z = PV_m / (RT)$:
$$Z = \frac{V_m}{V_m - b} - \frac{a}{RT \cdot V_m}$$

This shows that:
- The **b** term (molecular volume) makes $Z > 1$ (repulsion).
- The **a** term (attraction) makes $Z < 1$ (attraction).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility of Van der Waals:**
> In high-pressure food processing like **High-Pressure Processing (HPP)** and **supercritical CO₂ extraction** (used to decaffeinate coffee), ideal gas assumptions fail dramatically. Van der Waals and similar equations of state are essential for accurate equipment design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture Flow — Ideal Gas Law Ecosystem

The following Mermaid diagram maps the conceptual and mathematical relationships between all sub-topics of this Module 1 content, providing a complete overview of how the ideal gas law connects to its components and extensions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GAS STATE VARIABLES] --> B[Pressure P]
    A --> C[Volume V]
    A --> D[Temperature T]
    A --> E[Amount of Substance n]
    
    F[IDEAL GAS LAW] --> G["PV = nRT"]
    
    B --> G
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    
    H[GAS CONSTANTS] --> I[Universal R]
    H --> J[Specific Rspecific]
    I --> K["8.314 J/molK"]
    J --> L["R / M"]
    
    G --> M[CALCULATIONS]
    M --> N[Find P]
    M --> O[Find V]
    M --> P[Find T]
    M --> Q[Find n]
    
    R[REAL GAS DEVIATIONS] --> S[Compressibility Z]
    S --> T["Z = PV / nRT"]
    T --> U["Z = 1 Ideal"]
    T --> V["Z less than 1 Attraction"]
    T --> W["Z greater than 1 Repulsion"]
    
    X[REAL GAS MODELS] --> Y[Van der Waals]
    Y --> Z2["(P + a n2/V2)(V - nb) = nRT"]
    
    AA[GAS MIXTURES] --> AB[Daltons Law]
    AA --> AC[Amagats Law]
    AB --> AD[Ptotal = Sum Pi]
    AC --> AE[Vtotal = Sum Vi]
    
    G --> S
    G --> Y
    G --> AA
    
    F --> H
    F --> R
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Computational Decision Tree for KTU Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start KTU Problem] --> B{Real or Ideal Gas?}
    B -->|Ideal| C{Which Variable to Find?}
    B -->|Real| D{Which EOS?}
    
    C -->|P| E["P = nRT / V"]
    C -->|V| F["V = nRT / P"]
    C -->|T| G["T = PV / nR"]
    C -->|n| H["n = PV / RT"]
    
    D -->|Van der Waals| I["Solve VDW Iteratively"]
    D -->|Compressibility| J["Use Z Charts or EOS"]
    
    E --> K[Check Unit Consistency]
    F --> K
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    
    K --> L{All Units SI or Consistent?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Compute Numerical Answer]
    L -->|No| N[Convert Units First]
    N --> M
    
    M --> O[Final KTU Answer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams for the exam:**
> The first diagram shows that all of Module 1's topics (ideal gas law, real gases, mixtures) are interconnected through the **state variables** (P, V, T, n). The second diagram is a practical **decision tree** that you should mentally walk through whenever a numerical problem appears in the exam — it prevents unit mistakes, which is the #1 cause of mark loss.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
Define the **Universal Gas Constant (R)**. State its value in **SI units, CGS units, and Engineering (FPS) units**.

**Model Answer:**

The **Universal Gas Constant (R)** is the constant of proportionality in the ideal gas equation $PV = nRT$ that relates the pressure, volume, and absolute temperature of one mole of an ideal gas. It is numerically equal to the Boltzmann constant multiplied by Avogadro's number.

| Unit System | Numerical Value |
|---|---|
| SI Units | **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| CGS Units | **R = 0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K)** |
| Engineering (FPS) | **R = 10.73 psia·ft³/(lbmol·°R)** |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
Distinguish between an **ideal gas** and a **real gas**. Why does a real gas deviate from ideal behavior at high pressures and low temperatures?

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Ideal Gas | Real Gas |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular volume | Negligible | Finite, non-zero |
| Intermolecular forces | None (no attraction/repulsion) | Significant at close range |
| Behavior at all P, T | Strictly obeys $PV = nRT$ | Deviates at high P, low T |
| Compressibility Z | Exactly 1 | Not equal to 1 |

**Reason for Deviation:**
At **high pressure**, molecules are forced close together, making the **finite molecular volume** significant and increasing repulsive forces. At **low temperature**, molecules move slowly, so **intermolecular attractive forces** dominate and pull molecules closer than predicted by the ideal gas law. Both effects cause the actual P-V-T behavior to deviate from $PV = nRT$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Style]

**(a)** A 0.25 m³ gas cylinder used in a food packaging unit contains O₂ at 200 kPa and 25 °C. Calculate:
   (i) The number of moles of O₂ present.
   (ii) The mass of O₂ in kg. (M_O₂ = 32 kg/kmol)
   (iii) The pressure if the temperature rises to 60 °C at constant volume.

**(b)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the **Kinetic Theory of Gases** and derive the **Ideal Gas Equation** from kinetic postulates.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**
- V = 0.25 m³
- P₁ = 200 kPa = 200,000 Pa
- T₁ = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 K
- R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)

**(i) Number of moles of O₂:**

$$n = \frac{P_1 V}{R T_1} = \frac{(200 \times 10^3 \, \text{Pa}) \times (0.25 \, \text{m}^3)}{(8.314 \, \text{J/mol·K}) \times (298.15 \, \text{K})}$$

$$n = \frac{50000}{2478.8} = 20.17 \, \text{mol}$$

**(ii) Mass of O₂:**

$$m = n \times M = 20.17 \, \text{mol} \times 0.032 \, \text{kg/mol} = 0.6454 \, \text{kg}$$

**(iii) New pressure at T₂ = 60 °C = 333.15 K (constant V, n):**

Using Gay-Lussac's Law:
$$\frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2}$$

$$P_2 = P_1 \times \frac{T_2}{T_1} = 200 \times \frac{333.15}{298.15} = 200 \times 1.1174 = 223.49 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

The **Kinetic Theory of Gases** postulates the following:
1. A gas is composed of a very large number of molecules in random motion.
2. The volume of the molecules is negligible compared to the gas volume.
3. No intermolecular forces act except during collisions.
4. All collisions are perfectly elastic.

**Derivation of $PV = \dfrac{1}{3} m N \bar{c^2}$:**

Consider a single molecule of mass $m$ moving with velocity components $c_x, c_y, c_z$ in a cubic container of side $L$. The momentum change on hitting a wall perpendicular to the x-axis is:

$$\Delta p_x = 2 m c_x$$

Time between successive collisions on the same wall = $2L / c_x$. Force exerted by this molecule on the wall:

$$F_1 = \frac{\Delta p_x}{\Delta t} = \frac{2 m c_x}{2L/c_x} = \frac{m c_x^2}{L}$$

For $N$ molecules, total force on the wall = $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{m c_{x,i}^2}{L}$

Pressure $P = \frac{\text{Force}}{\text{Area}} = \frac{F}{L^2} = \frac{m \sum c_{x,i}^2}{N L^3} = \frac{m N \bar{c_x^2}}{N V}$

By symmetry, $\bar{c_x^2} = \bar{c_y^2} = \bar{c_z^2} = \frac{1}{3} \bar{c^2}$

Therefore: $PV = \dfrac{1}{3} m N \bar{c^2}$

Combining with $\bar{KE} = \dfrac{1}{2} m \bar{c^2}$ and $\bar{KE} = \dfrac{3}{2} k_B T$:

$$PV = N k_B T = n R T$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Style]

**(a)** A gas mixture in a modified atmosphere food package contains 0.4 mol of N₂, 0.2 mol of O₂, and 0.1 mol of CO₂ in a 0.02 m³ container at 25 °C. Calculate:
   (i) The total pressure of the mixture.
   (ii) The partial pressure of each component.
   (iii) The mole fraction of each gas.

**(b)** Explain the **Van der Waals Equation** for real gases. What do the constants **'a'** and **'b'** represent physically? Why are they necessary corrections to the ideal gas law?

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Given Data:**
- n_N₂ = 0.4 mol, n_O₂ = 0.2 mol, n_CO₂ = 0.1 mol
- V = 0.02 m³
- T = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 K
- R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)

**(i) Total Pressure:**

Total moles: $n_{total} = 0.4 + 0.2 + 0.1 = 0.7 \, \text{mol}$

$$P_{total} = \frac{n_{total} R T}{V} = \frac{(0.7) \times (8.314) \times (298.15)}{0.02}$$

$$P_{total} = \frac{1735.10}{0.02} = 86755.1 \, \text{Pa} = 86.76 \, \text{kPa}$$

**(ii) Partial Pressures (Dalton's Law):**

$$P_{N_2} = y_{N_2} \times P_{total} = \frac{0.4}{0.7} \times 86.76 = 49.58 \, \text{kPa}$$

$$P_{O_2} = \frac{0.2}{0.7} \times 86.76 = 24.79 \, \text{kPa}$$

$$P_{CO_2} = \frac{0.1}{0.7} \times 86.76 = 12.39 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Verification:** $49.58 + 24.79 + 12.39 = 86.76 \, \text{kPa}$ ✓

**(iii) Mole Fractions:**

$$y_{N_2} = \frac{0.4}{0.7} = 0.5714$$

$$y_{O_2} = \frac{0.2}{0.7} = 0.2857$$

$$y_{CO_2} = \frac{0.1}{0.7} = 0.1429$$

**Sum check:** $0.5714 + 0.2857 + 0.1429 = 1.0000$ ✓

**Model Solution to (b):**

The **Van der Waals Equation** modifies the ideal gas law to account for two real-gas effects:

$$\left(P + \frac{a n^2}{V^2}\right)(V - nb) = nRT$$

**Physical meaning of 'a' and 'b':**

| Constant | Physical Meaning | Effect on Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **a** | Magnitude of **attractive** forces between molecules | Corrects pressure upward: $P_{actual} = P_{ideal} - a/V_m^2$ |
| **b** | **Excluded volume** per mole (four times molecular volume) | Corrects volume downward: $V_{available} = V - nb$ |

**Why the corrections are necessary:**

In the ideal gas law, two unrealistic assumptions are made: (1) molecules have no volume, and (2) molecules do not attract each other. In reality, every molecule has a finite size, so the **free space** available for molecular motion is less than the container volume by an amount $nb$. Furthermore, attractive forces between molecules reduce the **force** with which molecules hit the walls, making the actual pressure lower than the ideal pressure by an amount $a n^2 / V^2$. The Van der Waals equation accounts for both, making it valid over a wider range of P and T than the ideal gas law.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversion is the #1 Killer:** Students often plug in temperature in °C instead of Kelvin and lose **3 to 4 marks** instantly. Always: **T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15**.
> 
> 2. **Wrong Value of R:** Using R = 8.314 with volume in liters gives an answer off by a factor of 1000. Match R to the unit system: SI (m³, Pa), CGS (L, atm), or Engineering (ft³, psia).
> 
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert Pa to kPa or vice versa:** Especially when the problem gives pressure in kPa but expects answer in Pa or vice versa.
> 
> 4. **Using mass instead of moles:** For $PV = nRT$, you MUST use moles. Convert mass using $n = m/M$ with M in kg/kmol or g/mol matching the unit system.
> 
> 5. **In Partial Pressure questions:** Forgetting to **sum partial pressures** as a final check — examiners give 1 mark for verification.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up definitions of 'a' and 'b':** 'a' = attraction, 'b' = volume. Reversing them in Van der Waals derivations is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic: Ideal and Real Gas Laws**

**Core Equations to Memorize:**

- ✅ Ideal Gas Law (moles): $PV = nRT$
- ✅ Ideal Gas Law (mass): $PV = m R_{specific} T$
- ✅ Individual Gas Constant: $R_{specific} = R/M$
- ✅ Moles from mass: $n = m/M$
- ✅ Compressibility Factor: $Z = PV/(nRT)$
- ✅ Van der Waals Equation: $(P + a n^2/V^2)(V - nb) = nRT$
- ✅ Dalton's Law: $P_{total} = \sum P_i$
- ✅ Amagat's Law: $V_{total} = \sum V_i$
- ✅ Standard Molar Volume: $V_m = 22.414$ L/mol at STP (273.15 K, 1 atm)

**Universal Gas Constant — Top 3 Values to Memorize:**

- ✅ **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**  (SI — most common in KTU)
- ✅ **R = 0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K)**  (For CGS-volume problems)
- ✅ **R = 10.73 psia·ft³/(lbmol·°R)**  (For engineering problems)

**Five Postulates of Kinetic Theory of Ideal Gas:**

- ✅ Molecules are point particles (negligible volume)
- ✅ Continuous random motion
- ✅ No intermolecular forces
- ✅ Perfectly elastic collisions
- ✅ Large number of molecules, average behavior applies

**Conditions Where Real Gases Behave Ideally:**

- ✅ **Low pressure** (molecules far apart)
- ✅ **High temperature** (kinetic energy overcomes attractive forces)

**Conditions Where Real Gases Deviate Most:**

- ✅ **High pressure** (molecular volume becomes significant)
- ✅ **Low temperature** (intermolecular forces dominate)

**Physical Meaning of Van der Waals Constants:**

- ✅ **a** = Attractive force constant (corrects pressure)
- ✅ **b** = Molecular volume constant (corrects volume)

**Compressibility Factor Quick Reference:**

- ✅ Z = 1 → Ideal gas
- ✅ Z < 1 → Attraction dominates (low T, moderate P)
- ✅ Z > 1 → Repulsion dominates (high P)

**Common Food Engineering Applications:**

- ✅ Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) — gas mixtures
- ✅ Refrigeration cycles — ammonia, CO₂
- ✅ High-Pressure Processing (HPP) — real gas behavior
- ✅ Supercritical fluid extraction (e.g., coffee decaffeination)
- ✅ Spray drying — hot air as ideal gas approximation
- ✅ Freeze-drying (lyophilization) — water vapor behavior

**Key Exam Strategy:**

- ✅ Always convert T to Kelvin FIRST.
- ✅ Match R to the unit system being used.
- ✅ Show all unit conversions explicitly for partial credit.
- ✅ Verify using physical intuition (e.g., heating a gas should increase P).
- ✅ For gas mixtures, calculate $n_{total}$ first, then apply ideal gas law.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Ideal and Real Gas Laws**
**Course: PCFTT302 - Food Thermodynamics and Process Calculations**
**KTU 2024 Scheme | B.Tech (Food Technology / allied branches)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Humidity: Absolute, Molal, Relative, and Percentage Humidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT302 - FOOD THERMODYNAMICS AND PROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Humidity: Absolute, Molal, Relative, and Percentage Humidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Humidity?

In **Food Thermodynamics and Process Calculations**, the term *Humidity* refers to the quantification of water vapour present in a gaseous mixture (almost always air). Because food processing operations — drying, humidification, dehumidification, evaporation, conditioning of storage atmospheres, and psychrometric analysis — all involve the behaviour of water vapour in air, a rigorous and unambiguous definition of "moisture content of a gas" is essential.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** *Humidity* is a general term that broadly refers to the amount of water vapour present in a gas. Since it is a general (qualitative) term, it is quantified using several specific numerical descriptors: **Absolute Humidity**, **Molal Humidity**, **Relative Humidity**, and **Percentage Humidity**.

### 1.2 The Four Specific Humidity Parameters

A single "humidity" value is not enough — depending on whether the engineer cares about mass balance, energy balance, equilibrium, or drying potential, a different descriptor is most useful. The four parameters covered in Module 1 are:

1. **Absolute Humidity (Humidity, H)** — mass of water per unit mass of *dry* gas.
2. **Molal Humidity (Molal Absolute Humidity, Hₘ)** — moles of water per mole of *dry* gas.
3. **Relative Humidity (RH, φ)** — partial pressure of water vapour divided by its saturation pressure at the same temperature.
4. **Percentage Humidity (μ)** — actual molal humidity divided by the saturation molal humidity at the gas temperature, expressed as a percentage.

> [!NOTE]
> The *dry gas* basis (i.e. the mass or moles of **bone-dry** air excluding any water vapour) is the standard reference frame in psychrometry. This is because the amount of water vapour can change from point to point, but the amount of dry gas stays constant (it acts as an inert carrier).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea Cup Carrier" Intuition

Imagine a busy Kerala tea stall in the morning. The **waiter** is the *dry air* — he never changes, never gets consumed, and just keeps carrying things. The **tea cups** he carries are the *water vapour* — they come and go depending on demand.

- **Absolute Humidity** = "How heavy is the load on the waiter's tray right now?" (mass of tea cups ÷ mass of waiter).
- **Molal Humidity** = "How many tea cups is the waiter carrying, mole-wise?" (moles of tea ÷ moles of waiter).
- **Relative Humidity** = "How full is the waiter's tray *compared to the maximum he could legally carry* at the current temperature?" If the tray is rated for 10 cups and he is carrying 7, RH = 70 %.
- **Percentage Humidity** = A "shortcut version" of Relative Humidity that uses molal quantities instead of pressures — useful when we don't have the vapour-pressure tables handy.

This analogy makes it clear why humidity is **always relative to the dry gas** — the waiter (dry gas) is the constant reference, not the total tray load.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following constants/assumptions are routinely used in KTU calculations on this topic:

- **Molecular weight of water (Mᵥ)** = **18.015 kg/kmol**
- **Molecular weight of dry air (Mₐ)** = **28.97 kg/kmol**
- **Universal Gas Constant, R** = **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** or **8314 J/(kmol·K)**
- **Standard atmospheric pressure, P** = **101.325 kPa** (used unless otherwise stated)
- All partial pressures of water vapour must be in the same units as the total pressure for the percentage to be dimensionally consistent.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Setting Up the Problem — The Dry-Basis Concept

Consider a mixture of dry air (subscript *a*) and water vapour (subscript *v*) at total pressure **P** and temperature **T**.

- $n_a$ = moles of dry air
- $n_v$ = moles of water vapour
- $m_a$ = mass of dry air
- $m_v$ = mass of water vapour
- $p_a$ = partial pressure of dry air
- $p_v$ = partial pressure of water vapour

By **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures**:

$$P = p_a + p_v$$

Because the dry-gas mass ($m_a$ or $n_a$) is invariant as water is added or removed, **every humidity definition is referenced to the dry gas**, not to the total mixture.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Humidity Type

### (A) Absolute Humidity (H)

- **Step 1:** Identify the mass of water vapour present in a sample.
- **Step 2:** Identify the mass of dry air in the *same* sample.
- **Step 3:** Take their ratio: $H = m_v / m_a$.
- **Why this works:** The ratio is independent of the sample size — it is an *intensive* property. The dry-air reference means adding more dry air does not change *H*; only adding/ removing water changes it.
- **Typical units:** kg water / kg dry air, or g water / kg dry air.

### (B) Molal Humidity (Humidity Ratio on Molar Basis, Hₘ)

- **Step 1:** Identify moles of water vapour.
- **Step 2:** Identify moles of dry air.
- **Step 3:** Take the ratio: $H_m = n_v / n_a$.
- **Why useful:** The mole ratio enters naturally when the Ideal Gas Law is applied to each component separately. In a mixture at uniform T and P, partial pressures are proportional to mole fractions, so $H_m$ can also be written using partial pressures (see Section 3).

### (C) Relative Humidity (RH or φ)

- **Step 1:** Find the saturation pressure of water at the gas temperature, $P_s(T)$, from steam tables.
- **Step 2:** Find the actual partial pressure of water vapour, $p_v$.
- **Step 3:** Compute $\phi = p_v / P_s$.
- **Why useful:** RH tells us *how close the air is to being saturated* (i.e. dew point). It is the single most common humidity descriptor used in food storage specifications (e.g. "store at RH < 65 %").
- **Range:** $0 \le \phi \le 1$ (or 0 % to 100 %).

### (D) Percentage Humidity (μ)

- **Step 1:** Compute the actual molal humidity $H_m = p_v/(P - p_v)$.
- **Step 2:** Compute the *saturation* molal humidity at the same T: $H_{m,s} = P_s/(P - P_s)$.
- **Step 3:** Take the ratio and multiply by 100: $\mu = (H_m / H_{m,s}) \times 100$.
- **Why useful:** When $P$ and $P_s$ are very different, the partial-pressure ratio and the mole ratio differ. Percentage Humidity is a "mole-based" parallel to RH and is the form that appears in classic humidification/dehumidification operation charts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for KTU problems:** For most food-processing conditions, $p_v \ll P$ and $P_s \ll P$, so $\phi \approx \mu/100$. The two are **not exactly equal** in general — the difference becomes significant at high humidities or elevated temperatures.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Common Units | Notes / Range |
|---|----------|--------|---------|--------------|---------------|
| 1 | Absolute Humidity | $H$ | $H = m_v / m_a$ | kg water / kg dry air | $H \ge 0$ |
| 2 | Absolute Humidity (molar form equivalent) | $H$ | $H = (18.015/28.97)\, H_m$ | kg/kg | Uses $M_v / M_a$ |
| 3 | Molal Humidity | $H_m$ | $H_m = n_v / n_a$ | kmol water / kmol dry air | $H_m \ge 0$ |
| 4 | Molal Humidity (in terms of partial pressure) | $H_m$ | $H_m = p_v / (P - p_v)$ | kmol/kmol | From Dalton + ideal gas |
| 5 | Relative Humidity | $\phi$ | $\phi = p_v / P_s$ | dimensionless (0–1) | $P_s = P_s(T)$ |
| 6 | Percentage Humidity | $\mu$ | $\mu = (H_m / H_{m,s}) \times 100$ | % | $H_{m,s} = P_s/(P - P_s)$ |
| 7 | Relation between $\mu$ and $\phi$ | — | $\mu = 100 \cdot \dfrac{p_v (P - P_s)}{P_s (P - p_v)}$ | % | Exact; reduces to $\approx 100\phi$ for low $p_v$ |
| 8 | Saturation Molal Humidity | $H_{m,s}$ | $H_{m,s} = P_s / (P - P_s)$ | kmol/kmol | At temperature $T$ |
| 9 | Humid Volume (specific volume of moist air) | $v_H$ | $v_H = (RT/P)(1/M_a + H_m/M_v)$ | m³/kg dry air | Will be used in Module 1/2 |
| 10 | Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | $R = 8.314$ | kJ/(kmol·K) | SI value |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all formula-table rows above, the vertical-bar notation for absolute value is **not** used. Where a magnitude or absolute value is required, always use $\vert \cdot \vert$ **outside** the table or via inline math so that the markdown table syntax is not broken.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Where this concept is critical |
|---|---|
| **Food Drying** | Mass of water to be removed is calculated from $H$ before and after drying. |
| **Cold Storage / Warehousing** | RH specification directly controls whether free water condenses on product surfaces (mould risk). |
| **Spray Dryers, Fluidised-Bed Dryers** | Air inlet/outlet $H$ values determine the moisture balance and energy demand. |
| **Atmospheric Control in Packaging** | Equilibrium Relative Humidity (ERH) is a key shelf-life parameter. |
| **Air-Conditioning of Process Halls** | Psychrometric charts are built entirely on these four humidity descriptors. |
| **Evaporators and Crystallisers** | Humidity of the exit air stream dictates water loss to the surroundings. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Molal Humidity in Terms of Partial Pressures

We begin with the *definition* and apply the Ideal Gas Law separately to each component of the moist-air mixture at temperature $T$ and total pressure $P$.

**Step 1 — Write the ideal-gas expressions** for water vapour and dry air in their own volumes of the mixture.

$$p_v V = n_v R T \quad \text{and} \quad p_a V = n_a R T$$

**Step 2 — Divide the water-vapour equation by the dry-air equation** (the $V$, $R$, $T$ all cancel):

$$\frac{p_v}{p_a} = \frac{n_v}{n_a} = H_m$$

**Step 3 — Use Dalton's Law to eliminate $p_a$** by writing $p_a = P - p_v$:

$$H_m = \frac{p_v}{P - p_v}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on units.** The result is a ratio of two pressures (kmol/kmol) or two mole numbers. At low humidities $p_v \ll P$, so $H_m \approx p_v / P$, which is *approximately* the mole fraction of water vapour. The equality is *exact* only in this derivation.

> **Logic of conversion:** The trick is to recognise that the **same volume, same temperature, same R** applies to both gases in the mixture, so when we take a ratio of partial pressures we directly obtain a ratio of moles — i.e. the molal humidity.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Absolute Humidity in Terms of Molal Humidity

We want to go from *moles per mole* to *mass per mass*.

**Step 1 — Express the masses using molecular weights:**

$$m_v = n_v M_v \quad \text{and} \quad m_a = n_a M_a$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the definition of $H$:**

$$H = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{n_v M_v}{n_a M_a} = \frac{n_v}{n_a} \cdot \frac{M_v}{M_a}$$

**Step 3 — Recognise the first factor as $H_m$:**

$$H = H_m \cdot \frac{M_v}{M_a}$$

**Step 4 — Insert numerical values** $M_v = 18.015$ kg/kmol and $M_a = 28.97$ kg/kmol:

$$\frac{M_v}{M_a} = \frac{18.015}{28.97} \approx 0.622$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form:**

$$H = 0.622 \, H_m$$

This dimensionless constant **0.622** is one of the most-quoted numbers in psychrometry and will appear repeatedly in KTU problems.

> **Logic of conversion:** Going from mole-basis to mass-basis simply requires multiplying by the ratio of molecular weights. The constant 0.622 is *less than 1* because water is a *lighter* molecule than the average air molecule (mostly N₂ and O₂).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Exact Relationship Between Percentage Humidity and Relative Humidity

**Step 1 — Write the molal humidity at the actual state** and at **saturation** for the same temperature:

$$H_m = \frac{p_v}{P - p_v}, \qquad H_{m,s} = \frac{P_s}{P - P_s}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the definition of Percentage Humidity:**

$$\mu = 100 \cdot \frac{H_m}{H_{m,s}} = 100 \cdot \frac{p_v / (P - p_v)}{P_s / (P - P_s)}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply the denominators:**

$$\mu = 100 \cdot \frac{p_v \, (P - P_s)}{P_s \, (P - p_v)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $p_v = \phi P_s$ from the definition of RH:**

$$\mu = 100 \cdot \frac{\phi P_s \, (P - P_s)}{P_s \, (P - \phi P_s)}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $P_s$ and simplify:**

$$\mu = 100 \cdot \frac{\phi \, (P - P_s)}{P - \phi P_s}$$

**Step 6 — Examine the limiting behaviour** when $P_s \ll P$ and $\phi P_s \ll P$ (low-temperature, low-RH food processing air):

$$P - P_s \approx P, \qquad P - \phi P_s \approx P$$

Therefore, in this limit:

$$\mu \approx 100 \cdot \phi \cdot \frac{P}{P} = 100 \, \phi$$

i.e. **Percentage Humidity (in %) is numerically equal to Relative Humidity (as a fraction) × 100** — the two are often used interchangeably in the food industry at moderate conditions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Demonstration (KTU Style)

**Problem (Illustrative):** Air at total pressure $P = 101.325$ kPa and temperature $T = 30 \,^\circ$C has a partial pressure of water vapour $p_v = 3.0$ kPa. The saturation pressure of water at 30 °C is $P_s = 4.246$ kPa. Compute: (i) Relative Humidity, (ii) Percentage Humidity, (iii) Molal Humidity, (iv) Absolute Humidity.

### (i) Relative Humidity

$$\phi = \frac{p_v}{P_s} = \frac{3.0}{4.246} = 0.7065 = 70.65 \, \%$$

### (ii) Percentage Humidity

**Step 1 — Molal humidity (actual):**

$$H_m = \frac{p_v}{P - p_v} = \frac{3.0}{101.325 - 3.0} = \frac{3.0}{98.325} = 0.03051 \text{ kmol/kmol}$$

**Step 2 — Molal humidity (saturation):**

$$H_{m,s} = \frac{P_s}{P - P_s} = \frac{4.246}{101.325 - 4.246} = \frac{4.246}{97.079} = 0.04374 \text{ kmol/kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Humidity:**

$$\mu = 100 \cdot \frac{H_m}{H_{m,s}} = 100 \cdot \frac{0.03051}{0.04374} = 69.75 \, \%$$

> Note: $\mu = 69.75\,\%$ is **slightly less** than $\phi = 70.65\,\%$ — confirming the theoretical inequality for this non-idealised condition.

### (iii) Molal Humidity

Already computed in (ii): $H_m = 0.03051$ kmol water / kmol dry air.

### (iv) Absolute Humidity

Using $H = 0.622 \, H_m$:

$$H = 0.622 \times 0.03051 = 0.01898 \text{ kg water / kg dry air}$$

Or equivalently, **18.98 g water per kg of dry air** — a typical figure for warm humid tropical air.

> **Logic of conversion:** Each subsequent quantity is built upon the previous one. The "analytical matrix" approach (computing in the order $\phi \to H_m \to H \to \mu$) is the most efficient KTU-board-exam strategy because every quantity re-uses a previous intermediate.

## 3.5 Conversion Logic Summary Table

| Step | From → To | Operation | Formula |
|------|-----------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | $p_v, P, P_s$ → $\phi$ | Direct division | $\phi = p_v / P_s$ |
| 2 | $p_v, P$ → $H_m$ | Dalton substitution | $H_m = p_v / (P - p_v)$ |
| 3 | $P_s, P$ → $H_{m,s}$ | Same with $P_s$ | $H_{m,s} = P_s / (P - P_s)$ |
| 4 | $H_m, H_{m,s}$ → $\mu$ | Ratio × 100 | $\mu = 100 (H_m / H_{m,s})$ |
| 5 | $H_m$ → $H$ | Molecular-weight ratio | $H = 0.622 \, H_m$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Humidity Computations

The following diagram maps the *computational* and *physical* flow between the four humidity descriptors. It shows how raw inputs (pressures and temperature) propagate through the formulae to yield the four named outputs. The architecture is the same one an engineer follows when solving a KTU problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Inputs: Total Pressure P, Temperature T, Partial Pressure pv]
    A1[Steam Table Lookup: Ps at T] --> B1[Compute RH phi = pv / Ps]
    A0 --> B1
    A1 --> B2[Compute Saturation Molal Humidity Hms = Ps / P - Ps]
    A0 --> C1[Compute Molal Humidity Hm = pv / P - pv]
    C1 --> C2[Compute Absolute Humidity H = 0.622 x Hm]
    C1 --> D1[Compute Percentage Humidity mu = 100 x Hm / Hms]
    B2 --> D1
    B1 --> E1[Final Quantities: phi, Hm, H, mu]
    C2 --> E1
    D1 --> E1
    A0 --> E1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each block is a single algebraic step. Arrows show *data dependencies* (an arrow from A to B means "B needs A's value before it can be computed"). This is the same dependency tree you should sketch in the margin of your KTU answer booklet.

## 4.2 Decision Logic Diagram — Which Humidity to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1{What do I know?} -->|pv, P, T| R1[Use Absolute or Molal Humidity]
    Q1 -->|pv and Ps| R2[Use Relative Humidity]
    Q1 -->|Hm and Hms| R3[Use Percentage Humidity]
    R1 --> APP1[Drying Mass Balance: H in vs H out]
    R2 --> APP2[Storage Spec: 'Store below RH 65 percent']
    R3 --> APP3[Psychrometric Chart: mu isopleths]
    APP1 --> OUT1[Solve for kg water removed per kg dry air]
    APP2 --> OUT2[Solve for safe storage window]
    APP3 --> OUT3[Solve for humidification/dehumidification duty]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is a *selector* architecture. The first question (What data do I have?) determines which of the four humidity parameters is the natural descriptor for the engineering decision at hand. Food engineers learn to flip between these four without confusion precisely by using such a mental selector.

## 4.3 Physical Schematic — Moist Air as a Two-Component Mixture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MIX[Moist Air at Total Pressure P and Temperature T]
        direction TB
        DA[Dry Air: na moles, pa partial pressure, Ma = 28.97]
        WV[Water Vapour: nv moles, pv partial pressure, Mv = 18.015]
        DA --- WV
    end
    MIX -->|Divide by dry basis| RH[Relative Humidity phi = pv / Ps]
    MIX -->|Divide by dry basis| MA[Absolute Humidity H = mv / ma]
    MIX -->|Divide by dry basis| MH[Molal Humidity Hm = nv / na]
    MIX -->|Divide by dry basis| PH[Percentage Humidity mu = 100 Hm / Hms]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** All four humidity descriptors are *ratios taken on the dry-air basis*. The "divide by dry basis" arrow is the unifying theme of the entire topic. The water-vapour component can change at will; the dry-air component is the stable reference.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define the following with their mathematical expressions: (i) Absolute Humidity, (ii) Relative Humidity, (iii) Percentage Humidity.**

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Absolute Humidity (H):** The mass of water vapour carried by a unit mass of *dry* gas. Mathematically, $H = m_v / m_a$, expressed in kg water/kg dry air.
- **(ii) Relative Humidity (φ):** The ratio of the partial pressure of water vapour in a gas to its saturation pressure at the same temperature. Mathematically, $\phi = p_v / P_s$, a dimensionless quantity between 0 and 1.
- **(iii) Percentage Humidity (μ):** The ratio of the actual molal humidity to the molal humidity of the gas if it were saturated at the same temperature, expressed as a percentage. Mathematically, $\mu = 100 \cdot H_m / H_{m,s}$, expressed in %.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the relationship between Molal Humidity and Absolute Humidity. Why is the constant 0.622 used?**

**Model Answer:**

The two are related through the ratio of molecular weights of water and dry air:

$$H = 0.622 \times H_m$$

where $0.622 = M_v / M_a = 18.015 / 28.97$. This constant appears because when we convert from a *mole* ratio ($H_m = n_v / n_a$) to a *mass* ratio ($H = m_v / m_a$), each mole must be multiplied by its respective molecular weight. Water ($M_v = 18.015$ kg/kmol) is lighter than dry air ($M_a = 28.97$ kg/kmol), hence the multiplier is less than 1.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

A sample of moist air is at a total pressure of **101.325 kPa** and a temperature of **35 °C**. The partial pressure of water vapour in the sample is **5.0 kPa**. The saturation pressure of water at 35 °C is **5.628 kPa**.

**(a)** Calculate the (i) **Relative Humidity** and (ii) **Percentage Humidity** of the sample. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the (i) **Molal Humidity** and (ii) **Absolute Humidity** of the sample. Also, comment on whether the air is closer to "bone-dry" or "fully saturated" conditions. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — (i) Relative Humidity**

Direct substitution into the definition of $\phi$:

$$\phi = \frac{p_v}{P_s} = \frac{5.0}{5.628} = 0.8884 = 88.84 \, \%$$

> **Conversion logic:** Numerator is the actual vapour pressure, denominator is the saturation pressure at the same temperature. Result is a fraction; multiply by 100 to get %.

**Part (a) — (ii) Percentage Humidity**

**Step 1 — Actual molal humidity:**

$$H_m = \frac{p_v}{P - p_v} = \frac{5.0}{101.325 - 5.0} = \frac{5.0}{96.325} = 0.05191 \text{ kmol/kmol}$$

**Step 2 — Saturation molal humidity at 35 °C:**

$$H_{m,s} = \frac{P_s}{P - P_s} = \frac{5.628}{101.325 - 5.628} = \frac{5.628}{95.697} = 0.05881 \text{ kmol/kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Humidity:**

$$\mu = 100 \cdot \frac{H_m}{H_{m,s}} = 100 \cdot \frac{0.05191}{0.05881} = 88.27 \, \%$$

**Part (b) — (i) Molal Humidity**

Computed above in Part (a) Step 1:

$$H_m = 0.05191 \text{ kmol water / kmol dry air} \approx 0.0519$$

**Part (b) — (ii) Absolute Humidity**

Using $H = 0.622 \times H_m$:

$$H = 0.622 \times 0.05191 = 0.03229 \text{ kg water / kg dry air} = 32.29 \text{ g/kg}$$

**Comment on the state of the air:**

Since $\phi = 88.84 \,\%$ (very close to 1), the air is **close to saturation** (i.e. close to dew-point conditions) — definitely *not* bone-dry. At 35 °C tropical conditions, only a small further addition of moisture would cause condensation. In food-processing terms, this air would pose a **mould-risk** for stored grains and bakery items.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

A food-processing dryer takes in atmospheric air at **30 °C** with a **Relative Humidity of 60 %** and a total pressure of **101.325 kPa**. The saturation pressure of water at 30 °C is **4.246 kPa**.

**(a)** Determine the (i) **Partial pressure of water vapour** in the inlet air, (ii) **Molal Humidity**, and (iii) **Absolute Humidity** of the inlet air. (7 marks)

**(b)** The outlet air from the dryer is at **45 °C** with **Relative Humidity 25 %** and total pressure **101.325 kPa**. The saturation pressure of water at 45 °C is **9.583 kPa**. Calculate the **mass of water removed per kg of dry air** processed. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — (i) Partial Pressure of Water Vapour**

From the definition of RH:

$$p_{v,in} = \phi_{in} \times P_s(30\,^\circ\text{C}) = 0.60 \times 4.246 = 2.548 \text{ kPa}$$

> **Conversion logic:** We are given the *fractional* RH (60 % = 0.60) and the saturation pressure. Multiply them to recover the *actual* partial pressure.

**Part (a) — (ii) Molal Humidity of Inlet Air**

$$H_{m,in} = \frac{p_{v,in}}{P - p_{v,in}} = \frac{2.548}{101.325 - 2.548} = \frac{2.548}{98.777} = 0.02580 \text{ kmol/kmol}$$

**Part (a) — (iii) Absolute Humidity of Inlet Air**

$$H_{in} = 0.622 \times H_{m,in} = 0.622 \times 0.02580 = 0.01605 \text{ kg/kg dry air}$$

That is, **16.05 g of water per kg of dry air** at the inlet.

**Part (b) — Outlet State**

**Step 1 — Partial pressure at outlet:**

$$p_{v,out} = \phi_{out} \times P_s(45\,^\circ\text{C}) = 0.25 \times 9.583 = 2.396 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Molal humidity at outlet:**

$$H_{m,out} = \frac{p_{v,out}}{P - p_{v,out}} = \frac{2.396}{101.325 - 2.396} = \frac{2.396}{98.929} = 0.02422 \text{ kmol/kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Absolute humidity at outlet:**

$$H_{out} = 0.622 \times H_{m,out} = 0.622 \times 0.02422 = 0.01506 \text{ kg/kg dry air}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of water removed per kg of dry air:**

$$\Delta H = H_{in} - H_{out} = 0.01605 - 0.01506 = 0.00099 \text{ kg water / kg dry air}$$

> **Sanity check:** This is a small value (less than 1 g per kg of dry air) because the outlet air is *hotter and drier* (lower RH) than the inlet. The numerical result is small because the problem operates in a low-humidity regime; in real industrial dryers, $\Delta H$ is typically 0.01–0.05 kg/kg.

**Alternative mass-balance interpretation (moles):** One could equivalently write $\Delta n_v = n_a (H_{m,in} - H_{m,out})$ and then convert moles → mass by multiplying by $M_v = 18.015$ kg/kmol, arriving at the same $\Delta H$ value — confirming the result by an independent route.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dry-basis:** A common mistake is to divide by *total* mass or *total* moles (i.e. wet basis). Always divide by the *dry-air* mass/moles.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in $p_v$ and $P$:** Both pressures *must* be in the same units (both kPa or both Pa) before taking the ratio.
> 3. **Conflating $\phi$ and $\mu$:** Relative Humidity uses saturation pressure; Percentage Humidity uses saturation molal humidity. They are *not* exactly equal, although they are numerically close at low humidities.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor 0.622:** The molecular-weight ratio is a *conversion* constant, not a physical property of air. Use $H = 0.622 \cdot H_m$ only when going between the molal and absolute (mass) forms.
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer:** A board examiner *will* deduct a mark if you write "0.032" without "kg water/kg dry air".
> 6. **Using bar instead of kPa:** KTU board answers almost always work in kPa. Convert bar → kPa by multiplying by 100, atm → kPa by multiplying by 101.325, mmHg → kPa by multiplying by 0.1333.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Humidity is always referenced to the dry gas** — never to the total moist mixture.
- The **four** named humidity parameters are: **Absolute (H)**, **Molal (Hₘ)**, **Relative (φ)**, and **Percentage (μ)**.
- The **master formula** linking partial pressure to molal humidity: $H_m = p_v / (P - p_v)$.
- The **mass ↔ mole** conversion uses the constant **0.622** = $M_v / M_a$.
- **Relative Humidity** uses *partial pressure* of vapour divided by *saturation pressure*; **Percentage Humidity** uses *molal humidity* divided by *saturation molal humidity* × 100.
- For most food-processing conditions, $\mu (\text{in \%}) \approx 100 \cdot \phi$, but the **exact** form is $\mu = 100 \cdot \dfrac{\phi (P - P_s)}{P - \phi P_s}$.
- A high RH (≥ 80 %) means the air is close to **dew point** — risk of condensation and microbial growth in food products.
- A high $H$ (large mass of water per mass of dry air) means the air can **absorb more water** in a dryer — useful for sizing drying operations.
- Always check **steam tables** for the saturation pressure $P_s$ at the given temperature before computing any of the four parameters.
- In **KTU board answers**, present formulae *before* numbers, show *all* substitutions, retain **3–4 significant figures** in intermediate steps, and box the final numerical answer with units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Humidity in Condensation and Drying; Humidity Chart; Dew Point
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT302 - FOOD THERMODYNAMICS AND PROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Use of Humidity in Condensation and Drying; Humidity Chart; Dew Point

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Humidity** in food process engineering refers to the quantity of water vapour carried by a unit mass of dry air. Since food materials are predominantly processed in air-vapour mixtures (called *psychrometric systems*), the mastery of humidity calculations is essential for designing dryers, condensers, evaporators, and HVAC units used in food plants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams**
> - **Dry Air (D.A.)**: Theoretically pure air without water vapour — used as the reference mass in all psychrometric relations.
> - **Moist Air**: A binary mixture of dry air + superheated water vapour.
> - **Saturated Air**: Moist air in which the partial pressure of water vapour equals the saturation pressure at the prevailing dry-bulb temperature.

**Dew Point ($T_{dp}$)** is the temperature at which a given moist air sample becomes saturated (RH = 100%) when cooled at constant pressure and constant humidity ratio. Below this temperature, condensation of liquid water occurs.

**Wet-Bulb Temperature ($T_{wb}$)** is the dynamic equilibrium temperature reached by a water-wetted thermometer bulb exposed to a moving air stream. It is governed by simultaneous heat and mass transfer.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bath Towel on a Hot Day" Analogy**
>
> Imagine hanging a wet cotton towel in a hot, dry Kerala afternoon versus a humid monsoon evening:
>
> - On a **hot, dry day** (low relative humidity) — the towel dries fast. The dry air can "accept" a lot more water vapour because it is far from saturation.
> - On a **humid day** (RH close to 100%) — the towel stays damp. The air is already full of moisture and cannot accept much more.
>
> This simple observation is the foundation of all drying and condensation design. Food engineers quantify this acceptance capacity using *humidity charts* — just like a "saturation quota" for moisture in air.

### 1.3 Condensation & Drying — Food Industry Context

**Condensation** in food plants occurs when warm, moist air contacts cold surfaces (e.g., refrigerant coils in cold storage, evaporator coils of a chilling unit). Latent heat of vapourisation is released, and water is recovered.

**Drying** is the simultaneous heat-and-mass-transfer operation where moisture migrates from inside the food to its surface, and then from the surface into the surrounding air stream. The driving force is the *vapour-pressure gradient* between the food surface and the bulk air.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Conditions (KTU Convention)**
> Total Pressure $P = \mathbf{101.325 \text{ kPa}}$, Molecular weight of dry air $M_a = \mathbf{28.97 \text{ kg/kmol}}$, Molecular weight of water $M_w = \mathbf{18.02 \text{ kg/kmol}}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Psychrometric System

Moist air is treated as an **ideal gas mixture** because food processing temperatures (typically 0 °C – 150 °C) and pressures (≈1 atm) are well within the ideal-gas regime.

#### Step 1: Humidity Ratio (Absolute Humidity, $H$)

Defined as kg of water vapour per kg of dry air.

$$H = \frac{18.02}{28.97} \times \frac{p_w}{P - p_w} = 0.622 \times \frac{p_w}{P - p_w}$$

- $p_w$ = partial pressure of water vapour (kPa)
- $P$ = total system pressure (kPa)
- At **saturation**: replace $p_w$ with $p_{ws}(T)$ (saturation pressure of water at dry-bulb temperature $T$).

#### Step 2: Relative Humidity (%RH)

$$RH = \frac{p_w}{p_{ws}(T)} \times 100\,\%$$

#### Step 3: Percentage or Saturating Humidity (%H)

$$\%H = \frac{H}{H_s} \times 100$$

where $H_s$ is the humidity ratio of saturated air at the same temperature.

#### Step 4: Humid Volume, $V_H$ (m³/kg dry air)

The volume occupied by 1 kg of dry air **plus** the vapour it carries.

$$V_H = \left(\frac{1}{M_a} + \frac{H}{M_w}\right)\frac{RT}{P}$$

Numerically simplified form (air–water system, $P = 101.325$ kPa):

$$V_H \;[\text{m}^3/\text{kg D.A.}] = 0.00283 \, T[\text{K}] + 0.00456 \, H$$

#### Step 5: Humid Heat, $C_s$ (kJ/kg dry air·K)

Energy required to raise 1 kg dry air + its accompanying vapour by 1 K.

$$C_s = C_{p,a} + H \cdot C_{p,w} = 1.005 + 1.88\,H$$

#### Step 6: Dew Point, $T_{dp}$

Solve $p_{ws}(T_{dp}) = p_w$ from the steam tables. If $T_{dp} < 0$ °C, use ice tables.

#### Step 7: Adiabatic Saturation Temperature, $T_{as}$

The temperature approached by water-sprayed air in an *adiabatic* chamber at steady state. For the air–water system, $T_{as} \approx T_{wb}$.

#### Step 8: Psychrometric (Humidity) Chart

A graphical tool plotting:
- X-axis → Dry-bulb temperature ($T_{db}$)
- Y-axis → Humidity ratio ($H$)
- Curves of constant RH, $T_{wb}$, $V_H$, $C_s$, and $H_s$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the humidity chart is used in food industry:**
> It eliminates iterative calculations in drying/condensation problems, allowing graphical estimation of adiabatic cooling lines, dehumidification paths, and water-removal rates.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board Exam Ready)

| # | Quantity | Symbol / Equation | Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|----------|-------------------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | Humidity Ratio | $H = 0.622\,p_w/(P-p_w)$ | kg water/kg dry air | Ideal gas |
| 2 | Saturation Humidity | $H_s = 0.622\,p_{ws}/(P-p_{ws})$ | kg/kg D.A. | $RH = 100\%$ |
| 3 | Relative Humidity | $RH = p_w/p_{ws} \times 100$ | % | $0 \le RH \le 100$ |
| 4 | Percentage Humidity | $\%H = H/H_s \times 100$ | % | — |
| 5 | Humid Volume | $V_H = (1/M_a + H/M_w)RT/P$ | m³/kg D.A. | $P \approx 1$ atm |
| 6 | Humid Heat | $C_s = 1.005 + 1.88\,H$ | kJ/(kg D.A.·K) | $T \le 150$ °C |
| 7 | Dew Point | $p_{ws}(T_{dp}) = p_w$ | K or °C | Use steam tables |
| 8 | Wet-Bulb Depression | $\Delta T = T_{db} - T_{wb}$ | K | Driving force for drying |
| 9 | Water Removed in Dehumidification | $m_w = \dot{m}_a (H_1 - H_2)$ | kg/h | Mass balance on air |
| 10 | Latent Heat of Vaporisation | $\lambda_w \approx 2501 - 2.42\,T[°C]$ | kJ/kg | At $T \le 200$ °C |
| 11 | Heat to Vaporise Moisture | $q = \dot{m}_w \cdot \lambda_w$ | kW | Steady-state drying |
| 12 | Sensible Heat to Air | $q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot C_s \cdot \Delta T$ | kW | Heating/cooling air |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorisation Priority for KTU:** Equations 1, 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11 carry the maximum weightage in numericals. Practice them repeatedly using the saturation pressure tables (Appendix A.1, any standard textbook).

---

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Food Plants

- **Drying (dehydration) of food**: Design of belt-tray, spray, fluidised-bed, and rotary dryers — all governed by the *psychrometric balance*.
- **Cold-storage condensation**: Selecting coils, calculating condensate rate, designing drip trays.
- **Spray drying of milk/eggs**: Critical to determine outlet air humidity and energy recovery.
- **HVAC for processing halls**: Temperature and humidity control to prevent microbial spoilage.
- **Milling and storage**: Predicting moisture migration and caking in powders.

> [!TIP]
> In a **typical KTU 2-mark question**, they often ask: *"Define humidity ratio and write its expression."* Always quote the constant **0.622** — its omission costs the mark.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Humidity Ratio $H$

Starting from Dalton's Law of partial pressures for an ideal gas mixture:

$$P = p_a + p_w$$

Using the ideal gas equation $pV = nRT$, the number of moles per unit volume gives the ratio:

$$\frac{n_w}{n_a} = \frac{p_w}{p_a} = \frac{p_w}{P - p_w}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $n_a$ and converting to mass basis using molecular weights:

$$\frac{m_w}{m_a} = \frac{n_w M_w}{n_a M_a} = \frac{M_w}{M_a} \cdot \frac{p_w}{P - p_w}$$

Substituting $M_w/M_a = 18.02/28.97 = 0.622$:

$$H = \frac{m_w}{m_a} = 0.622 \times \frac{p_w}{P - p_w}$$

This expression is the **most fundamental equation** in all psychrometric calculations.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Finding Dew Point, Humid Volume, Humid Heat

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Typical Style):**
> Moist air at 1 atm total pressure has a dry-bulb temperature of 35 °C and a wet-bulb temperature of 25 °C. Calculate: (a) Humidity ratio $H$, (b) Relative humidity $RH$, (c) Dew point $T_{dp}$, (d) Humid volume $V_H$, (e) Humid heat $C_s$.

#### Step 1 — Reading Saturation Pressures from Steam Tables

| Temperature (°C) | $p_{ws}$ (kPa) |
|------------------|----------------|
| 25 | 3.169 |
| 35 | 5.628 |

#### Step 2 — Compute $p_w$ from Wet-Bulb Data

The wet-bulb relation comes from the energy balance of an adiabatic saturator:

$$\frac{H_s - H}{H_s - H_w} = \frac{C_s(T_{db} - T_{wb})}{\lambda_{wb}}$$

For the first iteration, assume $C_s \approx 1.005 + 1.88(0.020)$ and $\lambda_{25} \approx 2442$ kJ/kg. Solving iteratively (taking a textbook table value):

$$p_w \approx 2.45\ \text{kPa}$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate Humidity Ratio $H$

$$H = 0.622 \times \frac{2.45}{101.325 - 2.45}$$

$$H = 0.622 \times \frac{2.45}{98.875}$$

$$H = 0.622 \times 0.02478$$

$$H = 0.0154\ \text{kg water/kg dry air}$$

#### Step 4 — Relative Humidity $RH$

$$RH = \frac{p_w}{p_{ws}(35\,°C)} \times 100 = \frac{2.45}{5.628} \times 100$$

$$RH = 43.53\,\%$$

#### Step 5 — Dew Point $T_{dp}$

Find $T$ such that $p_{ws}(T) = 2.45$ kPa. From steam tables:

$$T_{dp} \approx 21.0\ \text{°C}$$

#### Step 6 — Humid Volume $V_H$

Using the simplified equation at $T = 35\,°C = 308.15$ K:

$$V_H = 0.00283 \times 308.15 + 0.00456 \times 0.0154$$

$$V_H = 0.8721 + 0.0000702$$

$$V_H \approx 0.872\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg dry air}$$

#### Step 7 — Humid Heat $C_s$

$$C_s = 1.005 + 1.88 \times 0.0154$$

$$C_s = 1.005 + 0.0290$$

$$C_s = 1.034\ \text{kJ/(kg D.A.·K)}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Drying Process Energy/Mass Balance

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern Problem:**
> Air enters a dryer at $T_1 = 90\,°C$, $H_1 = 0.010$ kg/kg, leaves at $T_2 = 50\,°C$, $H_2 = 0.030$ kg/kg. Mass flow rate of dry air $\dot{m}_a = 100$ kg D.A./h. Find (i) Water removed per hour, (ii) Heat supplied in pre-heater.

#### (i) Water Removed

$$\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a (H_2 - H_1) = 100 \times (0.030 - 0.010)$$

$$\dot{m}_w = 100 \times 0.020 = 2.0\ \text{kg/h}$$

#### (ii) Pre-heater Load (Humid Heat × ΔT)

Assume pre-heater heats ambient air ($T_0 = 30\,°C$) to dryer inlet ($T_1 = 90\,°C$). Humid heat at inlet:

$$C_{s,1} = 1.005 + 1.88(0.010) = 1.0238\ \text{kJ/(kg D.A.·K)}$$

$$q_{preheater} = \dot{m}_a C_{s,1}(T_1 - T_0)$$

$$q_{preheater} = 100 \times 1.0238 \times (90 - 30)$$

$$q_{preheater} = 100 \times 1.0238 \times 60$$

$$q_{preheater} = 6142.8\ \text{kJ/h} = 1.706\ \text{kW}$$

#### Heat Actually Used for Vaporisation in the Dryer (for cross-check)

$$\dot{Q}_{latent} = \dot{m}_w \cdot \lambda_{70\,°C}$$

$$\lambda_{70\,°C} \approx 2333\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{latent} = 2.0 \times 2333 = 4666\ \text{kJ/h} = 1.296\ \text{kW}$$

The remainder (≈ 0.41 kW) goes as sensible heating of vapour, tray losses, and dryer walls.

---

### 3.4 Condensation Case — Rate of Condensate Recovery

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A refrigerant coil at 5 °C is exposed to 200 kg/h of moist air at 30 °C, $RH = 60\%$. Calculate the condensate rate (assume air leaves saturated at 5 °C).

**Step A**: At 30 °C, $p_{ws} = 4.246$ kPa. So $p_w = 0.6 \times 4.246 = 2.548$ kPa.

$$H_1 = 0.622 \times \frac{2.548}{101.325 - 2.548} = 0.01604\ \text{kg/kg D.A.}$$

**Step B**: At 5 °C, $p_{ws} = 0.8726$ kPa.

$$H_2 = 0.622 \times \frac{0.8726}{101.325 - 0.8726} = 0.00539\ \text{kg/kg D.A.}$$

**Step C**: Condensate rate

$$\dot{m}_w = 200 \times (0.01604 - 0.00539) = 200 \times 0.01065 = 2.13\ \text{kg/h}$$

---

### 3.5 Comparative Summary Table (Drying vs Condensation)

| Feature | Drying Operation | Condensation Operation |
|---------|------------------|------------------------|
| Direction of mass transfer | Water → Air | Water vapour → Liquid |
| Driving force | $T_{db} > T_{wb}$, low RH in bulk air | Surface temperature $< T_{dp}$ |
| Humidity change | $H_2 > H_1$ (air picks up moisture) | $H_2 < H_1$ (air loses moisture) |
| Key equation | $\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a (H_2 - H_1)$ | Same equation, opposite sign |
| Heat duty | Mainly $\dot{m}_w \cdot \lambda_w$ | Mainly removal of $\dot{m}_w \cdot \lambda_w$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Psychrometric System Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[INPUT Moist Air Inlet]
    nodeB[DB Temperature 35 C]
    nodeC[WB Temperature 25 C]
    nodeD[Relative Humidity Sensor]
    nodeE[Pressure P 101.325 kPa]
    nodeF[Steam Table Lookup]
    nodeG[Compute pw and pws]
    nodeH[Humidity Ratio H]
    nodeI[Humid Heat Cs]
    nodeJ[Humid Volume Vh]
    nodeK[Dew Point Tdp]
    nodeL[OUTPUT Property Set]
    nodeM[Application Drying or Condensation]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM
```

### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Adiabatic Drying System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Ambient Air]
    node2[Pre Heater]
    node3[Heated Air T1 H1]
    node4[Food on Tray]
    node5[Moisture Transfer]
    node6[Exhaust Air T2 H2]
    node7[Condensate or Vapour Exit]

    node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> exhaust[To Atmosphere or Heat Recovery]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Condensation on a Cold Coil

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP[Warm Moist Air T1 H1]
    nodeQ[Refrigerant Coil Tcoil]
    nodeR[Condensate Drip]
    nodeS[Cold Air T2 H2 Lower]
    nodeT[Drain Pan]

    nodeP --> nodeQ
    nodeQ --> nodeS
    nodeQ --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeT
    nodeS --> outlet[Plenum Outlet]
```

### 4.4 Psychrometric Chart — Conceptual Component Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    axisX[Horizontal Axis Dry Bulb Temperature]
    axisY[Vertical Axis Humidity Ratio H]
    curveA[Curve Constant RH Lines 20 to 100 percent]
    curveB[Curve Constant WB Temperature Lines]
    curveC[Curve Humid Volume Lines]
    curveD[Curve Saturation Line 100 percent RH Upper Boundary]
    curveE[Adiabatic Saturation Line Slope Cs by Lambda]

    axisX --> curveD
    axisY --> curveD
    curveD --> curveA
    curveD --> curveB
    curveD --> curveC
    curveD --> curveE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip (KTU Theory):** The saturation line forms the upper boundary. Any process that crosses the saturation line results in fog formation — a frequent 2-mark conceptual question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCFTT302, Module 1]`

Define the following terms with their mathematical expressions: (i) Humidity ratio, (ii) Relative humidity, (iii) Percentage humidity.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Humidity Ratio (H):** Mass of water vapour associated with 1 kg of dry air. Mathematically,

$$H = 0.622 \times \frac{p_w}{P - p_w}$$

where $p_w$ = partial pressure of water vapour (kPa), $P$ = total pressure (kPa).

2. **Relative Humidity (RH):** Ratio of the partial pressure of water vapour to its saturation pressure at the dry-bulb temperature, expressed as a percentage.

$$RH = \frac{p_w}{p_{ws}(T)} \times 100\,\%$$

3. **Percentage Humidity (%H):** Ratio of actual humidity ratio to the saturation humidity ratio at the same temperature.

$$\%H = \frac{H}{H_s} \times 100$$

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCFTT302, Module 1]`

Explain the concept of dew point. How is it different from wet-bulb temperature?

**Model Answer:**

The **dew point ($T_{dp}$)** is the temperature at which moist air, when cooled at constant pressure, becomes saturated (RH = 100%) and condensation just begins. Below this temperature, liquid water condenses out. Mathematically, $T_{dp}$ is found by solving $p_{ws}(T_{dp}) = p_w$.

**Difference:**

| Parameter | Dew Point ($T_{dp}$) | Wet-Bulb Temperature ($T_{wb}$) |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------------------|
| Definition | Saturation temperature at constant $H$ and $P$ | Dynamic equilibrium temperature of wetted bulb |
| Process | Cooling at constant humidity | Evaporative cooling (simultaneous heat & mass transfer) |
| Depends on | Only $H$ | Both $H$ and $T_{db}$ |
| Typical numerical relation | $T_{dp} \le T_{wb} \le T_{db}$ | — |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the equation for the humidity ratio of moist air. Show that at atmospheric pressure the constant is 0.622. (7 marks)

**(b)** A dryer processes 500 kg/h of food from 80 % moisture (wet basis) to 10 % moisture (wet basis). The drying air enters the dryer at 95 °C with humidity ratio $H_1 = 0.015$ kg/kg and leaves at 55 °C with $H_2 = 0.045$ kg/kg. Determine: (i) mass of dry air required per hour, (ii) total heat supplied to the dryer if the ambient air is at 30 °C with $H = 0.012$ kg/kg. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

Assume moist air obeys Dalton's law of partial pressures:

$$P = p_a + p_w$$

where $p_a$ and $p_w$ are partial pressures of dry air and water vapour. From ideal-gas law applied to each component:

$$p_a V = n_a R T \quad \text{and} \quad p_w V = n_w R T$$

Dividing:

$$\frac{p_w}{p_a} = \frac{n_w}{n_a}$$

Substituting $p_a = P - p_w$:

$$\frac{n_w}{n_a} = \frac{p_w}{P - p_w}$$

Converting moles to mass:

$$n_w = \frac{m_w}{M_w}, \quad n_a = \frac{m_a}{M_a}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{m_w}{m_a} = \frac{M_w}{M_a} \cdot \frac{p_w}{P - p_w}$$

The humidity ratio is defined as:

$$H = \frac{m_w}{m_a} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_w}{P - p_w}$$

The constant is:

$$\frac{M_w}{M_a} = \frac{18.02}{28.97} = 0.622$$

Hence proved.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Water to be removed per hour:**

Mass of food in = 500 kg/h. Initial moisture = 80 % wet basis → water mass = $500 \times 0.80 = 400$ kg/h; dry food = 100 kg/h.

Final moisture = 10 % wet basis → water mass = $(100/0.90) \times 0.10 = 11.11$ kg/h on the 111.11 kg/h of product; water removed = $400 - 11.11 = 388.89$ kg/h.

Wait, the more accurate approach: dry food is constant at 100 kg/h. Final product = $100/(1-0.10) = 111.11$ kg/h. So water remaining in product = $11.11$ kg/h. Therefore:

$$\dot{m}_w = 400 - 11.11 = 388.89\ \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Dry air required:**

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{\dot{m}_w}{H_2 - H_1} = \frac{388.89}{0.045 - 0.015} = \frac{388.89}{0.030}$$

$$\dot{m}_a = 12{,}963\ \text{kg dry air/h}$$

**Step 3 — Heat supplied in pre-heater:**

Humid heat at ambient ($H = 0.012$ kg/kg):

$$C_s = 1.005 + 1.88 \times 0.012 = 1.0276\ \text{kJ/(kg D.A.·K)}$$

Pre-heater heats ambient air from 30 °C to 95 °C:

$$q_{pre} = 12963 \times 1.0276 \times (95 - 30) = 12963 \times 1.0276 \times 65$$

$$q_{pre} = 865{,}778\ \text{kJ/h} = 240.5\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Heat supplied inside dryer (for water vaporisation, at ~75 °C average):**

$\lambda_{75} \approx 2320$ kJ/kg.

$$q_{dryer} = 388.89 \times 2320 = 902{,}225\ \text{kJ/h} = 250.6\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Total Heat:**

$$q_{total} = q_{pre} + q_{dryer} = 240.5 + 250.6 = 491.1\ \text{kW}$$

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Backlog Paper]`

**(a)** What is a psychrometric chart? List its main features and explain how it is used in drying operations. (7 marks)

**(b)** Moist air at 1 atm has dry-bulb temperature 40 °C and wet-bulb temperature 30 °C. Using the steam table values $p_{ws}(40\,°C) = 7.384$ kPa and $p_{ws}(30\,°C) = 4.246$ kPa, determine: (i) Humidity ratio, (ii) Relative humidity, (iii) Dew point, (iv) Humid volume, (v) Humid heat. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

A **psychrometric chart** (also called humidity chart) is a graphical representation of the thermodynamic properties of moist air at a fixed pressure (usually 1 atm). It plots dry-bulb temperature on the abscissa and humidity ratio on the ordinate, with the following features:

1. **Saturation curve (100 % RH line):** Upper boundary of the chart; locus of saturated air.
2. **Constant relative humidity curves:** Family of curves below the saturation line for various RH (10 %, 20 %, ..., 90 %).
3. **Constant wet-bulb temperature lines:** Slope ≈ $C_s/\lambda_{wb}$.
4. **Constant humid volume lines:** Sloping upward; nearly parallel to saturation line.
5. **Constant humid heat lines:** Approximately horizontal.
6. **Enthalpy lines:** For complete thermodynamic work.

**Use in Drying Operations:**

- Reading inlet and outlet air states from $T_{db}$ and $T_{wb}$ measurements.
- Finding the moisture-pickup capacity of air: $\Delta H = H_2 - H_1$.
- Predicting final exit temperature and humidity.
- Designing adiabatic saturation lines for dryer chambers.
- Calculating the enthalpy change of air for energy balance.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Given:** $T_{db} = 40$ °C, $T_{wb} = 30$ °C, $P = 101.325$ kPa.

**Step (i) Humidity Ratio:**

We use the wet-bulb relation to find $p_w$. From steam tables at $T_{wb} = 30$ °C: $\lambda_{wb} \approx 2430$ kJ/kg. Assume $C_s \approx 1.005 + 1.88H$ kJ/(kg·K). The wet-bulb equation:

$$\frac{H_s(T_{wb}) - H}{H_s(T_{wb}) - H_w} = \frac{C_s (T_{db} - T_{wb})}{\lambda_{wb}}$$

with $H_w \approx 0.622 \times p_{ws}(30)/(P - p_{ws}(30)) = 0.622 \times 4.246/97.079 = 0.0272$ kg/kg.

Solving iteratively, the textbook table gives $p_w \approx 3.20$ kPa. Hence:

$$H = 0.622 \times \frac{3.20}{101.325 - 3.20} = 0.622 \times \frac{3.20}{98.125} = 0.0203\ \text{kg/kg D.A.}$$

**Step (ii) Relative Humidity:**

$$RH = \frac{3.20}{7.384} \times 100 = 43.3\,\%$$

**Step (iii) Dew Point:**

Solve $p_{ws}(T_{dp}) = 3.20$ kPa. From tables:

$$T_{dp} \approx 25.0\ \text{°C}$$

**Step (iv) Humid Volume at $T_{db} = 313.15$ K:**

$$V_H = 0.00283 \times 313.15 + 0.00456 \times 0.0203 = 0.886 + 0.0001 = 0.886\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg D.A.}$$

**Step (v) Humid Heat:**

$$C_s = 1.005 + 1.88 \times 0.0203 = 1.005 + 0.038 = 1.043\ \text{kJ/(kg D.A.·K)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losses**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant 0.622** in the humidity ratio — full 1-mark cut.
> 2. **Using $T_{db}$ instead of $T_{wb}$** to find $p_{ws}$ in $H$ formula. Always use saturation pressure at the **dry-bulb** temperature for $H_s$ when computing %H or for the humid volume.
> 3. **Unit mismatch** — humid heat must be in kJ/(kg D.A.·K), not kJ/kg·K (clarify "per kg dry air").
> 4. **In drying balance**, forgetting to subtract inlet moisture content when computing product mass. Use $m_{dry\,food}$ consistently.
> 5. **In dew point**, students often write $T_{dp} = T_{wb}$. These are **never** equal unless RH = 100 %.
> 6. **Wet-bulb vs adiabatic saturation** — for air–water system they are practically equal; for other systems they differ. Mention this nuance only if asked.

---

### 5.1 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] Humidity ratio $H = 0.622\,p_w/(P-p_w)$ — derive from Dalton's law.
- [x] Always express $H$ **per kg dry air**, never per kg moist air.
- [x] Relative humidity $RH = (p_w/p_{ws})\times 100$, requires steam tables.
- [x] Dew point: $p_{ws}(T_{dp}) = p_w$ — purely a property of vapour pressure.
- [x] Wet-bulb temperature ≈ adiabatic saturation temperature (air–water only).
- [x] Humid volume $V_H$ uses total mass of moist gas, but reference is dry air.
- [x] Humid heat $C_s = 1.005 + 1.88H$ — handy for sensible-heat calculations.
- [x] Drying balance: $\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a (H_2 - H_1)$; condensation reverses sign.
- [x] Latent heat of water $\lambda_w \approx 2501 - 2.42\,T_{°C}$ (linear approx).
- [x] In a food dryer, energy = sensible heating of air + vaporisation of water.
- [x] In a condenser, the air leaves saturated at the coil surface temperature.
- [x] Psychrometric chart is a graphical shortcut — read $H$ from $T_{db}$ & $T_{wb}$.
- [x] Saturation line is the upper envelope; processes crossing it → fog formation.
- [x] KTU gold standard: show units at every step; board examiners deduct marks for unit omission.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Day-Before Revision:**
> **"0.622 — Always D.A. — Steam Tables First — Units Always Last"**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Balance in Unit Operations: Evaporation, Crystallization, Mixing, Leaching, Extraction, Absorption, Distillation and Drying
topics.content:
