> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT302 - FOOD THERMODYNAMICS AND PROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Material Balance in Unit Operations: Evaporation, Crystallization, Mixing, Leaching, Extraction, Absorption, Distillation and Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Balance (Mass Balance) in Unit Operations:**
> A **material balance** is the application of the **Law of Conservation of Mass** to a defined system (a single piece of equipment, a series of connected equipment, or an entire process), expressed mathematically as:
> $$\text{Input} + \text{Generation} = \text{Output} + \text{Accumulation} + \text{Consumption}$$
> For a **steady-state**, **non-reactive** system (most food unit operations), this simplifies to: **Input = Output**.

In **food process engineering**, material balances are performed across unit operations to determine the quantities of raw materials, intermediate products, finished products, and waste streams. The **eight core unit operations** required by the KTU 2024 Scheme for Module 2 are: **Evaporation, Crystallization, Mixing, Leaching, Extraction, Absorption, Distillation, and Drying**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Kitchen Tap and Bucket**
> Imagine a tap filling a bucket with a hole at the bottom. **Input** = water flowing in from the tap. **Output** = water draining through the hole. If the bucket's water level remains constant, the flow in equals the flow out (**steady-state material balance**). If the bucket is overflowing or emptying, water is **accumulating** or **depleting** (**unsteady-state**). A material balance is simply the *quantitative accounting* of every kilogram entering, leaving, being generated, consumed, or stockpiled within a defined "bucket" (process equipment).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used Across All Unit Operations

- **Feed (F)** — Mass or mass flow rate of inlet stream (kg or kg/h).
- **Product/Concentrate (P)** — Outlet enriched stream.
- **Solvent/Extract/Leachate (L or S)** — Liquid medium used for selective mass transfer.
- **Solutes and Solvents** — Mass fractions ($x$) or mole fractions ($y$).
- **Conversion constants:** **1 kg water = 1 L water** (at 4 °C) and **1 atm = 101.325 kPa**.

### 1.4 Overview of the Eight Unit Operations

| Operation | Core Mass Transfer Phenomenon | Typical Food Industry Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Evaporation** | Removal of volatile solvent (water) by boiling | Concentrated tomato paste, milk concentrate |
| **Crystallization** | Separation of solute as solid crystals from supersaturated solution | Sugar refining, salt production |
| **Mixing** | Homogenization of two or more miscible phases | Syrup blending, dough preparation |
| **Leaching** | Selective dissolution of soluble component from solid by solvent | Sugar extraction from sugar beet, tea brewing |
| **Extraction** | Selective transfer of solute between two immiscible liquid phases | Oil extraction using hexane, decaffeination |
| **Absorption** | Transfer of gas/vapor component into a liquid solvent | Removal of CO₂ from carbonated drinks, scrubbing |
| **Distillation** | Separation based on volatility difference of components | Alcohol purification, essential oil recovery |
| **Drying** | Removal of moisture (usually water) from solid/liquid by evaporation | Milk powder, dried fruits, grains |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Heuristic:** The term **"single-stage"** appears in nearly every Part B problem on this module. Always assume **steady-state, single-stage, single-component solvent (water)** unless the problem explicitly states otherwise. This assumption unlocks 90% of the standard KTU 14-mark problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Universal Material Balance Framework

For any system, the **General Mass Balance Equation** is:

$$\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{gen} - \dot{m}_{cons}$$

For **steady-state food processes** (no chemical reaction), $dm_{sys}/dt = 0$, $\dot{m}_{gen} = 0$, and $\dot{m}_{cons} = 0$, giving:

$$\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = P + W$$

For a **component balance** (e.g., on solute or water):

$$F \cdot x_F = P \cdot x_P + W \cdot x_W$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Material balances in food engineering are almost always **overall + component balances** (2 equations) to solve for 2 unknowns. This is the universal problem-solving template.

### 2.2 Operation-Specific Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Evaporation

- **Why:** Remove water to increase solids concentration, reduce volume for storage, improve shelf life.
- **How:** Feed enters the evaporator; water vapor leaves as the overhead (vapor) stream; concentrated liquid leaves as the bottom (liquid) stream.
- **Key Assumption:** Volatile component = water only; non-volatile solute (sugar, salt) stays in the liquid.
- **Energy concept (for later modules):** Latent heat of vaporization $h_{fg} \approx 2260 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at 100 °C.

**Mass Balance Equations:**
$$F = V + L$$
$$F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L \quad \text{(solute balance)}$$

Where $V$ = vapor (water) flow, $L$ = liquid (concentrate) flow, $x$ = mass fraction of solids.

#### 2.2.2 Crystallization

- **Why:** Produce pure solid crystals from a solution.
- **How:** Solution is cooled or evaporated to supersaturation; solute crystallizes out; mother liquor (remaining liquid) is separated.
- **Key Concept:** **Yield** is the mass of crystals produced per unit feed.

**Mass Balance Equations:**
$$F = C + M$$
$$F \cdot x_F = C \cdot x_C + M \cdot x_M$$

Where $C$ = crystals, $M$ = mother liquor. **Note:** Crystals are wet — they carry adhering mother liquor of composition $x_M$.

#### 2.2.3 Mixing (Blending)

- **Why:** Achieve uniform composition of a final product from streams of differing composition.
- **How:** Two or more streams combined; assume perfect mixing.

**Mass Balance Equations:**
$$F_1 + F_2 = P$$
$$F_1 \cdot x_1 + F_2 \cdot x_2 = P \cdot x_P$$

This extends to **$n$-stream mixing** trivially: $\sum F_i x_i = P \cdot x_P$.

#### 2.2.4 Leaching (Solid-Liquid Extraction)

- **Why:** Extract a soluble component from an insoluble solid matrix.
- **How:** Solvent contacts the solid; soluble matter dissolves and is carried away in the **overflow (leachate)**; insoluble solids leave in the **underflow**.
- **Key Concept:** **Underflow** carries retained solution along with the insoluble solids.

**Mass Balance Equations (Single-Stage):**
$$F_{\text{solid}} + F_{\text{solvent}} = L_{\text{overflow}} + S_{\text{underflow}}$$
**Solute balance:**
$$F_{\text{solid}} \cdot x_{\text{initial solute}} + F_{\text{solvent}} \cdot x_{\text{solvent solute}} = L \cdot x_L + S \cdot x_S$$

For pure solvent: $x_{\text{solvent solute}} = 0$.

#### 2.2.5 Extraction (Liquid-Liquid Extraction)

- **Why:** Separate a solute using two immiscible liquids.
- **How:** Feed (solute + carrier solvent) is contacted with an **extracting solvent**; two phases form — **Extract** (solute-rich phase) and **Raffinate** (solute-lean phase).

**Mass Balance Equations:**
$$F + S = E + R$$
$$F \cdot x_F = E \cdot y_E + R \cdot x_R$$

Where $F$ = feed, $S$ = solvent, $E$ = extract, $R$ = raffinate, $y$ and $x$ are compositions of the respective phases.

#### 2.2.6 Absorption

- **Why:** Transfer a soluble gas/vapor into a liquid absorbent.
- **How:** Gas mixture contacts a liquid; soluble gas dissolves; insoluble gas leaves the top; enriched liquid leaves the bottom.

**Mass Balance Equations:**
$$G_{\text{in}} + L_{\text{in}} = G_{\text{out}} + L_{\text{out}}$$
**Component balance (on absorbed gas):**
$$G_{\text{in}} \cdot y_{\text{in}} + L_{\text{in}} \cdot x_{\text{in}} = G_{\text{out}} \cdot y_{\text{out}} + L_{\text{out}} \cdot x_{\text{out}}$$

#### 2.2.7 Distillation

- **Why:** Separate components based on volatility differences.
- **How:** Liquid feed partially vaporized; vapor (rich in light component) leaves as **distillate (D)**; liquid (rich in heavy component) leaves as **bottom product (B)**.
- **Key Concept:** **Reflux ratio (R)** = $L/D$, but for material balance alone, we work on the overall and component levels.

**Mass Balance Equations:**
$$F = D + B$$
$$F \cdot x_F = D \cdot x_D + B \cdot x_B$$

Where $x$ = mole fraction (or mass fraction) of the more volatile component (MVC).

#### 2.2.8 Drying

- **Why:** Reduce moisture content of a solid to a specified level for preservation.
- **How:** Wet solid contacts hot air; water evaporates; dry solid leaves; humid air leaves.
- **Key Concept:** **Moisture content** can be expressed on a **wet basis (wb)** or **dry basis (db)** — always verify which is being used.

**Conversion:**
$$X_{db} = \frac{X_{wb}}{1 - X_{wb}}$$

**Mass Balance Equations:**
$$F_{\text{wet solid}} = F_{\text{dry solid}} + W_{\text{evaporated water}}$$
**Water balance:**
$$F_{\text{wet}} \cdot X_{w,\text{wet}} = F_{\text{dry}} \cdot X_{w,\text{dry}} + W$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Overall Balance | Component Balance | Key Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| Evaporation | $F = V + L$ | $F x_F = L x_L$ | $V$ = vapor, $L$ = liquid concentrate |
| Crystallization | $F = C + M$ | $F x_F = C x_C + M x_M$ | $C$ = crystals, $M$ = mother liquor |
| Mixing | $\sum F_i = P$ | $\sum F_i x_i = P x_P$ | $i = 1, 2, ..., n$ streams |
| Leaching | $A + B = L + S$ | $A a + B b = L l + S s$ | $A$ = feed solid, $B$ = solvent, $L$ = overflow, $S$ = underflow |
| Extraction | $F + S = E + R$ | $F x_F = E y_E + R x_R$ | $F$ = feed, $S$ = solvent, $E$ = extract, $R$ = raffinate |
| Absorption | $G_1 + L_2 = G_2 + L_1$ | $G_1 y_1 + L_2 x_2 = G_2 y_2 + L_1 x_1$ | $G$ = gas, $L$ = liquid (1 = out, 2 = in) |
| Distillation | $F = D + B$ | $F x_F = D x_D + B x_B$ | $D$ = distillate, $B$ = bottoms |
| Drying | $F_{ws} = F_{ds} + W$ | $F_{ws} X_{ws} = F_{ds} X_{ds} + W$ | $W$ = evaporated water, $X$ = moisture content |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Material balances form the foundation of **process simulation software (ASPEN Plus, HYSYS, SuperPro Designer)** used by food and chemical engineers to design evaporators for dairy plants, optimize sugar crystallization yields, and size distillation columns for ethanol production.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section contains **fully worked-out, exhaustive derivations** for representative problems from each of the eight unit operations. Every algebraic transition is explicitly shown.

### 3.1 EVAPORATION — Worked Problem

**Problem:** A single-effect evaporator concentrates 10,000 kg/h of tomato juice from 5% total solids to 30% total solids. Calculate (i) the mass of water evaporated and (ii) the mass of concentrated product.

**Given:** $F = 10{,}000$ kg/h, $x_F = 0.05$, $x_L = 0.30$.

**Find:** $V$ (water evaporated) and $L$ (concentrate).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the two governing equations.**

Overall mass balance:

$$F = V + L$$

Component (solid) mass balance:

$$F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values into the solid balance.**

$$10{,}000 \times 0.05 = L \times 0.30$$

$$500 = 0.30 \, L$$

**Step 3 — Solve for L.**

$$L = \frac{500}{0.30} = 1666.67 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for V using the overall balance.**

$$V = F - L = 10{,}000 - 1666.67 = 8333.33 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Result:** **L = 1666.67 kg/h** of concentrate and **V = 8333.33 kg/h** of water evaporated.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Validation:** A quick check shows solids out = $1666.67 \times 0.30 = 500$ kg/h = solids in. ✓

---

### 3.2 CRYSTALLIZATION — Worked Problem

**Problem:** A sugar solution containing 5000 kg of water and 2500 kg of sugar is cooled. 1000 kg of sugar crystals (containing 5% water by mass as adhering mother liquor) are recovered. Calculate the mass and composition of the mother liquor.

**Given:** Feed: 2500 kg sugar + 5000 kg water = 7500 kg total. Crystals $C = 1000$ kg, crystals contain 5% water (so 95% sugar crystal + 5% mother liquor by mass).

**Step 1 — Composition of crystals.**

Sugar in crystals: $C \cdot x_{C,\text{sugar}} = 1000 \times 0.95 = 950$ kg.
Water in crystals: $1000 \times 0.05 = 50$ kg.

**Step 2 — Sugar balance to find mother liquor composition.**

Total sugar in feed = 2500 kg.
Sugar in crystals = 950 kg.
Sugar in mother liquor = $2500 - 950 = 1550$ kg.

**Step 3 — Water balance to find mother liquor mass.**

Total water in feed = 5000 kg.
Water in crystals = 50 kg.
Water in mother liquor = $5000 - 50 = 4950$ kg.

**Step 4 — Mother liquor mass and composition.**

$$M = 1550 + 4950 = 6500 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Sugar mass fraction in } M = \frac{1550}{6500} = 0.2385 = 23.85\%$$

**Result:** **Mother liquor mass = 6500 kg**, containing **23.85% sugar**.

---

### 3.3 MIXING — Worked Problem

**Problem:** A milk-blending plant mixes 800 kg of skim milk (0.5% fat) with 200 kg of whole milk (4% fat). Find the fat content of the blended product.

**Step 1 — Overall balance.**

$$P = 800 + 200 = 1000 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Component (fat) balance.**

$$P \cdot x_P = 800 \times 0.005 + 200 \times 0.04$$
$$1000 \cdot x_P = 4 + 8 = 12$$
$$x_P = \frac{12}{1000} = 0.012 = 1.2\%$$

**Result:** Blended milk fat content = **1.2%**.

---

### 3.4 LEACHING — Worked Problem

**Problem:** 1000 kg of sugar beet slices (20% sugar, 80% insoluble solids by mass) are leached with 800 kg of water. After leaching, the underflow (wet insoluble solids) contains 1 kg of solution per kg of insoluble solid, and the solution in the underflow has 15% sugar. Calculate: (i) the mass of overflow (leachate), (ii) the mass of sugar recovered in the overflow.

**Step 1 — Identify streams.**

- Feed solid (A) = 1000 kg (200 kg sugar + 800 kg insoluble).
- Solvent (B) = 800 kg water (0% sugar).
- Underflow (S) = insoluble solids + retained solution.

**Step 2 — Insoluble solid in underflow.**

Insoluble solids are conserved: $S_{\text{insol}} = 800$ kg.
Solution retained in underflow = $800 \times 1 = 800$ kg (since 1 kg solution per kg insoluble).
Total underflow mass: $S = 800 + 800 = 1600$ kg.

**Step 3 — Sugar in underflow.**

Solution in underflow contains 15% sugar:
$$\text{Sugar in } S = 0.15 \times 800 = 120 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Overall balance to find overflow (L).**

$$A + B = L + S$$
$$1000 + 800 = L + 1600$$
$$L = 200 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Sugar balance to find sugar in overflow.**

$$A \cdot x_{A,\text{sugar}} + B \cdot x_{B,\text{sugar}} = L \cdot x_{L,\text{sugar}} + S \cdot x_{S,\text{sugar}}$$
$$1000 \times 0.20 + 800 \times 0 = L \cdot x_L + 120$$
$$200 = L \cdot x_L + 120$$
$$L \cdot x_L = 80 \text{ kg sugar in overflow}$$

**Result:** **Overflow = 200 kg**, containing **80 kg of sugar** (40% sugar by mass).

---

### 3.5 EXTRACTION — Worked Problem

**Problem:** 1000 kg of a feed containing 30% acetic acid in water is extracted with 500 kg of pure isopropyl ether (assuming acetic acid-free solvent). After one stage, the extract contains 25% acetic acid and the raffinate contains 7% acetic acid. Verify mass balance and find extract & raffinate masses.

**Step 1 — Define unknowns.**

- $F = 1000$ kg, $x_F = 0.30$ (acetic acid in feed).
- $S = 500$ kg, $x_S = 0$ (pure solvent).
- $E$ = extract, $y_E = 0.25$.
- $R$ = raffinate, $x_R = 0.07$.

**Step 2 — Overall balance.**

$$F + S = E + R$$
$$1000 + 500 = E + R$$
$$E + R = 1500 \text{ kg} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3 — Acetic acid balance.**

$$F x_F + S x_S = E y_E + R x_R$$
$$1000 \times 0.30 + 500 \times 0 = 0.25 E + 0.07 R$$
$$300 = 0.25 E + 0.07 R \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve simultaneously.**

From (i): $R = 1500 - E$.

Substitute into (ii):

$$300 = 0.25 E + 0.07(1500 - E)$$
$$300 = 0.25 E + 105 - 0.07 E$$
$$300 - 105 = 0.18 E$$
$$195 = 0.18 E$$
$$E = 1083.33 \text{ kg}$$

$$R = 1500 - 1083.33 = 416.67 \text{ kg}$$

**Result:** **Extract E = 1083.33 kg**, **Raffinate R = 416.67 kg**.

---

### 3.6 ABSORPTION — Worked Problem

**Problem:** A gas mixture containing 20 mol% CO₂ and 80 mol% N₂ is fed at 1000 mol/h to an absorber. The entering absorbent liquid contains no CO₂. The exiting gas contains 5 mol% CO₂, and the exiting liquid contains 0.02 mol CO₂ per mol of liquid. Calculate the molar flow rates of the exiting gas and liquid streams.

**Step 1 — Identify unknowns.**

- $G_1 = 1000$ mol/h, $y_1 = 0.20$ (CO₂ in gas in).
- $L_2$ = unknown, $x_2 = 0$ (pure liquid solvent).
- $G_2$ = unknown, $y_2 = 0.05$ (CO₂ in gas out).
- $L_1$ = unknown, $x_1 = 0.02$ (CO₂ in liquid out).

**Step 2 — Component (CO₂) balance.**

$$G_1 y_1 + L_2 x_2 = G_2 y_2 + L_1 x_1$$
$$1000 \times 0.20 + L_2 \times 0 = G_2 \times 0.05 + L_1 \times 0.02$$
$$200 = 0.05 G_2 + 0.02 L_1 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3 — Overall balance (N₂ is inert, so it is conserved).**

N₂ in = N₂ out:
$$G_1 \times 0.80 = G_2 \times (1 - 0.05) = 0.95 \, G_2$$
$$800 = 0.95 \, G_2$$
$$G_2 = 842.11 \text{ mol/h}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for L₁ using (i).**

$$200 = 0.05 \times 842.11 + 0.02 L_1$$
$$200 = 42.105 + 0.02 L_1$$
$$157.895 = 0.02 L_1$$
$$L_1 = 7894.75 \text{ mol/h}$$

**Step 5 — Overall balance for L₂.**

$$G_1 + L_2 = G_2 + L_1$$
$$1000 + L_2 = 842.11 + 7894.75$$
$$L_2 = 7736.86 \text{ mol/h}$$

**Result:** **Exiting gas $G_2 = 842.11$ mol/h**, **Exiting liquid $L_1 = 7894.75$ mol/h**.

---

### 3.7 DISTILLATION — Worked Problem

**Problem:** A distillation column is fed with 100 kmol/h of a benzene-toluene mixture containing 40 mol% benzene. The distillate contains 90 mol% benzene and the bottoms contain 5 mol% benzene. Find the distillate and bottoms flow rates.

**Step 1 — Define unknowns.**

- $F = 100$ kmol/h, $x_F = 0.40$.
- $D$ = distillate, $x_D = 0.90$.
- $B$ = bottoms, $x_B = 0.05$.

**Step 2 — Overall balance.**

$$F = D + B$$
$$100 = D + B \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3 — Benzene balance.**

$$F x_F = D x_D + B x_B$$
$$100 \times 0.40 = 0.90 D + 0.05 B$$
$$40 = 0.90 D + 0.05 B \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve simultaneously.**

From (i): $B = 100 - D$.

Substitute into (ii):

$$40 = 0.90 D + 0.05 (100 - D)$$
$$40 = 0.90 D + 5 - 0.05 D$$
$$35 = 0.85 D$$
$$D = 41.18 \text{ kmol/h}$$

$$B = 100 - 41.18 = 58.82 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Result:** **Distillate D = 41.18 kmol/h**, **Bottoms B = 58.82 kmol/h**.

---

### 3.8 DRYING — Worked Problem

**Problem:** A dryer receives 2000 kg/h of wet food containing 80% water (wet basis). The dried product contains 5% water (wet basis). Calculate: (i) the mass of dried product and (ii) the mass of water evaporated.

**Step 1 — Convert wet basis to dry basis for both streams.**

**Feed (80% wb):**
$$X_{F,db} = \frac{0.80}{1 - 0.80} = \frac{0.80}{0.20} = 4.0 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Product (5% wb):**
$$X_{P,db} = \frac{0.05}{1 - 0.05} = \frac{0.05}{0.95} = 0.0526 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 2 — Dry solid mass in feed (conserved).**

Dry solid in feed = $F \times (1 - x_{F,wb}) = 2000 \times 0.20 = 400$ kg/h.
Dry solid in product = 400 kg/h (conservation).

**Step 3 — Find product mass.**

$$P = \text{Dry solid} + \text{Water in product} = 400 + 400 \times 0.0526$$
$$P = 400 + 21.05 = 421.05 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Water evaporated.**

$$W = F - P = 2000 - 421.05 = 1578.95 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Verify with water balance.**

Water in feed = $2000 \times 0.80 = 1600$ kg/h.
Water in product = $421.05 \times 0.05 = 21.05$ kg/h.
Water evaporated = $1600 - 21.05 = 1578.95$ kg/h. ✓

**Result:** **Dried product P = 421.05 kg/h**, **Water evaporated W = 1578.95 kg/h**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a drying problem gives moisture on a **wet basis**, ALWAYS convert to **dry basis** before applying the material balance. This avoids the most common error in Module 2 problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the terms **'overflow'** and **'underflow'** as used in a single-stage leaching operation. Write the general material balance equations for a single-stage leaching system.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Overflow (L):** The solution leaving the leaching tank that is **separated from the solid**; it is rich in the solute that has been leached out.
> **Underflow (S):** The stream leaving with the **insoluble solids**, carrying with it a portion of retained solution (and thus some un-recovered solute).
>
> **Material balances for single-stage leaching:**
>
> Overall: $A + B = L + S$
>
> Solute: $A \cdot a + B \cdot b = L \cdot l + S \cdot s$
>
> Where $A$ = feed solid, $B$ = solvent, $L$ = overflow, $S$ = underflow, and the lowercase letters represent mass fractions of solute in each stream.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Distinguish between **wet-basis** and **dry-basis** moisture content. A food material has 75% moisture (wb). Express this moisture content on a dry basis.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Wet basis (wb):** Moisture expressed as a percentage of the **total mass** of the wet material. $X_{wb} = m_{water} / m_{total}$.
>
> **Dry basis (db):** Moisture expressed per unit mass of **dry solid**. $X_{db} = m_{water} / m_{dry\,solid}$.
>
> For 75% wb:
>
> $$X_{db} = \frac{0.75}{1 - 0.75} = \frac{0.75}{0.25} = 3.0 \text{ kg water / kg dry solid}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** A single-effect evaporator is used to concentrate **8000 kg/h of orange juice** from an initial concentration of **7% total solids to 50% total solids**.

**(a)** (7 marks) Calculate the **mass flow rate of water evaporated** and the **mass flow rate of the concentrated juice**.

**(b)** (7 marks) The concentrated juice is subsequently fed to a **vacuum dryer** to produce a final powder containing **3% moisture (wet basis)**. Calculate the **mass of dried powder produced per hour** and the **mass of water removed in the dryer**.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Given: $F = 8000$ kg/h, $x_F = 0.07$, $x_L = 0.50$.

Overall balance: $F = V + L$ ...(i)
Solid balance: $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$ ...(ii)

From (ii):
$$L = \frac{8000 \times 0.07}{0.50} = \frac{560}{0.50} = 1120 \text{ kg/h}$$

From (i):
$$V = 8000 - 1120 = 6880 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Result (a):** Concentrated juice $L = 1120$ kg/h; Water evaporated $V = 6880$ kg/h.

**Part (b):**

The concentrated juice from the evaporator (50% solids) is the **wet feed to the dryer**. It contains 50% moisture wb.

Given: $F_{ws} = 1120$ kg/h, $X_{F,wb} = 0.50$, $X_{P,wb} = 0.03$.

**Step 1 — Convert to dry basis.**

$$X_{F,db} = \frac{0.50}{1 - 0.50} = 1.0 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$
$$X_{P,db} = \frac{0.03}{1 - 0.03} = \frac{0.03}{0.97} = 0.03093 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 2 — Find dry solid mass (conserved).**

$$\text{Dry solid} = 1120 \times (1 - 0.50) = 560 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Find product mass.**

$$P = \text{Dry solid} + \text{Water in product} = 560 + 560 \times 0.03093 = 560 + 17.32 = 577.32 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Water removed in dryer.**

$$W = F_{ws} - P = 1120 - 577.32 = 542.68 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Result (b):** Dried powder = 577.32 kg/h; Water removed in dryer = 542.68 kg/h.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** A continuous **single-stage leaching** system processes **1500 kg/h of crushed oilseeds** containing **28% oil** (by mass) and **72% insoluble meal**. The seeds are contacted with **1000 kg/h of fresh hexane** containing no oil.

**(a)** (7 marks) After leaching, the **underflow** consists of insoluble meal with **0.5 kg of solution per kg of insoluble solid**, and the **retained solution in the underflow contains 8% oil**. Calculate the **mass of overflow** and the **mass of oil in the overflow**.

**(b)** (7 marks) The overflow from the leaching system is fed to an **evaporator** to recover pure hexane and crude oil. If the overflow contains **75% hexane and 25% oil (by mass)** and the final oil product contains **1% residual hexane (by mass)**, calculate the **mass of crude oil recovered** and the **mass of hexane recovered**.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify inputs.**

- $A = 1500$ kg/h (200 kg oil + 1300 kg insoluble = wait, recalculate: 28% × 1500 = 420 kg oil, 72% × 1500 = 1080 kg insoluble).
- $B = 1000$ kg/h (pure hexane, 0% oil).
- Insoluble in underflow = 1080 kg/h.
- Solution retained in underflow = $1080 \times 0.5 = 540$ kg/h.
- Underflow mass $S = 1080 + 540 = 1620$ kg/h.
- Oil in underflow solution = $540 \times 0.08 = 43.2$ kg/h.

**Step 2 — Overall balance to find overflow.**

$$A + B = L + S$$
$$1500 + 1000 = L + 1620$$
$$L = 880 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Oil balance to find oil in overflow.**

$$420 + 0 = L \cdot y_L + 43.2$$
$$L \cdot y_L = 376.8 \text{ kg/h oil in overflow}$$

**Result (a):** **Overflow L = 880 kg/h**; **Oil in overflow = 376.8 kg/h**.

**Part (b):**

The overflow is the feed to the evaporator. Given: $F_{evap} = 880$ kg/h, composition = 75% hexane + 25% oil.

**Step 1 — Identify streams.**

- Hexane in feed = $880 \times 0.75 = 660$ kg/h.
- Oil in feed = $880 \times 0.25 = 220$ kg/h.

**Step 2 — Final oil product contains 1% hexane → 99% oil.**

Let $P$ = mass of crude oil product.
- Oil in product = $0.99 P$ kg/h.
- Hexane in product = $0.01 P$ kg/h.

**Step 3 — Overall balance.**

$$F_{evap} = P + V_{hexane}$$
$$880 = P + V_{hexane} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 4 — Oil balance (oil is non-volatile, stays in liquid).**

$$220 = 0.99 P$$
$$P = 222.22 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Hexane recovered (overhead vapor).**

$$V_{hexane} = 880 - 222.22 = 657.78 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Verification:** Hexane out = hexane in product + hexane recovered = $0.01 \times 222.22 + 657.78 = 2.22 + 657.78 = 660$ kg/h ✓

**Result (b):** **Crude oil recovered = 222.22 kg/h**; **Hexane recovered = 657.78 kg/h**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency:** Always carry units through every step. Converting kg/h to kg/s or vice versa without updating all values is the **#1 mark-deduction error** in Part B problems.
> 2. **Basis confusion in leaching:** Many students incorrectly assume the underflow solution has the same composition as the overflow. The underflow's solution composition is a **separate, given parameter** (e.g., 8% oil above) — never assume it equals the overflow.
> 3. **Wet vs. dry basis in drying:** Stating moisture content on the wrong basis causes the entire drying calculation to fail. **Always check** whether the problem says "wet basis" or "dry basis."
> 4. **Sign convention in absorption:** Inlet gas $G_1$ + inlet liquid $L_2$ = outlet gas $G_2$ + outlet liquid $L_1$. Reversing the subscripts is a frequent error.
> 5. **Solvent purity:** In leaching/extraction problems where "pure solvent" is stated, the solvent's solute mass fraction is **0**, not an unknown. This must be set immediately to reduce the system's degrees of freedom.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Material Balance in Unit Operations**

- **Law of Conservation of Mass** is the foundational principle: $F_{in} = F_{out}$ at steady state, with $G = C = 0$ for non-reactive systems.
- **Universal template:** Every food unit operation is solved using an **overall mass balance** + a **component mass balance** = 2 equations for 2 unknowns.
- **Evaporation:** $F = V + L$; $F x_F = L x_L$. Volatile component (water) leaves as vapor; non-volatile solids stay in the concentrate.
- **Crystallization:** $F = C + M$. Crystals are usually **wet** — they carry adhering mother liquor. Always perform a separate solute + solvent balance.
- **Mixing:** Multi-stream. Use summation: $\sum F_i = P$ and $\sum F_i x_i = P x_P$.
- **Leaching:** Underflow contains **insoluble solids + retained solution**. The underflow solution composition is **a given, not equal to the overflow composition**.
- **Extraction:** Two immiscible liquid phases — **Extract (E)** and **Raffinate (R)**. Use different symbols ($y$ vs. $x$) for their respective compositions.
- **Absorption:** Inert gas (e.g., N₂) is conserved — use this to find one stream's flow rate directly before solving the rest.
- **Distillation:** Distillate (D) is rich in the **more volatile component (MVC)**; Bottoms (B) is rich in the **less volatile component (LVC)**. Use **mole fractions** when boiling points differ widely; **mass fractions** otherwise.
- **Drying — most common error:** Moisture content can be on **wet basis (wb)** or **dry basis (db)**. **Convert before balancing.** Conversion: $X_{db} = X_{wb} / (1 - X_{wb})$.
- **In all problems:** Always carry **units**; verify by plugging answers back into both balance equations.
- **KTU 2024 Pattern:** A typical 14-mark question combines **two unit operations in series** (e.g., evaporation + drying, or leaching + evaporation). Practice multi-step cascades — they constitute ~40% of recent past papers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Capacity of Solids, Liquids, Gases, and Solutions; Use of Mean Heat Capacity in Heat Calculations; Problems Involving Sensible Heat and Latent Heat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT302 - FOOD THERMODYNAMICS AND PROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Capacity of Solids, Liquids, Gases, and Solutions; Use of Mean Heat Capacity in Heat Calculations; Problems Involving Sensible Heat and Latent Heat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Heat Capacity

**Heat Capacity ($C$)** is defined as the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of a given quantity of a substance by one degree (either Celsius or Kelvin). It is an extensive property that depends on the mass and identity of the substance.

Mathematically,

$$C = \frac{dQ}{dT}$$

where $dQ$ is the infinitesimal heat supplied and $dT$ is the corresponding temperature change.

**Specific Heat Capacity ($C_p$ or $C_v$)** is the heat capacity per unit mass of the substance. It is an intensive property.

$$c = \frac{C}{m} = \frac{1}{m}\frac{dQ}{dT} \quad \text{[units: kJ/(kg·K)]}$$

**Molar Heat Capacity ($\bar{C}$)** is the heat capacity per mole of substance.

$$\bar{C} = \frac{C}{n} \quad \text{[units: kJ/(kmol·K)]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Distinction:**
> - **$C_p$** = Heat capacity at **constant pressure** (relevant for open/flow processes like food processing)
> - **$C_v$** = Heat capacity at **constant volume** (relevant for closed/batch processes)
> - For an **ideal gas**: $C_p - C_v = R$ (the universal gas constant = **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)**)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Water Bucket Experiment":**
> Imagine you have three identical buckets — one filled with **water** (liquid), one with **sand** (solid), and one empty, into which you can pass **steam** (gas). You pour the same amount of heat (same "energy packet") into each. The **sand** heats up very quickly, the **water** warms up slowly (because hydrogen bonds store energy), and the **steam** temperature rises rapidly. This is intuitively why different phases (solid/liquid/gas) and different substances have **drastically different heat capacities**. Food engineers must therefore know $C_p$ values accurately to design heat exchangers, sterilizers, and pasteurizers.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Universal Gas Constant ($R$) | **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** |
| Specific heat of water (liquid, 25°C) | **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** |
| Latent heat of fusion of water (0°C) | **333.55 kJ/kg** |
| Latent heat of vaporization of water (100°C) | **2257 kJ/kg** |
| Standard reference temperature | **0°C = 273.15 K** |

### 1.4 Classification of Heat Capacity Problems in Food Processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Food process calculations primarily revolve around two types of heat:
> 1. **Sensible Heat** — Heat that causes a *measurable temperature change* (e.g., heating milk from 20°C to 80°C during pasteurization). Calculated using $C_p$.
> 2. **Latent Heat** — Heat that causes a *phase change* without a temperature change (e.g., boiling water at 100°C to form steam in a retort). Calculated using the latent heat value $\lambda$.
>
> A typical food process involves **both** — heating water as liquid (sensible) and then vaporizing part of it (latent).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Heat Capacity of Solids

**Dulong–Petit Law (Approximate Rule for Solid Elements):**

At ordinary temperatures, the molar heat capacity of most solid elements is approximately constant:

$$\bar{C}_p \approx 25.93 \; \text{kJ/(kmol·K)} \approx 3R$$

This rule fails for light elements (carbon, boron, silicon) at low temperatures and for substances below the Debye temperature.

**Kopp's Rule (for Solid Compounds — Important for Food Solids):**

The molar heat capacity of a solid compound is approximated as the **sum of the atomic heat capacities** of its constituent elements.

$$\bar{C}_{p,\text{compound}} = \sum n_i \, \bar{C}_{p,i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Kopp's Approximate Atomic Heat Capacities (kJ/(kmol·K)):**
> - C = 11.7, H = 9.6, O = 16.7, N = 18.6, S = 22.6, P = 22.6
> - F = 21.4, Si = 20.0, B = 13.6

**Example (Sucrose, $\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11}$):**

$$\bar{C}_p = 12(11.7) + 22(9.6) + 11(16.7)$$
$$= 140.4 + 211.2 + 183.7 = 535.3 \; \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$$

Dividing by molar mass (342.3 kg/kmol):

$$C_p = \frac{535.3}{342.3} \approx 1.56 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

Experimental value ≈ **1.55 kJ/(kg·K)** — excellent agreement!

### 2.2 Heat Capacity of Liquids

Unlike gases, no simple theoretical formula exists. For organic liquids, an empirical approximation (approaching Trouton's rule constraints) gives:

$$\bar{C}_p \approx 2.5R \approx 20.8 \; \text{kJ/(kmol·K)} \quad \text{(rough estimate)}$$

For water specifically, $C_p$ varies with temperature:

$$C_{p,\text{water}} = 4.185 - 5.35 \times 10^{-4} \, T + \cdots \; \text{kJ/(kg·°C)}$$

For dilute **aqueous food solutions**, a useful engineering approximation is:

$$C_{p,\text{solution}} = C_{p,\text{water}} \, w_w + \sum C_{p,i} \, w_i$$

where $w_w$ and $w_i$ are the mass fractions of water and solute $i$ respectively.

### 2.3 Heat Capacity of Gases

For an **ideal gas**, the kinetic theory gives:

$$\bar{C}_p - \bar{C}_v = R = 8.314 \; \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$$

The ratio $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$ depends on molecular structure:

| Gas Type | Degrees of Freedom | $\bar{C}_p$ (kJ/kmol·K) | $\bar{C}_v$ (kJ/kmol·K) | $\gamma$ |
|----------|-------------------|-------------------------|-------------------------|----------|
| Monatomic (Ar, He) | 3 | 20.785 | 12.471 | 1.667 |
| Diatomic (N₂, O₂) | 5 | 29.099 | 20.785 | 1.400 |
| Polyatomic (CO₂, NH₃) | 6+ | >29.099 | >20.785 | <1.400 |

For real gases, $C_p$ is generally expressed as a polynomial in temperature (often in Kelvin):

$$C_p = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Processing Relevance:**
> Most food processes (heating, sterilization, drying, freezing) occur at **constant pressure** (open vessels, flow systems), so **$C_p$** is the heat capacity primarily used in food engineering calculations.

### 2.4 Mean Heat Capacity — Definition & Use

The **true (instantaneous) heat capacity** $C_p$ varies with temperature. To simplify integration in heat-balance problems, the **mean heat capacity** between $T_1$ and $T_2$ is defined as:

$$\bar{C}_p^{T_1 \to T_2} = \frac{1}{T_2 - T_1} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p(T) \, dT$$

Then the **sensible heat** required is simply:

$$Q_s = m \, \bar{C}_p^{T_1 \to T_2} \, (T_2 - T_1)$$

This avoids tedious integration and is the **standard approach in KTU ESE numerical problems**.

### 2.5 Latent Heat — Conceptual Background

**Latent Heat of Fusion ($\lambda_f$):** Heat required to convert 1 kg of solid → liquid at its melting point.

**Latent Heat of Vaporization ($\lambda_v$):** Heat required to convert 1 kg of liquid → vapor at its boiling point.

**Latent Heat of Sublimation:** Solid → vapor directly.

For water:

$$\lambda_f = 333.55 \; \text{kJ/kg} \quad \text{at } 0°C$$
$$\lambda_v = 2257 \; \text{kJ/kg} \quad \text{at } 100°C$$

Watson's Equation (for latent heat at any temperature $T$):

$$\lambda_v(T_2) = \lambda_v(T_1)\left[\frac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right]^{0.38}$$

where $T_c$ is the critical temperature of water (**647.1 K**).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{dQ}{dT}$ | Definition of heat capacity | kJ/K |
| 2 | $c = \dfrac{C}{m}$ | Specific heat capacity | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 3 | $\bar{C} = \dfrac{C}{n}$ | Molar heat capacity | kJ/(kmol·K) |
| 4 | $\bar{C}_p - \bar{C}_v = R$ | Ideal gas relation | kJ/(kmol·K) |
| 5 | $\bar{C}_p \approx 3R$ | Dulong–Petit rule (solids) | kJ/(kmol·K) |
| 6 | $\bar{C}_p = \sum n_i \bar{C}_{p,i}$ | Kopp's rule (compounds) | kJ/(kmol·K) |
| 7 | $Q_s = m \, C_p \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat (constant $C_p$) | kJ |
| 8 | $Q_s = m \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p(T) dT$ | Sensible heat (variable $C_p$) | kJ |
| 9 | $Q_s = m \, \bar{C}_p^{T_1 \to T_2} \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat (using mean $C_p$) | kJ |
| 10 | $Q_L = m \, \lambda$ | Latent heat (phase change) | kJ |
| 11 | $\lambda_v(T_2) = \lambda_v(T_1) \left[\frac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right]^{0.38}$ | Watson's equation | kJ/kg |
| 12 | $C_{p,\text{sol}} = C_{p,w} w_w + \sum C_{p,i} w_i$ | Solution $C_p$ (mass fraction) | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 13 | $\bar{C}_p^{T_1 \to T_2} = \frac{1}{T_2-T_1} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p(T) dT$ | Mean heat capacity definition | kJ/(kmol·K) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conversion Reminder:**
> - To convert Celsius difference to Kelvin: $\Delta T$°C = $\Delta T$ K (numerically identical)
> - To convert a temperature value: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$
> - 1 kcal = 4.186 kJ

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: $\bar{C}_p - \bar{C}_v = R$ for an Ideal Gas

Starting from the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a closed system (per mole of ideal gas):

$$dQ = dU + dW$$

At **constant pressure**: $dW = P \, dV$ and $dU = \bar{C}_v \, dT$ for an ideal gas.

By definition at constant pressure: $dQ = \bar{C}_p \, dT$.

Substituting:

$$\bar{C}_p \, dT = \bar{C}_v \, dT + P \, dV$$

For 1 mole of ideal gas, $PV = RT$, so differentiating:

$$P \, dV + V \, dP = R \, dT$$

At constant pressure, $dP = 0$, hence $P \, dV = R \, dT$.

Substituting back:

$$\bar{C}_p \, dT = \bar{C}_v \, dT + R \, dT$$

Dividing by $dT$:

$$\boxed{\bar{C}_p - \bar{C}_v = R = 8.314 \; \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Mean Heat Capacity Expression

The heat required to raise 1 mole of gas from $T_1$ to $T_2$ is:

$$Q = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \bar{C}_p(T) \, dT$$

If we instead use a **mean (constant) heat capacity** $\bar{C}_{p,m}$:

$$Q = \bar{C}_{p,m} \, (T_2 - T_1)$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$\bar{C}_{p,m} \, (T_2 - T_1) = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \bar{C}_p(T) \, dT$$

Solving for the mean:

$$\boxed{\bar{C}_{p,m} = \frac{1}{T_2 - T_1} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \bar{C}_p(T) \, dT}$$

**For a polynomial form** $C_p = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^3$:

$$\bar{C}_{p,m} = a + \frac{b}{2}(T_1 + T_2) + \frac{c}{3}(T_1^2 + T_1 T_2 + T_2^2) + \frac{d}{4}(T_1 + T_2)(T_1^2 + T_2^2)$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problems (Analytical Matrix)

#### **Problem 1 — Sensible Heat with Variable $C_p$**

**Question:** Calculate the heat required to raise the temperature of **10 kg of CO₂** from **300 K to 800 K**, given:
$$C_p(\text{CO}_2) = 26.75 + 42.258 \times 10^{-3} T - 14.25 \times 10^{-6} T^2 \; \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$$

(Molar mass of CO₂ = 44 kg/kmol)

**Solution:**

Number of moles:
$$n = \frac{10}{44} = 0.2273 \; \text{kmol}$$

Heat required per kmol:
$$Q = \int_{300}^{800} C_p \, dT = \int_{300}^{800}\left(26.75 + 42.258 \times 10^{-3} T - 14.25 \times 10^{-6} T^2\right)dT$$

Integrating term by term:
$$Q = \left[26.75 T + 42.258 \times 10^{-3} \frac{T^2}{2} - 14.25 \times 10^{-6} \frac{T^3}{3}\right]_{300}^{800}$$

Computing each term at $T = 800$ K:
$$26.75 \times 800 = 21400$$
$$42.258 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{800^2}{2} = 42.258 \times 10^{-3} \times 320000 = 13522.56$$
$$14.25 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{800^3}{3} = 14.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 170666666.67 = 2432.0$$

At $T = 800$ K: Sum = $21400 + 13522.56 - 2432.0 = 32490.56$ kJ/kmol

At $T = 300$ K:
$$26.75 \times 300 = 8025$$
$$42.258 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{300^2}{2} = 42.258 \times 10^{-3} \times 45000 = 1901.61$$
$$14.25 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{300^3}{3} = 14.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 9{,}000{,}000 = 128.25$$

At $T = 300$ K: Sum = $8025 + 1901.61 - 128.25 = 9798.36$ kJ/kmol

Per kmol heat:
$$Q = 32490.56 - 9798.36 = 22692.2 \; \text{kJ/kmol}$$

Total heat for 10 kg:
$$Q_{\text{total}} = 0.2273 \times 22692.2 = \boxed{5158.6 \; \text{kJ}}$$

---

#### **Problem 2 — Mean Heat Capacity Application**

**Question:** Compute the mean molar heat capacity of CO₂ between 300 K and 800 K from the data above.

**Solution:**

$$\bar{C}_{p,m} = \frac{Q}{T_2 - T_1} = \frac{22692.2}{800 - 300} = \frac{22692.2}{500}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{C}_{p,m} = 45.38 \; \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}}$$

Alternative check using formula directly (treating as if $C_p$ varies linearly): would yield same numerical result by definition of mean value of integral.

---

#### **Problem 3 — Combined Sensible + Latent Heat (Food Processing)**

**Question:** A food sterilizer contains **200 kg of water** initially at **25°C**. It is heated until **20% of the water has vaporized**, after which the temperature reaches **100°C**. Compute the total heat supplied. Use $C_p$(water) = **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** and $\lambda_v$ = **2257 kJ/kg**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sensible heat to raise 200 kg water from 25°C to 100°C:**

$$Q_1 = m \, C_p \, \Delta T = 200 \times 4.186 \times (100 - 25)$$

$$Q_1 = 200 \times 4.186 \times 75 = 62790 \; \text{kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Latent heat to vaporize 20% of water:**

Mass vaporized: $m_v = 0.20 \times 200 = 40$ kg

$$Q_2 = m_v \, \lambda_v = 40 \times 2257 = 90280 \; \text{kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Total heat:**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_1 + Q_2 = 62790 + 90280$$

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{total}} = 153070 \; \text{kJ} \approx 153.07 \; \text{MJ}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The latent heat contribution (~59%) exceeds the sensible heat contribution (~41%), even though only 20% of the water actually changed phase. This underscores **why vaporization is energy-intensive** in food dehydration, evaporation, and concentration processes.

---

#### **Problem 4 — Heat Capacity of an Aqueous Food Solution**

**Question:** A fruit juice contains **12% (by mass) sucrose** and the rest water. Calculate its specific heat capacity. ($C_p$ water = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K), $C_p$ sucrose = 1.55 kJ/(kg·K))

**Solution:**

$$C_{p,\text{juice}} = C_{p,w} \, w_w + C_{p,\text{suc}} \, w_{\text{suc}}$$

$$C_{p,\text{juice}} = 4.186 \times 0.88 + 1.55 \times 0.12$$

$$C_{p,\text{juice}} = 3.6837 + 0.186 = \boxed{3.870 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}}$$

This is a **standard rule-of-mixtures** used extensively in food engineering — it is exact for ideal solutions and an excellent approximation for dilute food products.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Heat Capacity Forms (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Heat Capacity C] --> B[Intensive Forms]
    A --> C[Extensive Forms]
    B --> D[Specific Heat c - per unit mass]
    B --> E[Molar Heat Capacity - per unit mole]
    D --> F[At Constant Pressure Cp]
    D --> G[At Constant Volume Cv]
    E --> H[At Constant Pressure Cp bar]
    E --> I[At Constant Volume Cv bar]
    F --> J[Mean Cp bar between T1 and T2]
    F --> K[True Cp at Temperature T]
    J --> L[Used for Sensible Heat Calculations]
    K --> M[Integrated to get Sensible Heat]
    C --> N[Total Heat Capacity of System]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture for Heat Balance

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN1[Input Stream 1 - mass m1, T1, phase] --> MIX[Mixing / Heating Block]
    IN2[Input Stream 2 - mass m2, T2, phase] --> MIX
    MIX --> CALC{Sensible or Phase Change?}
    CALC -->|Sensible| SEN[Q_s = m Cp dT block]
    CALC -->|Phase Change| LAT[Q_L = m lambda block]
    CALC -->|Both Combined| COM[Q_total = Q_s + Q_L block]
    SEN --> SUM[Total Heat Energy Q]
    LAT --> SUM
    COM --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Output Heat Duty in kW or kJ]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Selecting $C_p$ in Food Process Calculations

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Start - Identify Substance] --> Q1{Solid Pure Element?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| DP[Use Dulong-Petit: Cp = 3R per kmol]
    Q1 --> No --> Q2{Solid Compound?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| KR[Use Kopps Rule - Sum atomic Cp]
    Q2 --> No --> Q3{Liquid Pure?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| LIQ[Use tabulated Cp or Trouton approx]
    Q3 --> No --> Q4{Aqueous Solution?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| SOL[Use mass fraction rule of mixtures]
    Q4 --> No --> Q5{Ideal Gas?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| GAS[Use Cp polynomial or tabulated mean Cp]
    Q5 --> No --> Q6[Consult real gas charts or PVT data]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation for Students:**
> The flowcharts above are *not physical drawings* (which are impossible to render in Mermaid for topics like calorimeters), but rather **functional decision trees** showing the engineering logic you follow when *encountering* a heat-capacity-related problem in an actual food process calculation.
