## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define Mean Heat Capacity. State its significance in process calculations.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **mean molar heat capacity** $\bar{C}_{p,m}$ between two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ is defined as:

$$\bar{C}_{p,m} = \frac{1}{T_2 - T_1} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \bar{C}_p(T) \, dT$$

**Significance:**
- It replaces the temperature-dependent $C_p(T)$ with a single constant value over the temperature range.
- It converts the integral $Q = \int C_p dT$ into a simple product $Q = \bar{C}_{p,m} \Delta T$.
- It **drastically simplifies engineering heat-balance calculations** in food processing equipment (heaters, exchangers, retorts).
- Mean heat capacity values are tabulated in standard references and are acceptable to use directly in KTU examinations.

#### **Question 2: Differentiate between Sensible Heat and Latent Heat with one food-processing example each.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Sensible Heat | Latent Heat |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| Definition | Heat causing measurable temperature change | Heat causing phase change at constant temperature |
| Formula | $Q_s = m \, C_p \, \Delta T$ | $Q_L = m \, \lambda$ |
| Phase change | None | Solid–liquid / liquid–vapour |
| Thermometer effect | Yes (temperature rises) | No (temperature constant) |

- **Sensible Heat Example:** Heating milk from 20°C to 80°C in a pasteurizer — temperature rises, no phase change.
- **Latent Heat Example:** Vaporizing water at 100°C inside an evaporator during juice concentration — temperature stays at 100°C while phase changes from liquid to vapour.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **frequently lose marks** by forgetting to convert units (e.g., using $R = 1.987$ cal/(mol·K) instead of $8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K), or mixing kg vs kmol).
> - In Kopp's Rule, students often **omit the multiplier for the number of atoms** of each element — a one-step loss worth up to 2 marks.
> - For variable $C_p$ integration, **forgetting the negative sign** in $cT^2$ term evaluation at upper and lower limits is the single most common error.
> - In Watson's equation, students forget that **$T$ must be in Kelvin** (not Celsius). Always convert.
> - Mark distribution: Sub-part (a) = **7 marks** (concept/derivation), Sub-part (b) = **7 marks** (numerical).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of the First Law of Thermodynamics, derive the relation $\bar{C}_p - \bar{C}_v = R$ for one mole of an ideal gas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The molar heat capacity of a gas is given by:
$$C_p = 28.07 + 1.96 \times 10^{-3} T + 4.44 \times 10^{-5} T^2 \; \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$$

Calculate: **(i)** the mean heat capacity between 300 K and 1500 K, **(ii)** the heat required to raise 50 kg of this gas (M = 28 kg/kmol) from 300 K to 1500 K. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Applied to 1 mole of ideal gas at constant pressure, the First Law gives:

$$dQ = dU + P \, dV$$

At constant pressure: $dQ = \bar{C}_p \, dT$, and $dU = \bar{C}_v \, dT$ (since internal energy of an ideal gas depends only on temperature).

$$\bar{C}_p \, dT = \bar{C}_v \, dT + P \, dV$$

For ideal gas: $PV = RT \Rightarrow P \, dV = R \, dT$ (at constant $P$).

Substituting:

$$\bar{C}_p \, dT = \bar{C}_v \, dT + R \, dT \Rightarrow \boxed{\bar{C}_p - \bar{C}_v = R}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i)** Integration:
$$\int_{300}^{1500} C_p \, dT = \left[28.07 T + 1.96 \times 10^{-3} \frac{T^2}{2} + 4.44 \times 10^{-5} \frac{T^3}{3}\right]_{300}^{1500}$$

At $T = 1500$ K:
$$28.07 \times 1500 = 42105.0$$
$$1.96 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{1500^2}{2} = 1.96 \times 10^{-3} \times 1125000 = 2205.0$$
$$4.44 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{1500^3}{3} = 4.44 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.125 \times 10^9 = 49950.0$$
Sum = $42105 + 2205 + 49950 = 94260$ kJ/kmol

At $T = 300$ K:
$$28.07 \times 300 = 8421.0$$
$$1.96 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{300^2}{2} = 1.96 \times 10^{-3} \times 45000 = 88.2$$
$$4.44 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{300^3}{3} = 4.44 \times 10^{-5} \times 27000000 = 1198.8$$
Sum = $8421 + 88.2 + 1198.8 = 9708.0$ kJ/kmol

Difference: $94260 - 9708 = 84552$ kJ/kmol

Mean $C_p$:
$$\bar{C}_{p,m} = \frac{84552}{1500 - 300} = \frac{84552}{1200} = \boxed{70.46 \; \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}}$$

**(ii)** Moles of gas: $n = 50/28 = 1.786$ kmol

$$Q = n \times \bar{C}_{p,m} \times \Delta T = 1.786 \times 70.46 \times 1200$$

Or directly: $Q = n \times 84552 = 1.786 \times 84552$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 150930 \; \text{kJ} \approx 150.93 \; \text{MJ}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define and explain Kopp's rule for estimating the molar heat capacity of solid compounds. Apply Kopp's rule to estimate the molar heat capacity of **glucose ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$, M = 180 kg/kmol)** and its specific heat capacity. Atomic $C_p$ values: C = 11.7, H = 9.6, O = 16.7 kJ/(kmol·K). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A milk-processing plant receives **5000 kg/h of milk at 10°C**. It must be heated to **72°C** for pasteurization and then to **95°C** for sterilization. Estimate the total hourly heat duty if $C_p$(milk) = **3.93 kJ/(kg·K)**. If 8% of water in the milk evaporates during sterilization at 95°C, calculate the additional latent heat load. Take $\lambda_v$ at 95°C = **2268 kJ/kg**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Kopp's Rule Definition:**

Kopp's rule states that the molar heat capacity of a solid compound at ordinary temperatures is approximately equal to the **sum of the atomic heat capacities** of all the constituent elements in one mole of the compound.

$$\bar{C}_{p,\text{compound}} = \sum_i n_i \, \bar{C}_{p,i}$$

where $n_i$ is the number of atoms of element $i$ per molecule and $\bar{C}_{p,i}$ is its atomic heat capacity.

**Application to Glucose ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$):**

$$\bar{C}_p = 6 \times 11.7 + 12 \times 9.6 + 6 \times 16.7$$

$$\bar{C}_p = 70.2 + 115.2 + 100.2 = \boxed{285.6 \; \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}}$$

**Specific heat capacity:**

$$C_p = \frac{\bar{C}_p}{M} = \frac{285.6}{180} = \boxed{1.587 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Sensible heat to heat milk from 10°C to 72°C (pasteurization):**

$$Q_1 = m \, C_p \, \Delta T = 5000 \times 3.93 \times (72 - 10)$$
$$Q_1 = 5000 \times 3.93 \times 62 = 1{,}218{,}300 \; \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 2 — Sensible heat to heat milk from 72°C to 95°C (sterilization):**

$$Q_2 = 5000 \times 3.93 \times (95 - 72)$$
$$Q_2 = 5000 \times 3.93 \times 23 = 451{,}950 \; \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 3 — Total sensible heat:**

$$Q_s = Q_1 + Q_2 = 1{,}218{,}300 + 451{,}950 = 1{,}670{,}250 \; \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 4 — Latent heat from evaporation:**

Milk is ~87% water by mass (typical). Water evaporated = $0.87 \times 5000 \times 0.08 = 348$ kg/h.

$$Q_L = 348 \times 2268 = 789{,}264 \; \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 5 — Total heat duty:**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_s + Q_L = 1{,}670{,}250 + 789{,}264$$

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{total}} \approx 2{,}459{,}514 \; \text{kJ/h} \approx 683.2 \; \text{kW}}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Heat Capacity Module:**

- ✅ **Heat Capacity** $C = dQ/dT$ (extensive). **Specific heat** $c = C/m$ (intensive). **Molar heat capacity** $\bar{C} = C/n$ (intensive).
- ✅ **Units to remember:** kJ/(kg·K) for $c$; kJ/(kmol·K) for $\bar{C}$; kJ/K for $C$. Always state units in KTU answers.
- ✅ **Two key $C_p$ paths:** Constant pressure (open flow / food processes — primary) vs constant volume (closed systems).
- ✅ **Ideal gas master relation:** $\bar{C}_p - \bar{C}_v = R = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K) — derivation expected in exams.
- ✅ **Dulong–Petit rule:** $\bar{C}_p \approx 3R \approx 25.93$ kJ/(kmol·K) for solid elements at ordinary temperatures.
- ✅ **Kopp's rule:** Sum of (number of atoms × atomic $C_p$). Common atomic values: C = 11.7, H = 9.6, O = 16.7 kJ/(kmol·K).
- ✅ **Mean heat capacity** $\bar{C}_{p,m}$ = integral of $C_p(T)$ divided by $\Delta T$. Used to convert integration into multiplication.
- ✅ **Sensible heat:** $Q_s = m C_p \Delta T$ or $Q_s = m \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p(T) dT$. Temperature rises, no phase change.
- ✅ **Latent heat:** $Q_L = m \lambda$. No temperature change, phase change occurs.
- ✅ **Watson's equation** (for $\lambda$ vs temperature): $\lambda(T_2) = \lambda(T_1) \left[\frac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right]^{0.38}$ — $T$ in Kelvin only.
- ✅ **Solution heat capacity (mass fraction rule):** $C_{p,\text{sol}} = \sum w_i C_{p,i}$ — exact for ideal/dilute food solutions.
- ✅ **Unit conversion pitfalls:** 1 kcal = 4.186 kJ. 1 kg-mol = 1000 mol. 1 kmol = 1000 mol.
- ✅ **Temperature conversion:** $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. Differences are numerically equal in K and °C.
- ✅ **Exam trap:** When $C_p$ varies with $T$, **integrate**; when given as mean, just multiply. Read the problem carefully.
- ✅ **Practical food engineering insight:** Latent heat often dominates total energy demand even for small mass fractions undergoing phase change.
- ✅ **Sanity check rule of thumb:** Most food solids have $C_p$ between 1.0 and 2.0 kJ/(kg·K); most liquid foods have $C_p$ between 3.0 and 4.5 kJ/(kg·K); water-rich liquids approach 4.186 kJ/(kg·K).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamental Concepts and Definitions: Closed, Open, and Isolated Systems; Intensive and Extensive Properties; Path and State Functions; Reversible and Irreversible Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT302 - FOOD THERMODYNAMICS AND PROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Concepts and Definitions: Closed, Open, and Isolated Systems; Intensive and Extensive Properties; Path and State Functions; Reversible and Irreversible Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

Welcome to the foundational bedrock of Food Thermodynamics and Process Calculations. Before we can analyze a pasteurizer, a refrigeration cycle, or a heat exchanger in a food processing plant, we must first agree on a **common vocabulary**. This module builds that universal thermodynamic dictionary. Every equation you will encounter from Module 3 onwards — energy balances, entropy generation, phase equilibria — depends on a rock-solid understanding of these eight concepts.

---

## 1.1 Systems and Their Surroundings

The **system** is the precisely defined region of the universe that we choose to study. Everything outside this region is the **surroundings**. The boundary separating the two can be real (the wall of a milk storage tank) or imaginary (an arbitrary mathematical surface drawn around a flowing stream of fruit juice). The classification of a system depends on what is allowed to cross this boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus Anchor):** A *system* is any specified quantity of matter or region in space chosen for thermodynamic analysis. The *surroundings* are everything external to the system that can interact with it.

### 1.1.1 Closed System (Control Mass)

A **closed system** is one in which **no mass crosses the system boundary**, but energy (in the form of heat or work) is permitted to cross. The total mass inside the system remains constant.

- **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a sealed pressure cooker on your kitchen stove. The amount of dal and water inside is fixed — you cannot add more dal or pour water in. However, heat enters through the bottom from the flame, and the cooker may bulge slightly as it does work against the atmosphere. The *mass* is constant; *energy* is not.
- **Food Engineering Example:** A retort pouch being sterilized in an autoclave. The sealed pouch has a fixed mass of food, but heat and (after venting) some pressure-volume work are exchanged with the autoclave environment.

### 1.1.2 Open System (Control Volume)

An **open system** is one in which **both mass and energy can cross the system boundary**. This is the most common type encountered in continuous food processing operations.

- **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a section of a fruit juice pasteurizer pipeline between two flanges. Raw juice flows in at one end, pasteurized juice flows out at the other, and heat is transferred through the pipe wall. The mass inside your chosen "system" is *not* constant because fluid is moving through it.
- **Food Engineering Example:** A plate heat exchanger used for milk pasteurization, an evaporator concentrating tomato paste, a spray dryer producing milk powder — all are open systems.

### 1.1.3 Isolated System

An **isolated system** is one in which **neither mass nor energy can cross the system boundary**. It is completely disconnected from the surroundings.

- **Conceptual Analogy:** A perfectly insulated, perfectly sealed Thermos flask (dewar) left on a shelf. No heat enters or leaves; no mass escapes. The total mass and total energy inside remain forever constant. (Note: truly perfect isolation is an *idealization*; no real system achieves it perfectly.)
- **Food Engineering Example:** A calorimeter bomb used to measure the calorific value of food samples approximates an isolated system for a short experimental duration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for Students:** Ask two questions to classify any system:
> 1. **Does mass cross the boundary?** If no → Closed or Isolated. If yes → Open.
> 2. **Does energy cross the boundary?** If no → Isolated. If yes → Closed.

| System Type | Mass Transfer? | Energy Transfer? | Food Engineering Example |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Closed** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Sealed retort pouch in autoclave |
| **Open** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Pasteurizer heat exchanger |
| **Isolated** | ❌ No | ❌ No | (Idealized) Thermos flask |

---

## 1.2 Properties of a System — Intensive vs. Extensive

A **property** (or thermodynamic variable) is any observable, measurable characteristic of a system. Properties are classified by how they respond when you double the size of the system.

### 1.2.1 Extensive Properties

**Extensive properties** depend on the **size (extent) of the system**. If you split a system into two equal halves, each half will have *half* the value of the original extensive property.

- **Conceptual Analogy:** If you have 2 kg of rice and divide it into two 1 kg bags, the *total volume* of rice in each bag is half. Volume is extensive.
- **Common Examples:** Mass ($m$), volume ($V$), total internal energy ($U$), total enthalpy ($H$), total entropy ($S$), heat capacity ($C_p$, $C_v$).
- **Mathematical Test:** A property $X$ is extensive if $X(\lambda \cdot \text{System}) = \lambda \cdot X(\text{System})$ where $\lambda$ is a scaling factor.

### 1.2.2 Intensive Properties

**Intensive properties** are **independent of the size** of the system. They describe the *quality* or *state* of the material at a point.

- **Conceptual Analogy:** Take a beaker of water at 80 °C. Split it into two equal beakers. Each beaker is *still* at 80 °C. Temperature is intensive.
- **Common Examples:** Temperature ($T$), pressure ($P$), density ($\rho$), specific volume ($v$), specific internal energy ($u$), specific enthalpy ($h$), specific entropy ($s$), composition (mass or mole fraction).
- **Mathematical Test:** A property $X$ is intensive if $X(\lambda \cdot \text{System}) = X(\text{System})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Ratio Rule:** The ratio of two extensive properties is always intensive. For example, mass ($m$, extensive) divided by volume ($V$, extensive) gives density ($\rho = m/V$, intensive). This is why the lowercase letters $u$, $h$, $s$, $v$ denote *specific* (per-unit-mass) properties — they are intensive versions of their extensive uppercase counterparts.

---

## 1.3 State and Path Functions

This is one of the **most examinable distinctions** in KTU papers. The difference governs how we write energy balances and which differential is an *exact differential*.

### 1.3.1 State Functions

A **state function** (or point function) depends **only on the current state of the system**, not on the path by which that state was reached. It has a *single, well-defined value* at every equilibrium state.

- **Conceptual Analogy:** Your bank account balance. Whether you earned money through a salary or a lottery, once the money is in the account, the balance just *is* what it is. You do not ask "what path did the money take to get here?" to know the current balance.
- **Examples:** Internal energy ($U$), enthalpy ($H$), entropy ($S$), temperature ($T$), pressure ($P$), volume ($V$), Gibbs free energy ($G$).
- **Mathematical Consequence:** A state function $F$ has an **exact differential**, meaning $\oint dF = 0$ around any closed cycle. The integral depends only on the endpoints:
$$F_2 - F_1 = \int_{1}^{2} dF$$

### 1.3.2 Path Functions

A **path function** depends on the **specific sequence of intermediate states** (i.e., the process path) the system follows between the initial and final states. Different paths give different values.

- **Conceptual Analogy:** The amount of fuel your car burns on a trip from Kottayam to Thiruvananthapuram depends on whether you took the straight NH-66 highway or the winding ghat-road through the Western Ghats. Same start, same end, different path, different fuel. Fuel consumed is a path function.
- **Examples:** Heat ($Q$) and Work ($W$). Both depend on how the process is carried out.
- **Mathematical Consequence:** The differentials $\delta Q$ and $\delta W$ are **inexact differentials** (denoted by the slashed $\delta$, not $d$). They cannot be integrated as $dF$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** If a KTU question asks "Is heat a state function?", the answer is a firm **NO**. Heat and work are *boundary phenomena* — they describe energy *in transit* across the system boundary — whereas state functions are *properties* of the system at equilibrium.

---

## 1.4 Reversible and Irreversible Processes

### 1.4.1 Reversible Process

A **reversible process** is an idealized, quasi-equilibrium process that can be **exactly reversed by an infinitesimal change** in the driving force. The system passes through a continuous sequence of equilibrium states, and both the system and surroundings can be restored to their original states with **no net change in the universe**.

- **Conceptual Analogy:** Slowly compressing a gas in a frictionless piston with a handful of fine sand grains. Removing one grain at a time allows the gas to remain in internal equilibrium. You could reverse the process by adding the grains back, one by one, and the gas would trace its states backward.
- **Real-World Status:** Reversibility is an **idealization** — no real process is perfectly reversible. It is a theoretical benchmark used to define the maximum possible efficiency of engines, refrigerators, and food processing cycles.

### 1.4.2 Irreversible Process

An **irreversible process** is any real process that **cannot be reversed without leaving permanent changes** in the system or surroundings. All spontaneous natural processes are irreversible.

- **Sources of Irreversibility (the "friction family"):**
  1. **Friction** (mechanical, viscous, fluid)
  2. **Unrestrained expansion** of a gas into vacuum
  3. **Heat transfer across a finite temperature difference**
  4. **Mixing of two different substances** (e.g., milk into coffee)
  5. **Chemical reactions** in food (Maillard browning, oxidation)
  6. **Phase changes** under non-equilibrium conditions
- **Conceptual Analogy:** Dropping a raw egg on the floor. The egg breaks. No amount of filming, analyzing, or wishing can un-break it without leaving the floor dirty. That is irreversibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Second Law Connection:** Reversible processes are the *only* processes for which $\Delta S_{universe} = 0$. All irreversible processes produce entropy, i.e., $\Delta S_{universe} > 0$. This is why reversible processes are *fictitious benchmarks* used to bound the performance of real food processing equipment like extruders, dryers, and freezers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

In this section, we unpack the operational logic behind each definition and consolidate every formula you will need for numerical problems and derivations in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

---

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Decision Tree of System Classification

A thermodynamic problem will always begin by asking you to identify the system. The KTU examiner's preferred way to test this is: *"Classify the following as open, closed, or isolated."* Use the decision logic below.

### Step 1: Identify the Boundary
- Is it a real physical surface (e.g., a pipe wall, a container)?
- Is it an imaginary mathematical surface (e.g., a control volume drawn around a turbine)?

### Step 2: Track Mass Flow
- Does any material enter or leave the region?
  - **Yes** → **Open System** (Control Volume). Mass is *not* conserved within; mass flow must be tracked.
  - **No** → proceed to Step 3.

### Step 3: Track Energy Transfer
- Does heat ($Q$) or work ($W$) cross the boundary?
  - **No** → **Isolated System.** Total internal energy is constant forever ($dU = 0$).
  - **Yes** → **Closed System** (Control Mass). Mass is fixed; energy balance applies.

### Why This Matters
- For a **closed system**, the First Law reduces to $dU = \delta Q - \delta W$ (or $\Delta U = Q - W$).
- For an **open system** (steady-state, the most common case in food plants), it becomes $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left(h_2 - h_1 + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right)$ — the famous **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)**.
- Misclassifying the system is the **#1 cause of losing 2–3 marks** on Part A and Part B in KTU papers.

---

## 2.2 Intensive vs. Extensive — The Algebra of Scaling

Let us formalize the scaling behavior mathematically. Suppose we have a system with mass $m$ and we duplicate it exactly so that the new system has mass $2m$.

- **Extensive property** $E$: $E(2m) = 2E(m)$. The value doubles.
- **Intensive property** $I$: $I(2m) = I(m)$. The value is unchanged.

**Molar and Specific Properties (conversion tool):**
$$I_{\text{specific}} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{m} \quad \text{(per unit mass, e.g., kJ/kg)}$$
$$I_{\text{molar}} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{n} \quad \text{(per mole, e.g., kJ/kmol)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Property Summary Table for Food Systems:**
> - $T$ (Temperature) → Intensive
> - $P$ (Pressure) → Intensive
> - $\rho$ (Density) → Intensive
> - $v$ (Specific volume) → Intensive
> - $u, h, s, g$ (specific) → Intensive
> - $U, H, S, G$ (total) → Extensive
> - $C_p, C_v$ (heat capacities — total) → Extensive
> - $c_p, c_v$ (specific heat) → Intensive

---

## 2.3 State vs. Path — The Mathematical Heart of the Distinction

This is the most subtle and most-tested pair. Let us examine the differential forms.

### State Function ($dF$ is an exact differential)
For any state function $F = F(x, y)$:
$$dF = \left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial x}\right)_y dx + \left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y}\right)_x dy = M\, dx + N\, dy$$

The integrability condition (Euler reciprocity) holds:
$$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$$

And the cyclic integral vanishes:
$$\oint dF = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \int_{1}^{2} dF = F_2 - F_1$$

### Path Function ($\delta F$ is an inexact differential)
For a path function like heat or work:
$$\delta Q \neq dQ$$

You **cannot** speak of "the heat content of a system" in the same way you speak of its internal energy. The integral depends on the path:
$$\int_{1}^{2} \delta Q = Q_{\text{path-dependent}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common exam trap is to ask *"What is the change in heat of a system undergoing a cyclic process?"* The correct answer is **"Heat is not a state function, so the question is ill-posed; only $\oint \delta Q$ has meaning for the cycle, and it equals the net work done, $\oint \delta W$."**

### Real-World Utility in Food Engineering
- **Energy audits of a bakery oven:** Use state functions ($U$, $H$) to compute energy stored in bread; use path functions ($Q$, $W$) to size the gas burners.
- **Refrigeration cycle analysis:** The refrigeration *coefficient of performance* is defined as a ratio of path functions (heat absorbed in evaporator to net work input), but the state of the refrigerant at each corner of the cycle is given by state functions ($P$, $T$, $h$, $s$).
- **Drying calculations:** Mass of water removed is *extensive*; moisture content on a dry basis is *intensive*. The *enthalpy change* of the food is a *state function*; the *heat supplied* in the dryer is a *path function*.

---

## 2.4 Reversible vs. Irreversible — Engineering Benchmarks

### Step-by-Step: Is a Process Reversible?
A process is reversible **if and only if** all of the following are true:
1. The process is **quasi-static** (the system passes through a continuum of equilibrium states).
2. There is **no friction** of any kind (viscous, mechanical, electrical).
3. **No heat transfer occurs across a finite temperature difference.**
4. The process can be **traced backwards** by an infinitesimal change in the external condition.

If any one of these is violated, the process is **irreversible**.

### Quantitative Measures
- **Reversible process:** $\Delta S_{sys} = -\Delta S_{surr}$, so $\Delta S_{universe} = 0$.
- **Irreversible process:** $\Delta S_{universe} > 0$ (Clausius Inequality).
- The **lost work** (or exergy destruction) in an irreversible process:
$$W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen} = T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{universe}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Food Engineering Application:** A freeze-drying (lyophilization) process is inherently irreversible because heat must flow from the warm shelf to the cold product across a finite $\Delta T$. The reversible benchmark for freeze-drying is used to size the minimum theoretical energy input; real freeze dryers always require more.

---

## 2.5 KTU 2024 Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is your **master reference** for this module. Memorize the *symbols* and *units* — KTU papers will provide the numerical values, but you must be fluent in notation.

| Concept | Symbol/Formula | Type | Unit (SI) | KTU Exam Tip |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Total internal energy | $U$ | Extensive, State | J (or kJ) | Use $U$ for closed, $u$ for open |
| Specific internal energy | $u = U/m$ | Intensive, State | kJ/kg | Per unit mass |
| Total enthalpy | $H = U + PV$ | Extensive, State | kJ | Always use $H$ in flow processes |
| Specific enthalpy | $h = u + Pv$ | Intensive, State | kJ/kg | $h_2 - h_1$ is central to SFEE |
| Total entropy | $S$ | Extensive, State | J/K | $S$ is a state function |
| Specific entropy | $s = S/m$ | Intensive, State | kJ/(kg·K) | Use for open systems |
| Density | $\rho = m/V$ | Intensive | kg/m³ | Inverse of specific volume $v$ |
| Specific volume | $v = V/m$ | Intensive | m³/kg | $v = 1/\rho$ |
| Heat (path function) | $Q$ or $\delta Q$ | Path, Extensive | kJ | Symbol uses slashed $\delta$ |
| Work (path function) | $W$ or $\delta W$ | Path, Extensive | kJ | Distinguish from $W$ for watts |
| Closed system 1st Law | $\Delta U = Q - W$ | Equation | kJ | Sign convention critical! |
| Open system 1st Law (SFEE) | $\dot{m}(h_1 + V_1^2/2 + gz_1) + \dot{Q} = \dot{m}(h_2 + V_2^2/2 + gz_2) + \dot{W}_s$ | Equation | kW | Used for turbines, compressors, nozzles |
| Cyclic integral of state function | $\oint dF = 0$ | Theorem | — | Apply to $U$, $H$, $S$, $T$, $P$ |
| Clausius inequality (reversible) | $\oint \delta Q_{rev}/T = 0$ | Theorem | — | Definition of entropy |
| Clausius inequality (irreversible) | $\oint \delta Q/T < 0$ | Theorem | — | Real processes only |
| Entropy generation | $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{sys} - \int \delta Q/T_{surr}$ | Equation | kJ/K | Always $S_{gen} \geq 0$ |
| Lost work (exergy destruction) | $W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ | Equation | kJ | Tied to Second Law |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Snapshot

| Concept | Where it appears in a Food Plant |
| :--- | :--- |
| Open System | Evaporators, pasteurizers, extruders, dryers, pumps |
| Closed System | Batch retort sterilization, mixing tanks, dough proofers |
| Isolated System | (Idealized) Insulated holding tubes, calorimeters |
| Intensive Properties | Control loop set-points ($T$, $P$, flow rate per unit) |
| Extensive Properties | Plant-wide mass/energy balances, inventory management |
| State Functions | Reference tables, steam tables, refrigerant property tables |
| Path Functions | Heat exchanger duty, compressor work input |
| Reversible Process | Carnot benchmarks, ideal work of compression |
| Irreversible Process | Every real process — used to compute lost work and 2nd-Law efficiency |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the mathematical backbone of the definitions. KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions often ask you to **prove** or **derive** a specific relationship — here are the proofs you must master.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: The Ratio of Two Extensive Properties is Intensive

**Claim:** If $E_1$ and $E_2$ are both extensive, then $I = E_1 / E_2$ is intensive.

**Proof:**

Let the original system have properties $(E_1, E_2)$. Duplicate the system exactly to obtain a system of twice the mass. Because both properties are extensive:

$$E_1(2m) = 2 E_1(m)$$
$$E_2(2m) = 2 E_2(m)$$

The ratio for the doubled system is:

$$I(2m) = \frac{E_1(2m)}{E_2(2m)} = \frac{2 E_1(m)}{2 E_2(m)} = \frac{E_1(m)}{E_2(m)} = I(m)$$

Since the ratio is unchanged by scaling, the ratio is **intensive**. $\blacksquare$

**Physical Interpretation:** This is why **density** ($\rho = m/V$, mass per volume), **specific volume** ($v = V/m$), **molar concentration** ($C = n/V$), and **specific enthalpy** ($h = H/m$) are all intensive, despite being constructed from extensive quantities.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Exact vs. Inexact Differentials — A Worked Test

**Question:** Show that internal energy $U(T, V)$ is a state function by demonstrating the Euler reciprocity of its differential.

**Step 1 — Write the total differential.**
For an ideal gas, $U = U(T)$ only, so $dU = (\partial U/\partial T)_V dT$. For a general fluid, $U = U(T, V)$:
$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

Define $M(T,V) = (\partial U/\partial T)_V$ and $N(T,V) = (\partial U/\partial V)_T$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Euler reciprocity (Maxwell) test for an exact differential.**
A differential $dF = M\, dx + N\, dy$ is exact if and only if:
$$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$$

So we must show:
$$\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial V}\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V\right]_T = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T\right]_V$$

**Step 3 — Use the symmetry of mixed partial derivatives.**
The mixed partials of any sufficiently smooth function are equal regardless of order of differentiation (Clairaut–Schwarz theorem):
$$\frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial V \, \partial T} = \frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial T \, \partial V}$$

**Step 4 — Conclude.**
The reciprocity condition is automatically satisfied, so $dU$ is an **exact differential**. Therefore $U$ is a state function. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Contrast with $\delta Q$:** The differential $\delta Q$ from the First Law $\delta Q = dU + \delta W$ has $M$ and $N$ that do *not* satisfy reciprocity. That is why we use the slashed $\delta$ — to remind us that $\delta Q$ is not the differential of any state function.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cyclic Integral of a State Function is Zero

**Claim:** For any state function $F$ and any closed cycle (process that returns the system to its initial state), $\oint dF = 0$.

**Proof:**

Let the cycle begin at state 1, pass through intermediate states, and return to state 1. The integral over the cycle is:
$$\oint dF = \int_{1}^{\text{via path A}} dF + \int_{\text{via path B}}^{1} dF$$

Since $F$ is a state function, the value depends only on the endpoints:
$$\int_{1}^{2} dF = F_2 - F_1 \quad \text{(along any path)}$$

Therefore:
$$\oint dF = (F_2 - F_1) + (F_1 - F_2) = 0 \quad \blacksquare$$

**Implication for Thermodynamic Cycles:** A refrigeration cycle, a vapor-compression heat pump, or a steam power cycle (Rankine) all return the working fluid to its initial state. Therefore, $\oint dU = 0$, $\oint dH = 0$, $\oint dS_{sys} = 0$. However, $\oint \delta Q$ and $\oint \delta W$ are **not** zero in general — they represent the net heat and net work exchanged in the cycle.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: The Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) — A First-Principles Approach

This is the workhorse equation for **every open system problem** in food engineering (pumps, compressors, heat exchangers, throttling valves, nozzles, evaporators). Let us derive it carefully.

**Step 1 — Start from the First Law for a Control Volume.**
For any system (open or closed) over a time interval $dt$:
$$dE_{cv} = \delta Q_{cv} - \delta W_{cv} + \sum \dot{m}_i h_i \, dt - \sum \dot{m}_e h_e \, dt + \sum \dot{m}_i \frac{V_i^2}{2} dt - \sum \dot{m}_e \frac{V_e^2}{2} dt + \sum \dot{m}_i g z_i dt - \sum \dot{m}_e g z_e dt$$

Here, $E_{cv}$ is the total energy stored in the control volume at any instant.

**Step 2 — Impose the Steady-State Condition.**
At steady state, the time-rate-of-change of energy inside the control volume is zero:
$$\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Restrict to a single inlet and a single outlet.**
For most food process equipment (a heat exchanger, a pump), there is one inlet (1) and one outlet (2). Let $\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}$ (mass conservation at steady state).

**Step 4 — Define shaft work as the only non-flow work.**
The only useful work crossing the boundary is the shaft work $\dot{W}_s$ (e.g., the impeller of a pump turning a shaft). The flow work $P\dot{V}$ is already accounted for inside the enthalpy $h = u + Pv$. Let $\dot{Q}$ be the net heat added to the control volume.

**Step 5 — Assemble the equation.**
After cancellation of flow work already contained in $h$, the First Law simplifies to:
$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_s = \dot{m}\left[\left(h_2 - h_1\right) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$$

This is the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)**. $\blacksquare$

**Special cases frequently tested in KTU:**
1. **Nozzle/Diffuser** ($\dot{W}_s = 0$, $\dot{Q} \approx 0$, $\Delta z = 0$): $\dot{m}(h_1 + V_1^2/2) = \dot{m}(h_2 + V_2^2/2)$.
2. **Throttling Valve** ($\dot{W}_s = 0$, $\dot{Q} \approx 0$, $\Delta V \approx 0$, $\Delta z \approx 0$): $h_1 = h_2$ (isenthalpic process).
3. **Heat Exchanger** ($\dot{W}_s = 0$, $\Delta V \approx 0$, $\Delta z \approx 0$): $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$.
4. **Pump/Compressor** ($\dot{Q} \approx 0$, $\Delta V \approx 0$, $\Delta z \approx 0$): $\dot{W}_s = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_2)$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Decision Aid for Problem Solving

| Given Equipment / Process | System Type | Applicable Equation | Typical Unknown |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Batch retort in autoclave | Closed | $\Delta U = Q - W$ | Sterilization value $F_0$ |
| Milk pasteurizer (HTST) | Open (steady) | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | Steam flow rate |
| Centrifugal pump on juice line | Open (steady) | $\dot{W}_s = \dot{m} v \Delta P$ | Power input (kW) |
| Spray dryer nozzle | Open (steady) | $h_1 + V_1^2/2 = h_2 + V_2^2/2$ | Exit velocity $V_2$ |
| Throttling valve (e.g., flash cooler) | Open (steady) | $h_1 = h_2$ | Outlet quality $x_2$ |
| Pressurized dough proofer | Closed | $\Delta U = Q - W$ | Heat required to proof |
| Insulated holding tube (ideal) | Isolated (approx.) | $\Delta U = 0$ | Final $T$ after residence time |
| Refrigeration cycle (one full cycle) | Cyclic | $\oint dU = 0 \Rightarrow \oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$ | COP |
| Reversible isothermal expansion of ideal gas | Closed | $W = nRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ | Work or heat |
| Irreversible free expansion of gas (Joule) | Closed | $W = 0$, $Q = 0$, $\Delta U = 0$ | $\Delta T = 0$ for ideal gas |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The diagrams below are rendered in Mermaid syntax. They map the conceptual structure of this module and will help you visualize the classification hierarchy.

---

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart — System Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify the system boundary] --> B{Does mass cross the boundary?}
    B -- "Yes" --> C[OPEN SYSTEM - Control Volume]
    C --> C1[Mass flow in and out is tracked]
    C --> C2[Examples: pasteurizer, evaporator, dryer]
    B -- "No" --> D{Does energy cross the boundary?}
    D -- "Yes" --> E[CLOSED SYSTEM - Control Mass]
    E --> E1[Mass is constant]
    E --> E2[Energy may enter as Q or leave as W]
    E --> E3[Examples: sealed retort, pressure cooker]
    D -- "No" --> F[ISOLATED SYSTEM]
    F --> F1[No mass transfer]
    F --> F2[No heat transfer]
    F --> F3[No work transfer]
    F --> F4[Total energy is conserved forever]
    F --> F5[Examples: ideal Thermos, calorimeter]
    C --> G[First Law: SFEE applies]
    E --> H[First Law: dU = dQ - dW]
    F --> I[First Law: dU = 0]
```

---

## 4.2 Property Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermodynamic Property] --> B{Does the value depend on the SIZE of the system?}
    B -- "Yes" --> C[EXTENSIVE Property]
    C --> C1[Scales linearly with mass]
    C --> C2[Examples: m, V, U, H, S, G, total Cp]
    C --> C3[Notation: UPPERCASE letters]
    B -- "No" --> D[INTENSIVE Property]
    D --> D1[Independent of system size]
    D --> D2[Examples: T, P, rho, v, u, h, s, g]
    D --> D3[Notation: lowercase letters for specific quantities]
    C --> E[Ratio of two extensive properties]
    E --> F[Intensive Property]
    F --> F1[Example: rho = m / V]
    F --> F2[Example: u = U / m]
    F --> F3[Example: h = H / m]
```

---

## 4.3 State Function vs. Path Function — A Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process 1 to 2 via Path A] --> P1[Intermediate states on Path A]
    P1 --> B[State 2]
    C[Process 1 to 2 via Path B] --> P2[Intermediate states on Path B]
    P2 --> B
    SF[State Function F: dF] --> SF1[Value depends only on state 1 and state 2]
    SF1 --> SF2[Integral: F2 minus F1]
    SF2 --> SF3[Independent of path]
    SF3 --> SF4[Cyclic integral is zero]
    PF[Path Function: delta Q or delta W] --> PF1[Value depends on specific path taken]
    PF1 --> PF2[Cannot be expressed as F2 minus F1]
    PF2 --> PF3[Different paths give different values]
    PF3 --> PF4[Cyclic integral is NOT zero in general]
    B --> SF
    B --> PF
```

---

## 4.4 Reversible vs. Irreversible Process — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Evaluate a real process] --> B{Is the process quasi-static? Passes only through equilibrium states?}
    B -- "No" --> IR1[IRREVERSIBLE]
    B -- "Yes" --> C{Is there any friction present?}
    C -- "Yes" --> IR2[IRREVERSIBLE]
    C -- "No" --> D{Does heat transfer occur across a finite delta T?}
    D -- "Yes" --> IR3[IRREVERSIBLE]
    D -- "No" --> E{Can the process be exactly reversed by an infinitesimal change?}
    E -- "No" --> IR4[IRREVERSIBLE]
    E -- "Yes" --> R[REVERSIBLE - Idealized benchmark]
    IR1 --> IR5[delta Suniverse greater than zero]
    IR2 --> IR5
    IR3 --> IR5
    IR4 --> IR5
    R --> R1[delta Suniverse equals zero]
    R1 --> R2[Maximum theoretical efficiency]
    IR5 --> R3[Lost work: Wlost = T0 times Sgen]
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Open System (Steady-State Control Volume) as Used in a Food Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SURROUNDINGS[Surroundings: ambient air, utility fluids] -- "Q dot heat" --> CV
    SURROUNDINGS -- "W dot shaft work" --> CV
    IN[Inlet stream 1: m1 dot, h1, V1, z1] -- "m1 dot" --> CV
    CV[Control Volume: e.g. pasteurizer heat exchanger] -- "m2 dot" --> OUT
    OUT[Outlet stream 2: m2 dot, h2, V2, z2]
    CV -- "delta E by delta t at steady state equals zero" --> CV
    IN -. "Enthalpy h1, KE V1squared by 2, PE g z1" .-> CV
    CV -. "Enthalpy h2, KE V2squared by 2, PE g z2" .-> OUT
    SURROUNDINGS -. "Surroundings act as heat reservoir at Tsurr" .-> CV
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Mapping Note:** The diagram above is a **functional representation** of an open food-processing system (control volume). The dashed arrows indicate energy-carrying streams; the solid arrows indicate mass flow. The relationship between the streams is governed by the SFEE.
