# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

The following questions are modeled on the actual KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern, with the **module internal choice** preserved (Question A vs. Question B).

---

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define the following terms with one food-engineering example for each:
(a) Open system, (b) Closed system, (c) Isolated system.

**Model Answer:**

| System | Definition | Food Engineering Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Open system** | A region in space across whose boundary **both mass and energy** can transfer. | A **plate heat exchanger** for HTST pasteurization of milk, where raw milk enters, hot water provides energy, and pasteurized milk exits. |
| **Closed system** | A region across whose boundary **no mass** but **energy (heat/work) can** transfer. The mass of the system is fixed. | A **sealed retort pouch** of ready-to-eat food being thermally processed in an autoclave. |
| **Isolated system** | A region across whose boundary **neither mass nor energy** can transfer. The total mass and total energy are constant. | A **perfectly insulated, sealed Thermos flask** containing hot soup (idealized case, used as a theoretical benchmark). |

**[3 Marks: 1 mark per system with example]**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Distinguish clearly between **state functions** and **path functions**. Give two examples of each. Why is it incorrect to speak of "the heat content of a system"?

**Model Answer:**

- **State Function:** A thermodynamic property whose value depends **only on the current state** of the system, not on the path by which that state was reached. *Examples:* Temperature ($T$), Pressure ($P$), Internal Energy ($U$), Enthalpy ($H$), Entropy ($S$), Gibbs Energy ($G$).
- **Path Function:** A quantity whose value depends on the **specific sequence of intermediate states** (the path) traversed between the initial and final states. *Examples:* Heat ($Q$) and Work ($W$).

**Why "heat content" is incorrect:** Heat is *energy in transit* across the system boundary. It is not a property stored within the system at equilibrium. A system at a given state does not *contain* a certain amount of heat — it contains internal energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy. Heat only has meaning **during** a process, and the total heat exchanged depends on the path. Hence, we use the differential $\delta Q$ (inexact), not $dQ$ (exact).

**[3 Marks: 1.5 marks for distinction + examples, 1.5 marks for the "heat content" explanation]**

---

## Part B: Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Format:** In the End Semester Examination, a single 14-mark question is asked from Module 3. Students are given an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Below, both alternatives are presented with full worked solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** 
**(a)** Classify the following food-processing operations as **open, closed, or isolated** systems, and justify each classification in one sentence: (7 marks)
   (i) Milk being pasteurized in a continuous-flow plate heat exchanger.
   (ii) A can of tuna being sterilized in a batch retort.
   (iii) A perfectly insulated calorimeter bomb containing a food sample being combusted.
   (iv) A pressure cooker filled with rice and water, heated on a gas stove.
   (v) A spray dryer producing milk powder.
   (vi) An insulated holding tube in an HTST pasteurizer where no heat is added.
   (vii) A piston-cylinder device compressing CO₂ for supercritical extraction of caffeine from coffee beans.

**(b)** With a neat sketch and the necessary assumptions, derive the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** for an open system with a single inlet and a single outlet. Show how it reduces to the form used for a **throttling valve** and a **pump**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) [7 marks]

| # | Operation | System | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | Continuous-flow plate heat exchanger (milk pasteurization) | **Open** | Raw milk enters, pasteurized milk leaves — mass crosses the boundary; heat crosses through the plates. |
| (ii) | Can of tuna in batch retort | **Closed** | The sealed can does not permit mass transfer; only heat crosses the can wall from the steam. |
| (iii) | Insulated calorimeter bomb (combustion) | **Isolated** (approximation) | No heat is lost to surroundings (insulated), and the bomb is sealed so no mass enters or leaves. |
| (iv) | Pressure cooker on stove | **Closed** | The sealed lid prevents mass transfer; heat enters from the stove and work is done lifting the pressure relief valve. |
| (v) | Spray dryer producing milk powder | **Open** | Concentrated milk enters as liquid droplets, hot dry air provides heat, and the dried powder plus moist air leave. |
| (vi) | Insulated holding tube in HTST pasteurizer | **Open** (technically), often idealized as **Isolated** for the brief residence time | Mass is clearly entering and leaving the tube, so it is open; however, since no heat is exchanged and no work is done across the tube wall, and the residence time is short, the process is often treated as adiabatic open flow (or, in some textbook simplifications, as a quasi-isolated plug of fluid). |
| (vii) | Piston-cylinder compressing CO₂ for supercritical extraction | **Closed** | The gas is sealed between the piston and the cylinder head; mass cannot escape, but work crosses the boundary as the piston compresses. |

**[Award 1 mark per correct classification with justification; 7 marks total.]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) [7 marks]

**Sketch:** A horizontal control volume with one inlet stream (1) and one outlet stream (2), a heat transfer $\dot{Q}$ from surroundings, and a shaft work $\dot{W}_s$ (e.g., from a pump impeller).

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state: $\dfrac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$.
2. Single inlet and single outlet.
3. Mass conservation at steady state: $\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}$.
4. The only non-flow work is shaft work $\dot{W}_s$.
5. Changes in potential energy are negligible unless stated.

**Derivation (see Section 3.4 for the full expansion):** Applying the First Law to the open system at steady state and collecting all energy flows:

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_s = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$$

**Reduction to a Throttling Valve** (e.g., expansion valve in a refrigeration cycle):
- $\dot{W}_s = 0$ (no shaft work).
- $\dot{Q} = 0$ (adiabatic throttling).
- $\Delta KE \approx 0$ and $\Delta PE \approx 0$ (negligible velocity and elevation change across the valve).

Substituting:
$$0 = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1) \quad \Rightarrow \quad h_1 = h_2$$

**Conclusion:** A throttling process is **isenthalpic** (constant enthalpy).

**Reduction to a Pump** (e.g., a centrifugal pump in a milk transfer line):
- $\dot{Q} \approx 0$ (pumping time is short, adiabatic approximation).
- $\Delta KE \approx 0$ and $\Delta PE \approx 0$ (cross-section and height change small).

Substituting:
$$- \dot{W}_s = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{W}_s = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_2)$$

The negative sign convention tells us that $\dot{W}_s$ is *input* to the fluid (work done *on* the system by the pump).

**[7 marks: 2 marks for assumptions + sketch, 3 marks for the SFEE derivation, 2 marks for the two special-case reductions.]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Define and distinguish between **intensive** and **extensive** properties. Give three examples of each in the context of food processing, and prove mathematically that the ratio of two extensive properties is always intensive. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is a **reversible process**? State the **four conditions** that must be satisfied for a process to be reversible. In a food-processing context, explain why no real process is truly reversible and illustrate with the example of a **batch milk pasteurizer in a stirred heating vessel**. Compute the entropy generated when 1.5 kg of milk is heated reversibly from 20 °C to 72 °C, given $c_p = 3.93$ kJ/(kg·K) and a surrounding temperature of 298 K. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) [7 marks]

**Definitions:**
- **Intensive Property:** A property whose value is **independent of the size (mass or extent) of the system**. It describes the *state* or *quality* of the material at a point.
- **Extensive Property:** A property whose value **scales linearly with the size** of the system. It depends on the total amount of material present.

**Three examples each in food processing:**

| Intensive Properties | Extensive Properties |
| :--- | :--- |
| Temperature $T$ (e.g., 72 °C pasteurization set-point) | Total mass $m$ (e.g., 1000 kg milk in a silo) |
| Pressure $P$ (e.g., 2 bar in a retort) | Total volume $V$ (e.g., volume of juice in a tank) |
| Density $\rho$ (e.g., 1030 kg/m³ for whole milk) | Total enthalpy $H$ (e.g., total energy content of a batch) |

**Mathematical Proof (Ratio of two extensive properties is intensive):**

Let $E_1$ and $E_2$ be two extensive properties. Consider duplicating the system. By the definition of extensivity:
$$E_1(\lambda m) = \lambda E_1(m) \quad \text{and} \quad E_2(\lambda m) = \lambda E_2(m)$$

Form the ratio $I = E_1/E_2$. For the scaled system:
$$I(\lambda m) = \frac{E_1(\lambda m)}{E_2(\lambda m)} = \frac{\lambda E_1(m)}{\lambda E_2(m)} = \frac{E_1(m)}{E_2(m)} = I(m)$$

Since the ratio is invariant under scaling, $I$ is **intensive**. $\blacksquare$

**Examples illustrating the ratio rule:**
- Density: $\rho = m/V$ (extensive/extensive → intensive)
- Specific enthalpy: $h = H/m$ (extensive/extensive → intensive)
- Specific heat capacity: $c_p = C_p/m$ (extensive/extensive → intensive)

**[7 marks: 2 marks for definitions, 2 marks for examples, 3 marks for the proof.]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) [7 marks]

**Definition of a Reversible Process:**
A reversible process is a **quasi-equilibrium, idealized process** that can be exactly reversed by an infinitesimal change in the external driving force, restoring both the system and surroundings to their initial states with **no net change in the universe**.

**Four conditions for reversibility:**
1. The process must be **quasi-static** — the system passes through a continuous sequence of internal equilibrium states.
2. There must be **no friction** (mechanical, viscous, or otherwise).
3. **No heat transfer** must occur across a finite temperature difference (the system and surroundings must be at the same $T$ at the boundary).
4. The process must be **tracable in reverse** by an infinitesimal change in the external condition.

**Why no real food process is truly reversible:** A batch milk pasteurizer in a stirred heating vessel violates all four conditions:
- **Non-quasi-static:** When the heating steam valve is opened, the milk temperature near the wall jumps rapidly above the bulk temperature — the system is not in internal equilibrium.
- **Friction:** The agitator impeller imparts viscous dissipation into the milk.
- **Finite $\Delta T$:** Heat flows from steam (typically at 85–90 °C) through the vessel wall into the milk (initially 20 °C) — a large, finite temperature difference.
- **Spontaneous un-mixing impossible:** Once the milk is heated, the heat cannot spontaneously flow back into the cooling water.

**Numerical Computation — Entropy generated in reversible heating of milk:**

For a **reversible** heating process (hypothetical, ideal), the entropy change of the milk is:
$$\Delta S_{milk} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$$

Converting temperatures to Kelvin:
$$T_1 = 20 + 273 = 293 \text{ K}, \quad T_2 = 72 + 273 = 345 \text{ K}$$

Plugging in:
$$\Delta S_{milk} = 1.5 \text{ kg} \times 3.93 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} \times \ln\left(\frac{345}{293}\right)$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{345}{293}\right) = \ln(1.1775) = 0.1634$$

$$\Delta S_{milk} = 1.5 \times 3.93 \times 0.1634 = 0.9634 \text{ kJ/K}$$

For a **reversible** process, $\Delta S_{universe} = 0$, so:
$$S_{gen} = \Delta S_{milk} - \frac{Q_{rev}}{T_{surr}} = 0 \text{ kJ/K}$$

(Although the milk's entropy *increases* by 0.9634 kJ/K, the surroundings lose an exactly equal amount of entropy through the heat rejected at $T_{surr}$, so the net entropy generation is zero.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU Students:** If the question explicitly said "irreversible heating with a constant wall temperature of 90 °C," then you would compute the actual heat $Q = m c_p \Delta T = 1.5 \times 3.93 \times 52 = 306.5$ kJ, the actual entropy change of the milk as $0.9634$ kJ/K, and the entropy transferred to the milk as $Q/T_{surr}$ only when the boundary is at 90 °C. Always read the problem statement carefully!

**[7 marks: 2 marks for definition + conditions, 2 marks for the food-context explanation, 3 marks for the numerical computation.]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** In the SFEE, $\dot{Q}_{in}$ is positive when heat enters the control volume, and $\dot{W}_{s,out}$ is positive when work is done by the system. Mixing the IChemE and physics conventions costs 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the "specific" prefix:** Writing "$H_2 - H_1$" in a per-unit-mass balance is a fatal mistake. Always use $h_2 - h_1$ for SFEE.
> 3. **Saying "heat is a state function":** This is the most common wrong statement. Heat is a **path function**. State that clearly: $\delta Q$ vs. $dQ$, and $\oint \delta Q \neq 0$ in general.
> 4. **Treating a "perfectly insulated holding tube" as a closed system:** It is still an **open** system because milk is moving through it. Insulation only means $\dot{Q} = 0$, not that the system is closed.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** KTU examiners explicitly deduct 0.5 marks for missing units. Always write kJ, kW, kg/s, kJ/(kg·K), etc.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in entropy calculations. $\ln$ of a ratio of Celsius values is **dimensionally and numerically wrong**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3)**

- [x] A **system** is what we choose to study; the **surroundings** are everything else.
- [x] **Closed system** = no mass transfer, energy can transfer (e.g., retort pouch).
- [x] **Open system** = both mass and energy can transfer (e.g., pasteurizer).
- [x] **Isolated system** = neither mass nor energy transfers (ideal Thermos).
- [x] **Extensive properties** scale with system size: $m$, $V$, $U$, $H$, $S$, $G$.
- [x] **Intensive properties** are size-independent: $T$, $P$, $\rho$, $v$, $u$, $h$, $s$.
- [x] **Ratio of two extensive properties** is always intensive.
- [x] **State functions**: depend only on state, $\oint dF = 0$. Examples: $U$, $H$, $S$, $T$, $P$, $G$.
- [x] **Path functions**: depend on the process route. Examples: $Q$ and $W$ (denoted $\delta Q$, $\delta W$).
- [x] **Reversible process**: quasi-static, frictionless, no $\Delta T$ for heat transfer, reversible by infinitesimal change.
- [x] **Irreversible process**: every real process; $\Delta S_{universe} > 0$.
- [x] **First Law, Closed System**: $\Delta U = Q - W$.
- [x] **First Law, Open System (SFEE)**: $\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_s = \dot{m}[(h_2 - h_1) + \Delta KE + \Delta PE]$.
- [x] **Throttling valve**: $h_1 = h_2$ (isenthalpic).
- [x] **Adiabatic nozzle**: $h_1 + V_1^2/2 = h_2 + V_2^2/2$.
- [x] **Cyclic process**: $\oint dU = \oint dH = \oint dS = 0$, but $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W \neq 0$ in general.
- [x] Always convert **°C → K** before any entropy or $\ln(T_2/T_1)$ calculation.
- [x] Always include **units** in final numerical answers.
- [x] A process with $\Delta T > 0$ at the heat-transfer boundary is **always irreversible**.
- [x] The **Carnot efficiency** is the upper bound for any heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs — no food-processing heat engine can exceed it.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Fundamental Concepts and Definitions**
> *Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme, B.Tech Food Technology / Chemical Engineering syllabus.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT302 - FOOD THERMODYNAMICS AND PROCESS CALCULATIONS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition The **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics** is the foundational postulate that establishes the concept of **temperature** as a measurable, transitive physical property. In its precise thermodynamic form, the law states: > *"If two thermodynamic systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other."* Mathematically, if system $A$ is in thermal equilibrium with system $C$, and system $B$ is also in thermal equilibrium with system $C$, then systems $A$ and $B$ must be in thermal equilibrium with each other. $$A \leftrightarrow C \quad \text{and} \quad B \leftrightarrow C \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \leftrightarrow B$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why it is called the "Zeroth" Law:** > It was formulated *after* the First and Second Laws were already established (by Ralph Fowler in 1931). However, logically it must precede them — hence the name **Zeroth**, indicating foundational priority. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 3):** > The Zeroth Law forms the conceptual backbone for temperature measurement, calibration of thermometers, and the very definition of the thermodynamic temperature scale used in all food process calculations. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine three friends standing in a room: - **Friend A** feels comfortable (neither hot nor cold). - **Friend C** also feels comfortable (neither hot nor cold). - Therefore, **Friend B**, who is identical to C, will *also* feel comfortable with Friend A. This common sensation of "comfort" is what we call **temperature** — a property shared between bodies that are not exchanging net heat. The "comfort" (temperature) is a *transitive* property: if A matches C, and B matches C, then A must match B. **Real-World Food Engineering Analogy:** When measuring the temperature of milk in a pasteurization vat using a mercury-in-glass thermometer: - The mercury bulb (system $A$) comes to thermal equilibrium with the milk (system $B$) through the glass wall. - The mercury (system $A$) is also in thermal equilibrium with a calibrated reference (system $C$) at a known temperature. - By the Zeroth Law, we can confidently state the milk (system $B$) is at the same temperature as the reference (system $C$). ## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold for Emphasis) | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Thermal Equilibrium** | State where two systems in contact show no net heat exchange; they share the same **temperature**. | | **Diathermic Wall** | A wall that permits heat transfer (thermally conductive). | | **Adiabatic Wall** | A wall that does NOT permit heat transfer (thermally insulating). | | **Transitivity** | A logical property: if $A = C$ and $B = C$, then $A = B$. | | **Thermodynamic Temperature** | A state function whose equality defines thermal equilibrium. | ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Reference Points > [!NOTE] > - **Triple Point of Water:** $T = \mathbf{273.16 \text{ K}}$ (exactly, by SI definition) > - **Absolute Zero:** $T = \mathbf{0 \text{ K}} = -273.15 \text{ °C}$ > - **Standard Reference Pressure:** $P = \mathbf{101.325 \text{ kPa}}$ > - **Ice Point:** $T = \mathbf{273.15 \text{ K}}$ > - **Steam Point:** $T = \mathbf{373.15 \text{ K}}$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown ### Step 1: Defining Thermal Contact Two systems are said to be in **thermal contact** if they can exchange heat energy between each other, typically through a **diathermic wall** (a thermally conducting boundary). ### Step 2: Achieving Thermal Equilibrium When two systems at different temperatures are placed in thermal contact: - Heat flows from the hotter to the cooler system. - After a sufficient time, the temperatures become equal. - Net heat flow ceases — the systems have reached **thermal equilibrium**. - At this state, they share the same value of the property called **temperature**. ### Step 3: The Principle of Transitivity (Why this Law is Powerful) The Zeroth Law introduces the **transitive nature of temperature**. This single logical property allows us to: 1. **Build thermometers** — a small, easily readable system (e.g., mercury column) can be calibrated against a reference and then used to measure any other body. 2. **Define temperature scales** — by fixing reproducible reference points (ice point, steam point, triple point). 3. **Compare distant bodies** — without direct contact between them. ### Step 4: Establishing Temperature as a State Function Because thermal equilibrium is transitive, there exists a scalar quantity — **temperature** — that uniquely characterizes the thermal state of a system, just as pressure and volume characterize the mechanical state. Temperature is thus a **state function**. ### Step 5: Construction of Thermodynamic Scales Using the Zeroth Law, we can construct an **empirical temperature scale** by assigning numerical values to reproducible states: - Choose a reference state (e.g., ice point = **273.15 K**). - Use a thermometric property $X$ (e.g., length of mercury, resistance of platinum). - Define: $T(X) = a \cdot X + b$, where $a$ and $b$ are fixed by calibration points. ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Zeroth Law The Zeroth Law is essentially a **logical axiom**. Without it: - We could not guarantee that a thermometer reading is meaningful for any body other than the thermometer itself. - All temperature-based process calculations (heat balances, refrigeration cycles, pasteurization design) would be impossible. In food engineering, **pasteurization efficiency**, **freezing point depression**, and **water activity** calculations all fundamentally depend on a well-defined, transitive temperature. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description / Use | |---|-------------------|-------------------| | 1 | $T_A = T_B$ | Systems $A$ and $B$ in thermal equilibrium | | 2 | $A \leftrightarrow C \land B \leftrightarrow C \Rightarrow A \leftrightarrow B$ | Transitivity axiom (Zeroth Law) | | 3 | $T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$ | Celsius to Kelvin conversion | | 4 | $T(^{\circ}\text{F}) = \dfrac{9}{5} \, T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 32$ | Celsius to Fahrenheit conversion | | 5 | $T(\text{K}) = \dfrac{5}{9} \, [T(^{\circ}\text{F}) + 459.67]$ | Fahrenheit to Kelvin | | 6 | $T(^{\circ}\text{R}) = T(^{\circ}\text{F}) + 459.67$ | Fahrenheit to Rankine | | 7 | $T(\text{K}) = \dfrac{5}{9} \, T(^{\circ}\text{R})$ | Rankine to Kelvin | | 8 | $\dfrac{T(X) - T_{\text{ref}}}{100} = \dfrac{X - X_{\text{ref}}}{X_{\text{steam}} - X_{\text{ice}}}$ | Empirical linear temperature scale | | 9 | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \dfrac{X_1}{X_2}$ (for ideal-gas scale) | Ideal-gas absolute temperature | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always convert all temperatures to **Kelvin (K)** before performing any thermodynamic calculation. Markers strictly penalize mixed-unit usage in heat-balance problems. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering 1. **Pasteurization of Milk:** Standard High-Temperature Short-Time (HTST) pasteurization at **72 °C (345.15 K)** for 15 seconds — relies on Zeroth Law for accurate temperature verification. 2. **Cold Chain Logistics:** Freezer temperature monitoring at **-18 °C (255.15 K)** for frozen food storage. 3. **Spray Drying Control:** Inlet air at **180–220 °C**; outlet at **80–100 °C** — calibrated against standard references. 4. **Refrigeration Cycle Analysis:** Evaporator and condenser temperatures form the basis for COP calculations. 5. **Calorimetry in Bomb Calorimeter:** Food energy content (kcal/g) measured using temperature rise of water — calibrated thermometer at the core. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I: Logical Foundation of the Zeroth Law (Transitivity Proof) **Given:** - System $A$ is in thermal equilibrium with system $C$ (through diathermic wall $\alpha$). - System $B$ is in thermal equilibrium with system $C$ (through diathermic wall $\beta$). - All three systems are otherwise isolated from the surroundings by adiabatic walls. **To Prove:** Systems $A$ and $B$ are in thermal equilibrium with each other. ### Step-by-Step Logical Argument **Step 1:** Define thermal equilibrium as a state where no net heat flow occurs between bodies in thermal contact. **Step 2:** By definition of thermal equilibrium between $A$ and $C$: $$Q_{A \to C} = 0$$ **Step 3:** By definition of thermal equilibrium between $B$ and $C$: $$Q_{B \to C} = 0$$ **Step 4:** Now place $A$ and $B$ in thermal contact through a diathermic wall (call it $\gamma$). We must show no net heat flow occurs between them. **Step 5:** Assume, for contradiction, that net heat flows from $A$ to $B$: $$Q_{A \to B} > 0$$ **Step 6:** Since $A$, $B$, $C$ are connected through diathermic paths, this heat flow must ultimately disturb the equilibrium of $C$. Heat from $A$ would reach $C$ through $B$ (or directly), implying: $$Q_{A \to C} > 0$$ **Step 7:** But this **contradicts** Step 2 where we established $Q_{A \to C} = 0$. **Step 8:** Therefore, our assumption in Step 5 is invalid. The only consistent conclusion is: $$Q_{A \to B} = 0$$ **Conclusion:** Systems $A$ and $B$ are in thermal equilibrium. This proves transitivity. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Derivation II: Existence of a Single Property Called Temperature **Objective:** Show that there exists a scalar quantity $T$ (temperature) whose equality is necessary and sufficient for thermal equilibrium. ### Mathematical Framework Consider any two systems $A$ and $B$ in thermal contact. Let $\zeta_A$ represent a thermodynamic property of $A$, and $\zeta_B$ represent a property of $B$. The condition for thermal equilibrium between them can be expressed as: $$f_A(\zeta_A) = f_B(\zeta_B)$$ where $f$ is some function that converts local properties into a comparable form. **Step 1:** Introduce a third system $C$ as a "reference body". For $C$ in equilibrium with $A$: $$f_A(\zeta_A) = f_C(\zeta_C)$$ **Step 2:** For $C$ in equilibrium with $B$: $$f_B(\zeta_B) = f_C(\zeta_C)$$ **Step 3:** Equating the right-hand sides: $$f_A(\zeta_A) = f_B(\zeta_B)$$ **Step 4:** Define a universal function $T(\cdot)$ such that: $$T_A \equiv f_A(\zeta_A), \quad T_B \equiv f_B(\zeta_B)$$ **Step 5:** The equilibrium condition becomes: $$T_A = T_B$$ **Step 6:** This scalar $T$ is unique, universal, and independent of the material of the system — it is the **temperature**. ## 3.3 Derivation III: Establishing an Empirical Linear Temperature Scale Let $X$ be a thermometric property (e.g., length of mercury column, electrical resistance). The empirical temperature $T(X)$ is defined as a linear function: $$T(X) = a \cdot X + b$$ **Step 1:** Choose two reproducible calibration points: - **Ice Point:** Pure ice–water mixture at $1 \text{ atm}$, assigned $T_1 = 273.15 \text{ K}$. - **Steam Point:** Pure water boiling at $1 \text{ atm}$, assigned $T_2 = 373.15 \text{ K}$. **Step 2:** At the ice point, the thermometer reads $X_1$: $$273.15 = a X_1 + b$$ **Step 3:** At the steam point, the thermometer reads $X_2$: $$373.15 = a X_2 + b$$ **Step 4:** Subtract Step 2 from Step 3: $$100 = a (X_2 - X_1)$$ $$a = \dfrac{100}{X_2 - X_1}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute back into Step 2: $$b = 273.15 - a X_1 = 273.15 - \dfrac{100 \, X_1}{X_2 - X_1}$$ **Step 6:** Final expression for any reading $X$: $$T(X) = 273.15 + 100 \cdot \dfrac{X - X_1}{X_2 - X_1}$$ This linear interpolation defines the **empirical Celsius scale** for the chosen thermometer. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Derived Results | Derivation | Input Conditions | Output Equation | Physical Meaning | |------------|------------------|-----------------|------------------| | Transitivity Proof | $A \leftrightarrow C$, $B \leftrightarrow C$ | $A \leftrightarrow B$ | Justifies thermometer reading | | Temperature Existence | Existence of reference system | $T_A = T_B$ for equilibrium | Defines temperature as state function | | Linear Scale | Two reference points (273.15 K, 373.15 K) | $T(X) = 273.15 + 100 \cdot \dfrac{X - X_1}{X_2 - X_1}$ | Calibrates any thermometer | ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Conversion Across Scales **Problem:** A food reactor operates at **150 °C**. Express this temperature in **(a) Kelvin**, **(b) Fahrenheit**, **(c) Rankine**. ### Solution: **Part (a): Kelvin** $$T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15 = 150 + 273.15$$ $$\boxed{T = 423.15 \text{ K}}$$ **Part (b): Fahrenheit** $$T(^{\circ}\text{F}) = \dfrac{9}{5} \, T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 32 = \dfrac{9}{5} \times 150 + 32 = 270 + 32$$ $$\boxed{T = 302 \, ^{\circ}\text{F}}$$ **Part (c): Rankine** $$T(^{\circ}\text{R}) = T(^{\circ}\text{F}) + 459.67 = 302 + 459.67$$ $$\boxed{T = 761.67 \, ^{\circ}\text{R}}$$ **Verification using direct conversion:** $$T(\text{K}) = \dfrac{5}{9} \, T(^{\circ}\text{R}) = \dfrac{5}{9} \times 761.67 = 423.15 \text{ K} \;\; \checkmark$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Transitivity of Thermal Equilibrium (Zeroth Law Schematic) ```mermaid graph LR sysA["SYSTEM A
Temperature TA"] sysB["SYSTEM B
Temperature TB"] sysC["SYSTEM C
Reference Body
Temperature TC"] wall1["Diathermic Wall 1
Heat Exchange Allowed"] wall2["Diathermic Wall 2
Heat Exchange Allowed"] wall3["Adiabatic Outer Shell
No Heat Loss to Surroundings"] thermo["Mercury Thermometer
TC Known"] result["TA = TB = TC
Thermal Equilibrium Established"] sysA --- wall1 sysC --- wall1 sysB --- wall2 sysC --- wall2 sysC --- thermo sysA -. eq1 .- sysC sysB -. eq2 .- sysC sysA -. eq3 .- sysB thermo --> result ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Temperature Measurement System ```mermaid graph TD inputA["Food Sample A
Unknown Temperature"] inputB["Food Sample B
Unknown Temperature"] reference["Reference System C
Known Calibration"] stage1["Stage 1: Establish
Thermal Contact
Diathermic Wall"] stage2["Stage 2: Allow Heat
Transfer Until
Equilibrium"] stage3["Stage 3: Read
Thermometric
Property X"] stage4["Stage 4: Apply
Empirical
Formula T of X"] stage5["Stage 5: Convert
to Standard
Scale Kelvin"] output1["Output: TA in Kelvin"] output2["Output: TB in Kelvin"] compare["Comparison:
TA equals TB
Zeroth Law Verified"] inputA --> stage1 reference --> stage1 stage1 --> stage2 stage2 --> stage3 stage3 --> stage4 stage4 --> stage5 stage5 --> output1 inputB --> stage1 reference --> stage1 stage5 --> output2 output1 --> compare output2 --> compare ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Flowchart — Consequence Chain of the Zeroth Law ```mermaid graph TD law["Zeroth Law of
Thermodynamics"] consequence1["Existence of
Temperature T"] consequence2["Transitivity of
Thermal Equilibrium"] consequence3["Thermometer
Calibration Possible"] consequence4["Empirical and
Absolute Scales"] application1["Food Process
Design Calculations"] application2["Heat Exchanger
Sizing"] application3["Pasteurization
and Sterilization"] application4["Cold Chain
Management"] law --> consequence1 law --> consequence2 consequence1 --> consequence3 consequence2 --> consequence3 consequence3 --> consequence4 consequence4 --> application1 consequence4 --> application2 consequence4 --> application3 consequence4 --> application4 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified] **State and explain the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics. Why is it called the "Zeroth" Law?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Statement:** The Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics states that *if two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other.* $$A \leftrightarrow C \quad \text{and} \quad B \leftrightarrow C \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \leftrightarrow B$$ **Explanation:** This law establishes the existence of a fundamental property called **temperature** that determines whether two systems will exchange heat when placed in thermal contact. It justifies the use of thermometers — a third body — to compare temperatures of two systems without bringing them into direct contact. **Why "Zeroth":** It was formulated after the First and Second Laws were already well established. However, logically it is a prerequisite for them. To preserve the numbering of existing laws, it was designated as the "Zeroth" Law (by Ralph Fowler in 1931). **(1 mark for statement, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for naming origin)** --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified] **Define the following terms with one example each:** **(a)** Thermal Equilibrium **(b)** Diathermic Wall **(c)** Adiabatic Wall **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **(a) Thermal Equilibrium:** A state in which two systems in thermal contact with each other experience no net exchange of heat energy, and consequently share the same temperature. - *Example:* A metal spoon left in a hot cup of tea eventually reaches the same temperature as the tea. **(b) Diathermic Wall:** A boundary that allows heat transfer between the two systems it separates. - *Example:* The metallic wall of a heat exchanger. **(c) Adiabatic Wall:** A boundary that does not permit any heat transfer across it; it thermally isolates the system from its surroundings. - *Example:* The insulating walls of a thermos flask (vacuum flask). **(1 mark each)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B Module 3 questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Students must answer **ONE out of TWO** full questions. Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### ❓ Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** Derive the concept of temperature as a state function using the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics. Show that thermal equilibrium between two systems implies equality of a unique scalar quantity. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A platinum resistance thermometer has a resistance of **90.00 Ω** at the ice point (0 °C) and **115.50 Ω** at the steam point (100 °C). When inserted into a milk pasteurization vat, the resistance reads **108.36 Ω**. Calculate the temperature of the milk in **(i) °C** and **(ii) Kelvin**. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Answer — Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks) **Step 1:** Let two systems $A$ and $B$ be in thermal contact through a diathermic wall. They will reach a state where no net heat flows between them — this is thermal equilibrium. **(1 mark)** **Step 2:** For thermal equilibrium to be a well-defined physical state, there must exist some measurable property that is equal between the two systems. Call this property $\theta$. For systems $A$ and $B$: $$\theta_A = \theta_B$$ This equality is **necessary and sufficient** for thermal equilibrium. **(2 marks)** **Step 3:** Introduce a third system $C$ (the thermometer/reference body). By the Zeroth Law, if $A$ is in equilibrium with $C$ and $B$ is in equilibrium with $C$, then $A$ and $B$ are in equilibrium with each other: $$\theta_A = \theta_C \quad \text{and} \quad \theta_B = \theta_C \Rightarrow \theta_A = \theta_B$$ **(2 marks)** **Step 4:** Since this scalar quantity $\theta$ is uniquely determined by the state of the system and does not depend on the path (it is well-defined at equilibrium), it qualifies as a **state function**. We identify this property as the **temperature** $T$. **(2 marks)** --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks) **Given:** - $R_{\text{ice}} = 90.00 \text{ Ω}$ at $T_{\text{ice}} = 273.15 \text{ K}$ - $R_{\text{steam}} = 115.50 \text{ Ω}$ at $T_{\text{steam}} = 373.15 \text{ K}$ - $R_{\text{milk}} = 108.36 \text{ Ω}$ (unknown $T$) **Formula:** $$T = T_{\text{ice}} + (T_{\text{steam}} - T_{\text{ice}}) \cdot \dfrac{R - R_{\text{ice}}}{R_{\text{steam}} - R_{\text{ice}}}$$ **Step 1:** Substitute values: $$T = 273.15 + (373.15 - 273.15) \cdot \dfrac{108.36 - 90.00}{115.50 - 90.00}$$ **Step 2:** Simplify: $$T = 273.15 + 100 \cdot \dfrac{18.36}{25.50}$$ **Step 3:** Compute fraction: $$\dfrac{18.36}{25.50} = 0.7200$$ **Step 4:** Final temperature: $$T = 273.15 + 100 \times 0.7200 = 273.15 + 72.00$$ **Part (i) Answer:** $$\boxed{T(^{\circ}\text{C}) = 72.00 \, ^{\circ}\text{C}}$$ **Part (ii) Answer:** $$\boxed{T(\text{K}) = 345.15 \text{ K}}$$ **Verification:** This matches HTST pasteurization temperature of milk (72 °C / 345.15 K), confirming the answer is realistic. **(1 mark for units, 1 mark for logical flow, rest for steps)** --- ### ❓ Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]` **(a)** Explain in detail the physical significance of the Zeroth Law. How does it enable the construction of a thermometer and the definition of a temperature scale? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Convert the following temperatures used in food processing: **(i)** Freezer storage temperature: **$-18 \, ^{\circ}\text{C}$** to Kelvin and Fahrenheit. **(ii)** Baking oven temperature: **$220 \, ^{\circ}\text{C}$** to Rankine. **(iii)** Refrigerated transport: **$4 \, ^{\circ}\text{C}$** to Fahrenheit. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Answer — Question B #### Part (a) — Physical Significance (7 Marks) **1. Foundation of Temperature:** The Zeroth Law establishes that **temperature** is a measurable, universal property that determines the direction of spontaneous heat flow between bodies. **(1 mark)** **2. Transitivity of Thermal Equilibrium:** It ensures that if two bodies are both at the same temperature as a third reference body, they must be at the same temperature as each other. This logical property is essential for consistent temperature comparison. **(2 marks)** **3. Thermometer Construction:** A thermometer acts as the "third body" — a small, calibrated reference system. We bring it into thermal contact with the body whose temperature is to be measured. By the Zeroth Law, when the thermometer reaches equilibrium, it shares the temperature of the body. The thermometer reading (via some thermometric property like mercury expansion or electrical resistance) is therefore a direct indicator of the body's temperature. **(2 marks)** **4. Temperature Scale Definition:** By fixing reproducible reference points (ice point at 273.15 K, steam point at 373.15 K, triple point of water at 273.16 K) and assigning numerical values, we construct a quantitative temperature scale. The Zeroth Law guarantees that any two thermometers calibrated at these points will agree on the temperature of any third body. **(2 marks)** --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Conversions (7 Marks) **Part (i):** Convert $-18 \, ^{\circ}\text{C}$ to Kelvin and Fahrenheit. To Kelvin: $$T(\text{K}) = -18 + 273.15 = 255.15 \text{ K}$$ To Fahrenheit: $$T(^{\circ}\text{F}) = \dfrac{9}{5} \times (-18) + 32 = -32.4 + 32 = -0.4 \, ^{\circ}\text{F}$$ $$\boxed{-18 \, ^{\circ}\text{C} = 255.15 \text{ K} = -0.4 \, ^{\circ}\text{F}}$$ **Part (ii):** Convert $220 \, ^{\circ}\text{C}$ to Rankine. First to Kelvin: $$T(\text{K}) = 220 + 273.15 = 493.15 \text{ K}$$ Then to Rankine: $$T(^{\circ}\text{R}) = \dfrac{9}{5} \times T(\text{K}) = \dfrac{9}{5} \times 493.15 = 887.67 \, ^{\circ}\text{R}$$ $$\boxed{220 \, ^{\circ}\text{C} = 887.67 \, ^{\circ}\text{R}}$$ **Part (iii):** Convert $4 \, ^{\circ}\text{C}$ to Fahrenheit. $$T(^{\circ}\text{F}) = \dfrac{9}{5} \times 4 + 32 = 7.2 + 32 = 39.2 \, ^{\circ}\text{F}$$ $$\boxed{4 \, ^{\circ}\text{C} = 39.2 \, ^{\circ}\text{F}}$$ **Verification:** The well-known fact that $4 \, ^{\circ}\text{C}$ is the temperature of maximum density of water, often cited as approximately 39 °F — confirms our result. **(2 + 3 + 2 marks distribution)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Forgetting unit conversion:** Marks are routinely deducted if temperature is left in °C instead of K in heat balance problems. Always convert to **Kelvin** for thermodynamic calculations. > 2. **Mis-stating the Zeroth Law:** Many students write "heat flows from hotter to colder" — this is the **Second Law** (Clausius statement), NOT the Zeroth. The Zeroth Law is about the **transitivity of equilibrium**, not heat flow direction. > 3. **Confusing "Zeroth" with "Zero":** It is not a "Law of Zero" — it is the **foundational** (number zero) law. > 4. **Skipping step-by-step work in derivations:** KTU examiners expect every algebraic step shown. Skipping intermediate steps leads to **loss of 2–3 marks** in 7-mark sub-parts. > 5. **Incorrect reference points:** Triple point of water is **273.16 K** (NOT 273.15 K); ice point is **273.15 K**. Mixing these up costs a mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Notes — Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics** - ⭐ **Statement:** If $A \leftrightarrow C$ and $B \leftrightarrow C$, then $A \leftrightarrow B$. - ⭐ **Named by:** Ralph Fowler (1931); logically precedes First and Second Laws. - ⭐ **Establishes:** The existence and physical meaning of **temperature**. - ⭐ **Enables:** Construction of thermometers and definition of temperature scales. - ⭐ **Transitivity:** The mathematical/logical backbone of the law. - ⭐ **Reference points to memorize:** - Triple point of water: **273.16 K** - Ice point: **273.15 K** - Steam point: **373.15 K** - ⭐ **Key conversions:** - $T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$ - $T(^{\circ}\text{F}) = \dfrac{9}{5} \, T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 32$ - $T(^{\circ}\text{R}) = T(^{\circ}\text{F}) + 459.67 = \dfrac{9}{5} \, T(\text{K})$ - ⭐ **Temperature is a STATE FUNCTION** — depends only on the current state, not the path. - ⭐ **Diathermic walls** allow heat flow; **adiabatic walls** do not. - ⭐ **Always use Kelvin** for thermodynamic calculations in KTU exams. - ⭐ **Food Engineering Applications:** Pasteurization, sterilization, refrigeration, drying, cold-chain logistics — all depend on accurate, transitive temperature measurement rooted in the Zeroth Law. - ⭐ **Distinguish from Second Law:** Zeroth = existence of temperature; Second = direction of heat flow.

topics.title: First Law of Thermodynamics: Internal Energy, Enthalpy, Heat Capacity; First Law for Cyclic, Non-Flow, and Flow Processes; Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT302 - FOOD THERMODYNAMICS AND PROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** First Law of Thermodynamics: Internal Energy, Enthalpy, Heat Capacity; First Law for Cyclic, Non-Flow, and Flow Processes; Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 First Law of Thermodynamics — The Energy Conservation Master Law

The **First Law of Thermodynamics** is the formal statement of the **Law of Conservation of Energy** as applied to thermodynamic systems. It states that *energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another or transferred between a system and its surroundings.*

Mathematically (differential form):

$$dE_{system} = \delta Q - \delta W$$

where $\delta Q$ is the infinitesimal heat transferred **to** the system and $\delta W$ is the infinitesimal work done **by** the system. The use of the symbol $\delta$ (instead of $d$) denotes that heat and work are **path functions**, whereas $E_{system}$ is a **point function**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The First Law is universally applicable — to closed vessels (batch food processing), open pipes (continuous pasteurization), and even to cyclic heat engines/refrigerators. Every food process calculation in industry ultimately rests on this single law.

---

### 1.2 Internal Energy (U)

**Formal Definition:** *Internal Energy* is the total energy contained within a thermodynamic system, excluding the kinetic and potential energies of the system as a whole. It includes the translational, rotational, vibrational kinetic energies of all molecules, the potential energy of molecular interactions (bonds), and the energy of electrons and nuclei.

For a pure substance, internal energy is a **state function**, depending only on the state variables (typically two, e.g., $T$ and $V$, or $T$ and $P$).

$$U = f(T, V) \quad \text{or} \quad U = f(T, P)$$

A small change in internal energy:

$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

> [!NOTE]
> For an **ideal gas**, internal energy depends *only on temperature*: $U = f(T)$. For real substances (e.g., supercritical CO₂ in food extraction), internal energy also depends on pressure/volume.

---

### 1.3 Enthalpy (H)

**Formal Definition:** *Enthalpy* is a thermodynamic property defined as the sum of internal energy and the flow work (pressure–volume product):

$$H = U + PV$$

A differential change:

$$dH = dU + d(PV) = dU + PdV + VdP$$

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of internal energy as the *quiet* energy stored inside a substance (molecular motion, bonds). Enthalpy is the *total useful heat content* when that substance is at a given pressure — it includes the internal energy PLUS the energy required to "make room" for the substance at that pressure (the $PV$ flow work). This is exactly why enthalpies are tabulated for reactions (heat of reaction) and phase changes (latent heat) at constant pressure — they directly tell us how much heat must be supplied/removed at constant $P$.

> [!NOTE]
> In food processing, **enthalpy** is the workhorse property because most industrial operations (boiling, pasteurization, evaporation, drying with steam heating) occur at *constant pressure*.

---

### 1.4 Heat Capacity (C)

**Formal Definition:** *Heat capacity* is the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of a unit mass (or unit mole) of a substance by **one degree** (1 K or 1 °C).

$$C = \frac{\delta Q}{dT}$$

Two principal forms exist:

1. **Heat Capacity at Constant Volume ($C_v$):** the heat required per unit temperature rise when volume is held constant.

$$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V = \left(\frac{\delta Q}{dT}\right)_V$$

2. **Heat Capacity at Constant Pressure ($C_p$):** the heat required per unit temperature rise when pressure is held constant.

$$C_p = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P = \left(\frac{\delta Q}{dT}\right)_P$$

**Crucial relation for ideal gases:**

$$C_p - C_v = R \quad \text{(per mole basis)}$$

$$c_p - c_v = \frac{R}{M} \quad \text{(per unit mass basis)}$$

where $R$ is the universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K) and $M$ is the molar mass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For liquid water (food systems), $c_p \approx 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ and $c_v \approx 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ are nearly equal because liquids are nearly incompressible (the $PV$ work is negligible).

**Mean Heat Capacity vs. Instantaneous Heat Capacity:**

$$\bar{C}_p = \frac{\int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p \, dT}{T_2 - T_1} \quad \text{(Mean / Average)}$$

$$C_p = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^3 \quad \text{(Instantaneous, empirical form)}$$

---

### 1.5 Forms of the First Law — Preview

The First Law takes **three different mathematical forms** depending on the type of process:

| Process Type | First Law Form | Constant of System Mass |
|---|---|---|
| Cyclic Process | $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$ | Closed loop |
| Non-Flow Process (Closed System) | $Q = \Delta U + W$ | Fixed mass |
| Flow Process (Open System / SFEE) | $\Delta H + \Delta KE + \Delta PE = Q - W_s$ | Mass crosses boundary |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to *state and apply* the First Law to a specific food-process scenario. Memorizing the correct form for each case is essential for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Energy — The Molecular Energy Reservoir

Internal energy of a pure substance encompasses all microscopic energies:

- **Sensible internal energy** — energy associated with molecular motion (translation, rotation, vibration); a function of temperature.
- **Latent internal energy** — energy associated with phase changes (melting, vaporization, sublimation); changes at constant temperature.
- **Chemical internal energy** — energy stored in chemical bonds (relevant for reactions, Maillard browning in food).
- **Nuclear energy** — usually excluded (not relevant to food processing).

For a **change** in internal energy between two states:

$$\Delta U = U_2 - U_1 = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_v \, dT \quad \text{(at constant V, or ideal gas)}$$

For an **ideal gas**:

$$\Delta U = m \, c_v \, (T_2 - T_1) = n \, C_v \, (T_2 - T_1)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** Internal energy is the energy you "have inside"; enthalpy is internal energy *plus* the energy to push back the atmosphere to make room for the system at constant pressure.

---

### 2.2 Enthalpy — The Constant-Pressure Heat Content

Since most food processes operate at constant pressure (atmospheric or slightly above), enthalpy change is the natural measure of heat transfer:

$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(PV)$$

For an **ideal gas** at constant pressure:

$$\Delta H = m \, c_p \, (T_2 - T_1) = n \, C_p \, (T_2 - T_1)$$

For a **phase change** at constant $T$ and $P$ (e.g., water → steam at 100 °C):

$$\Delta H = m \, \lambda \quad \text{(latent heat)}$$

For a **chemical reaction** at standard conditions:

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \sum \nu_i H_{f,\text{products}}^\circ - \sum \nu_j H_{f,\text{reactants}}^\circ$$

---

### 2.3 Heat Capacity — Deep Dive

#### 2.3.1 True (Instantaneous) vs. Mean Heat Capacity

| Type | Definition | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| True $C_p$ | $C_p = dQ/dT$ at a specific $T$ | Differential analysis, exact computation |
| Mean $\bar{C}_p$ | Average over $T_1$ to $T_2$ | Quick calculations over wide $T$ ranges |

#### 2.3.2 Polygene (Polynomial) Form for Gases

Gases (especially combustion gases, N₂, O₂, CO₂, steam — all important in food drying/sterilization) have $C_p$ as a polynomial in $T$:

$$C_p = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^3 \quad \text{(J/mol·K)}$$

Typical constants (relevant to food-industry gases):

| Gas | $a$ | $b \times 10^3$ | $c \times 10^6$ | $d \times 10^9$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| N₂ | 29.00 | −0.2199 | 0.5723 | −2.871 |
| O₂ | 25.68 | 12.98 | −3.851 | 0.3950 |
| CO₂ | 22.26 | 59.01 | −33.71 | 10.14 |
| H₂O(g) | 33.46 | 0.6900 | 0.0752 | −0.0992 |
| Air | 29.06 | −0.1570 | 0.8080 | −2.873 |

Heat required for a temperature rise from $T_1$ to $T_2$:

$$Q = n \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p \, dT = n \left[ a(T_2 - T_1) + \frac{b}{2}(T_2^2 - T_1^2) + \frac{c}{3}(T_2^3 - T_1^3) + \frac{d}{4}(T_2^4 - T_1^4) \right]$$

#### 2.3.3 Solids & Liquids (Food Systems)

For solids and liquids, $C_p \approx C_v$ because the volume change is negligible. Approximate values:

| Substance | $c_p$ (kJ/kg·K) |
|---|---|
| Water (liquid) | **4.18** |
| Ice | 2.09 |
| Steam (100 °C) | 2.01 |
| Air | 1.00 |
| Stainless steel (food equipment) | 0.50 |
| Dry food (cereals, grains) | 1.5 – 2.0 |
| Fats & oils | 1.7 – 2.0 |
| Sugars (solid) | 1.25 |

---

### 2.4 The Three Forms of the First Law — Detailed Analysis

#### 2.4.1 First Law for a Cyclic Process

A **cyclic process** returns the system to its initial state. Since all properties are point functions, the net change over a cycle is zero.

$$\oint dU = 0 \implies \oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$$

> **Physical meaning:** The net heat transfer into the system over a complete cycle equals the net work done by the system. This is the principle of operation of all **heat engines** and **refrigerators** (e.g., the refrigeration cycle in cold storage of foods).

#### 2.4.2 First Law for a Non-Flow Process (Closed System)

A **non-flow (closed) process** has a fixed mass; no mass crosses the boundary, but energy (heat, work) can.

$$Q = \Delta U + W$$

$$Q - W = U_2 - U_1$$

Work done by the system on the surroundings can be of various types:

$$W = W_{PV} + W_{shaft} + W_{electrical} + \dots$$

For a **quasi-static (reversible) process** with $P-V$ work only:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Food application example:** A sealed autoclave (batch retort) used for canning foods is a closed system — no mass enters or leaves, but heat is added and $P\,dV$ work may be done on the contents.

#### 2.4.3 First Law for a Flow Process (Open System) — The Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)

A **flow process** has continuous mass transfer across the boundary (e.g., fluid flowing through a pipe, food passing through a heat exchanger).

For a **steady-state, steady-flow** process (most industrial cases):

$$\Delta H + \frac{1}{2}\Delta v^2 + g\Delta z = Q - W_s$$

where:
- $\Delta H = H_2 - H_1$ — change in enthalpy (kJ/kg)
- $\frac{1}{2}\Delta v^2$ — change in kinetic energy (kJ/kg)
- $g\Delta z$ — change in potential energy (kJ/kg)
- $Q$ — heat transferred to the fluid per unit mass (kJ/kg)
- $W_s$ — **shaft work** done by the fluid (e.g., pump, turbine) per unit mass (kJ/kg)

**Common simplified forms of the SFEE:**

1. **Adiabatic nozzle** ($Q = 0$, $W_s = 0$, $\Delta z \approx 0$):

$$H_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2} = H_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2}$$

2. **Adiabatic turbine** ($Q = 0$, $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$):

$$W_s = H_1 - H_2 \quad (\text{work extracted})$$

3. **Adiabatic compressor / pump** ($Q = 0$, $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$):

$$W_s = H_2 - H_1 \quad (\text{work input})$$

4. **Adiabatic throttling valve** ($Q = 0$, $W_s = 0$, $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$):

$$H_1 = H_2 \quad (\text{isenthalpic process})$$

5. **Heat exchanger / boiler / condenser** ($W_s = 0$, $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$):

$$Q = \Delta H = H_2 - H_1$$

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = U + PV$ | Definition of enthalpy |
| 2 | $C_p - C_v = R$ | Ideal gas, molar basis |
| 3 | $c_p - c_v = R/M$ | Ideal gas, per unit mass |
| 4 | $\Delta U = m c_v \Delta T$ | Ideal gas, any process |
| 5 | $\Delta H = m c_p \Delta T$ | Ideal gas, any process |
| 6 | $Q = m \int C_p \, dT$ | Heat for temperature change |
| 7 | $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$ | First Law, cyclic |
| 8 | $Q = \Delta U + W$ | First Law, closed system |
| 9 | $W = \int P \, dV$ | Reversible boundary work |
| 10 | $\Delta H + \frac{1}{2}\Delta v^2 + g\Delta z = Q - W_s$ | SFEE (general) |
| 11 | $W_s = -\Delta H$ | Adiabatic turbine/compressor |
| 12 | $H_1 = H_2$ | Throttling process |
| 13 | $Q = \Delta H$ | Heat exchanger (no work) |
| 14 | $\bar{C}_p = \frac{\int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p \, dT}{T_2 - T_1}$ | Mean heat capacity |
| 15 | $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ | Specific heat ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse $\Delta U = mc_v\Delta T$ with $\Delta H = mc_v\Delta T$. Remember — internal energy change uses $C_v$, enthalpy change uses $C_p$ for ideal gases. This error costs 1–2 marks consistently.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food canning (retort processing):** Closed system First Law gives the heat required to raise canned food from ambient to sterilization temperature.
- **Milk pasteurization (HTST):** Flow process SFEE computes the heating/holding/cooling energy, allowing plant energy balance.
- **Steam-jacketed cookers:** Boiler enthalpy minus condensate enthalpy yields the heat supplied per kg of steam.
- **Refrigerated cold storage:** Cyclic First Law applied to the vapor-compression cycle gives COP (coefficient of performance).
- **Spray drying:** Adiabatic expansion of hot air through nozzles; SFEE gives exit air temperature/humidity.
- **Freeze drying (lyophilization):** Sublimation enthalpy (latent heat of sublimation) governs the design heat load on the sublimation chamber.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of $C_p - C_v = R$ for an Ideal Gas

We start with the definitions:

$$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

$$C_p = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 1:** Express enthalpy in terms of internal energy and $PV$:

$$H = U + PV$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate $H$ with respect to $T$ at constant $P$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_P + \left(\frac{\partial (PV)}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 3:** For an ideal gas, $U$ depends only on $T$ (Joule's law), so $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_P = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V = C_v$.

**Step 4:** Using the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$ for $n$ moles, at constant $P$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial (PV)}{\partial T}\right)_P = \left(\frac{\partial (nRT)}{\partial T}\right)_P = nR$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back:

$$C_p = C_v + nR$$

**Step 6:** On a **per-mole basis** ($n = 1$):

$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = R}$$

On a **per-unit-mass basis** (divide both sides by molar mass $M$):

$$\boxed{c_p - c_v = \frac{R}{M}}$$

> **Engineering insight:** This identity is the bridge between constant-volume and constant-pressure heat capacities. For monatomic ideal gases, $C_v = \frac{3}{2}R$, so $C_p = \frac{5}{2}R$ and $\gamma = 5/3 = 1.67$. For diatomic gases, $C_v = \frac{5}{2}R$, so $C_p = \frac{7}{2}R$ and $\gamma = 7/5 = 1.4$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)

**Step 1:** Apply the **First Law to an open system** over a time interval $dt$:

$$\delta Q - \delta W = dE_{system}$$

**Step 2:** For a control volume with mass flow, the energy balance includes mass flow terms. The **total energy** of the flowing fluid is $E = U + \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + mgz$ per unit mass.

**Step 3:** Considering mass $\dot{m}$ entering at section 1 with enthalpy $H_1$ (per kg) and leaving at section 2 with enthalpy $H_2$, and applying **steady state** ($dE_{system}/dt = 0$):

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_s = \dot{m}\left[(H_2 - H_1) + \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$$

**Step 4:** Divide through by $\dot{m}$ to get the **SFEE on a unit-mass basis**:

$$\boxed{\Delta H + \frac{1}{2}\Delta v^2 + g\Delta z = Q - W_s}$$

where each term is per unit mass.

**Step 5:** For the special case of a **throttling valve** (a small porous plug):
- $Q = 0$ (adiabatic, fast process)
- $W_s = 0$ (no shaft work)
- $\Delta v^2 \approx 0$ (negligible velocity change)
- $\Delta z \approx 0$ (small device)

This reduces to:

$$H_1 = H_2 \quad \text{(isenthalpic)}$$

> **Food-engineering insight:** Throttling is what happens when pressurized liquid food (e.g., concentrated juice, milk concentrate) is suddenly released through a valve — the enthalpy remains constant, but pressure drops and some liquid flashes into vapor (flash evaporation), cooling the remaining liquid. This is the principle behind **flash cooling** of pasteurized milk and **vacuum cooling** of food products.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process → First Law Form → Work / Heat

| Process Type | System | First Law Form | Work Term | Heat Term |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Isothermal** (ideal gas) | Closed | $Q = W$ (since $\Delta U = 0$) | $W = \int P\,dV = nRT\ln(V_2/V_1)$ | $Q = W$ |
| **Isochoric** (constant $V$) | Closed | $Q = \Delta U$ | $W = 0$ | $Q = m c_v \Delta T$ |
| **Isobaric** (constant $P$) | Closed | $Q = \Delta U + W = \Delta H$ | $W = P\Delta V$ | $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ |
| **Isentropic** (reversible adiabatic) | Closed | $Q = 0$, $W = -\Delta U$ | $W = -\Delta U$ | $Q = 0$ |
| **Adiabatic Throttling** | Open (SFEE) | $H_1 = H_2$ | $W_s = 0$ | $Q = 0$ |
| **Adiabatic Turbine** | Open (SFEE) | $W_s = H_1 - H_2$ | $W_s = H_1 - H_2$ | $Q = 0$ |
| **Adiabatic Compressor** | Open (SFEE) | $W_s = H_2 - H_1$ | $W_s = H_2 - H_1$ | $Q = 0$ |
| **Adiabatic Nozzle** | Open (SFEE) | $H_1 + v_1^2/2 = H_2 + v_2^2/2$ | $W_s = 0$ | $Q = 0$ |
| **Heat Exchanger** | Open (SFEE) | $Q = \Delta H$ | $W_s = 0$ | $Q = \Delta H$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Study strategy:** Before plugging numbers into any First Law problem, *first identify the system type* (cyclic / non-flow / flow) and *the specific process* (isothermal, isobaric, adiabatic, etc.). Then select the appropriate simplified equation from this table.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Heat Required to Heat Milk in a Pasteurizer

**Problem:** Milk (treated as water, $c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K) flows through an HTST pasteurizer at a steady rate of 2,000 kg/h. It enters at 4 °C and leaves at 72 °C. The process operates at essentially constant pressure. Determine (a) the heat duty of the heater section in kW, and (b) the change in internal energy of the milk per hour.

**Step 1:** Identify process — **steady flow, isobaric**, $\Delta KE = \Delta PE \approx 0$, $W_s = 0$.

Apply SFEE: $Q = \Delta H = m c_p \Delta T$

**Step 2:** Compute $\Delta T$:

$$\Delta T = 72 - 4 = 68 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3:** Convert mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = 2000 \text{ kg/h} = \frac{2000}{3600} \text{ kg/s} = 0.5556 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4:** Compute heat duty:

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.5556 \times 4.18 \times 68$$

$$\dot{Q} = 157.94 \text{ kW} \approx 158 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5:** Change in internal energy per hour:
For liquids, $c_v \approx c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K.

$$\Delta U/\text{hour} = m \cdot c_v \cdot \Delta T = 2000 \times 4.18 \times 68$$

$$\Delta U/\text{hour} = 568{,}480 \text{ kJ/h} \approx 158 \text{ kWh}$$

> **Sanity check:** $Q = \Delta H$ for an isobaric process. $\Delta U = Q - W = Q - P\Delta V$. For an incompressible liquid, $\Delta V \approx 0$, so $W \approx 0$ and $\Delta U \approx Q$. Both numerical answers (~158) agree, confirming the consistency.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Air Compressor (Adiabatic)

**Problem:** Air ($\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K) enters an adiabatic compressor at 100 kPa, 300 K, and exits at 1000 kPa. Assuming the process is reversible (isentropic), find (a) exit temperature, and (b) work input per kg.

**Step 1:** Isentropic relation for ideal gas:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute exponent:

$$\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{1.4 - 1}{1.4} = 0.2857$$

**Step 3:** Pressure ratio:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{1000}{100} = 10$$

**Step 4:** Exit temperature:

$$T_2 = 300 \times (10)^{0.2857} = 300 \times 1.9307 = 579.2 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5:** Heat capacity at constant pressure:

$$c_p = \frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{1.4 \times 0.287}{0.4} = 1.0045 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

**Step 6:** Work input (adiabatic, $Q = 0$, $W_s = H_2 - H_1$):

$$w_s = c_p (T_2 - T_1) = 1.0045 \times (579.2 - 300) = 1.0045 \times 279.2$$

$$w_s = 280.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> **Engineering insight:** This is the *ideal* (isentropic) work. A real compressor has isentropic efficiency $\eta_c < 1$, so the actual work is $w_{actual} = w_s / \eta_c$, typically $1.2$ to $1.5$ times the ideal value.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Energy Flow in a Steady-Flow Open System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Inlet 1<br/>H1, v1, z1, m_dot] --> CV((Control Volume<br/>Open System))
    Q[Heat Transfer<br/>Q] -->|To system| CV
    CV -->|From system| QOUT[Q loss / 0]
    WS[Shaft Work<br/>Ws] -->|By system on surroundings| CV

```

> **Engineering reading:** The SFEE essentially states: *Energy entering with mass + Heat in + Work in = Energy leaving with mass + Heat out + Work out*. For steady state, all flows are balanced.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Define **enthalpy**. Show that for an ideal gas, the change in enthalpy depends only on temperature change.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Enthalpy is a thermodynamic property defined as:

$$H = U + PV$$

It represents the total heat content of a system at constant pressure and is measured in kJ or kJ/kg.

**Derivation for ideal gas (2 marks):**

For an ideal gas, $PV = nRT$, so:

$$H = U + nRT$$

Differentiating:

$$dH = dU + nR\,dT$$

From the First Law for a closed system undergoing a constant-volume process: $dU = nC_v\,dT$. Substituting:

$$dH = nC_v\,dT + nR\,dT = n(C_v + R)\,dT = nC_p\,dT$$

Hence:

$$\Delta H = nC_p\Delta T$$

For an ideal gas, $\Delta H$ depends **only on the temperature change**, independent of the actual process path. ∎

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Distinguish between **heat capacity at constant pressure ($C_p$)** and **heat capacity at constant volume ($C_v$)**. State the relationship between them for an ideal gas.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | $C_v$ (Constant Volume) | $C_p$ (Constant Pressure) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Heat required for 1 K rise at constant $V$ | Heat required for 1 K rise at constant $P$ |
| Path | Closed, rigid vessel | Open or piston-cylinder at constant $P$ |
| Energy used | Increases $U$ only | Increases $U$ AND does $P\Delta V$ work |
| Magnitude | Smaller | **Larger** |
| Formula (per mole) | $C_v = (\partial U/\partial T)_V$ | $C_p = (\partial H/\partial T)_P$ |

**Relationship for ideal gas (1 mark):**

$$C_p - C_v = R \quad \text{(per mole)}$$

$$c_p - c_v = \frac{R}{M} \quad \text{(per unit mass)}$$

where $R$ is the universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K) and $M$ is the molar mass. ∎

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** State the **First Law of Thermodynamics** for:
   (i) a cyclic process
   (ii) a non-flow (closed) process
   (iii) a steady-flow process.

Write the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** and reduce it to the special forms applicable to a **nozzle**, a **throttling valve**, and a **turbine**, clearly stating the assumptions made in each case. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A food-processing plant uses steam at 200 kPa (saturated, $H_g = 2706$ kJ/kg) flowing at 500 kg/h through a throttling valve to a lower-pressure steam line at 101.325 kPa ($H_f = 419$ kJ/kg, $H_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg). Assuming the valve is adiabatic and the kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible, determine: (i) the enthalpy of the throttled steam, (ii) the quality of the wet steam at the exit, and (iii) the temperature of the exit steam. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Statement of First Law for three cases (3.5 Marks):**

(i) **Cyclic process** — system returns to its initial state after a complete cycle. Since internal energy is a point function, $\oint dU = 0$. The First Law gives:

$$\boxed{\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W}$$

The net heat transfer to the system over a cycle equals the net work done by the system.

(ii) **Non-flow (closed system) process** — mass is fixed within the boundary; only heat and work cross the boundary:

$$\boxed{Q = \Delta U + W}$$

or in differential form: $\delta Q = dU + \delta W$. The work term includes all forms (boundary, shaft, electrical).

(iii) **Steady-flow (open system) process** — mass flows continuously through the control volume:

$$\boxed{\Delta H + \frac{1}{2}\Delta(v^2) + g\Delta z = Q - W_s}$$

where each term is per unit mass (kJ/kg), $W_s$ is shaft work.

**SFEE Special Forms (3.5 Marks):**

| Device | Assumptions | Reduced SFEE |
|---|---|---|
| **Nozzle** | $Q = 0$, $W_s = 0$, $\Delta z \approx 0$ | $H_1 + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2} = H_2 + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2}$ |
| **Throttling Valve** | $Q = 0$, $W_s = 0$, $\Delta v^2 \approx 0$, $\Delta z \approx 0$ | $H_1 = H_2$ |
| **Turbine** | $Q = 0$, $\Delta v^2 \approx 0$, $\Delta z \approx 0$ | $W_s = H_1 - H_2$ |

For all three, "adiabatic" is the common assumption ($Q = 0$). For nozzle and throttle, no shaft work ($W_s = 0$) is also assumed. For nozzle, KE matters because the fluid accelerates; for throttle, KE is negligible because the device is small and velocities remain low.

---

**(b) Throttling Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** Saturated steam at $P_1 = 200$ kPa, $H_1 = H_g = 2706$ kJ/kg. Exit pressure $P_2 = 101.325$ kPa.

**Step 1 — Apply SFEE for throttling (2 marks):**

For a throttling valve: $H_1 = H_2$ (isenthalpic).

$$H_2 = 2706 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Determine exit state and quality (3 marks):**

At $P_2 = 101.325$ kPa (≈ 1 atm), $H_f = 419$ kJ/kg and $H_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg.

Since $H_f < H_2 < (H_f + H_{fg})$, the exit steam is a **wet (saturated) mixture**.

Quality $x$ is found from:

$$H_2 = H_f + x \cdot H_{fg}$$

$$2706 = 419 + x(2257)$$

$$x = \frac{2706 - 419}{2257} = \frac{2287}{2257} = 1.0133$$

This indicates that the exit steam is **slightly superheated** (the enthalpy exceeds the saturated vapor enthalpy). In strict thermodynamic terms, the simple wet-steam formula does not strictly apply for $x > 1$; instead, superheated-steam tables should be used. For this KTU-level problem, we accept the near-saturation result with quality just above 1 (the steam is essentially saturated with trace superheat).

**Step 3 — Temperature at exit (2 marks):**

At 101.325 kPa, the saturation temperature of water is $T_{sat} = 100$ °C. Since the steam is essentially at saturation, the exit temperature is:

$$T_2 \approx 100 \text{ °C} = 373 \text{ K}$$

**Final Answers:** $H_2 = 2706$ kJ/kg, quality $\approx 1.01$ (trace superheat), $T_2 \approx 100$ °C.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-A):**
> (1) Students often forget to clearly state the **assumptions** before reducing the SFEE — this alone is worth 1.5 marks.
> (2) Many wrongly write $H_2 - H_1 = W_s$ for a throttle; the correct equation is $H_1 = H_2$ because *both* $Q$ and $W_s$ are zero, not just $Q$.
> (3) In part (b), students sometimes apply the wet-steam quality formula when $H_2 > H_g$, leading to $x > 1$. This signals superheated steam — recognize the limit.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define **internal energy** and **enthalpy** for a thermodynamic system. Show analytically that for one mole of an ideal gas, $C_p - C_v = R$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A batch retort (closed vessel) containing 250 kg of food ($c_v = 3.6$ kJ/kg·K) is heated from an initial temperature of 30 °C to a sterilization temperature of 121 °C. The vessel has a rigid metal mass of 80 kg with $c_v = 0.48$ kJ/kg·K. Determine the **total internal energy change** of the system (food + vessel) and the **heat supplied**, assuming the vessel volume is essentially constant (negligible boundary work). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions and Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Internal energy (1 mark):** Internal energy $U$ is the energy stored within a substance at the molecular level — the sum of all kinetic (translational, rotational, vibrational) and potential (intermolecular, chemical bond) energies of the molecules, **excluding** the bulk kinetic and potential energies of the system as a whole. It is a state function.

**Enthalpy (1 mark):** Enthalpy is defined as:

$$H = U + PV$$

It combines internal energy with the flow work needed to displace the surroundings at constant pressure. $H$ is a state function, particularly useful for analyzing constant-pressure processes.

**Derivation of $C_p - C_v = R$ (5 Marks):**

For 1 mole of an ideal gas, $PV = RT$.

**Step 1:** Differentiate the definition of enthalpy:

$$dH = dU + d(PV) = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

**Step 2:** For an ideal gas, the internal energy depends only on temperature (Joule's Law):

$$dU = C_v\,dT$$

**Step 3:** The ideal gas law gives, on differentiation:

$$P\,dV + V\,dP = R\,dT$$

**Step 4:** Substituting into Step 1:

$$dH = C_v\,dT + R\,dT = (C_v + R)\,dT$$

But by definition, $dH = C_p\,dT$ at constant pressure. Therefore:

$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = R}$$

(For $n$ moles: $C_p - C_v = nR$; for per unit mass: $c_p - c_v = R/M$.)

---

**(b) Retort Heating Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Food mass: $m_f = 250$ kg, $c_{v,f} = 3.6$ kJ/kg·K
- Vessel mass: $m_v = 80$ kg, $c_{v,v} = 0.48$ kJ/kg·K
- $T_1 = 30$ °C, $T_2 = 121$ °C
- Constant volume → negligible boundary work ($W = 0$)

**Step 1 — Temperature rise (1 mark):**

$$\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 121 - 30 = 91 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Internal energy change of food (1.5 marks):**

$$\Delta U_{food} = m_f \cdot c_{v,f} \cdot \Delta T = 250 \times 3.6 \times 91$$

$$\Delta U_{food} = 81{,}900 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Internal energy change of vessel (1.5 marks):**

$$\Delta U_{vessel} = m_v \cdot c_{v,v} \cdot \Delta T = 80 \times 0.48 \times 91$$

$$\Delta U_{vessel} = 3{,}494.4 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Total internal energy change (1 mark):**

$$\Delta U_{total} = \Delta U_{food} + \Delta U_{vessel} = 81{,}900 + 3{,}494.4$$

$$\boxed{\Delta U_{total} = 85{,}394.4 \text{ kJ} \approx 85.4 \text{ MJ}}$$

**Step 5 — Heat supplied (2 marks):**

For a constant-volume closed system with negligible boundary work:

$$Q = \Delta U + W = \Delta U + 0$$

$$\boxed{Q = \Delta U_{total} = 85{,}394.4 \text{ kJ} \approx 85.4 \text{ MJ}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-B):**
> (1) Many students forget to include the **vessel mass** in the energy balance. In retort design, the metal shell absorbs a non-trivial amount of heat — neglecting it loses 2 marks.
> (2) Some students use $c_p$ instead of $c_v$ for the food. Since the retort is a *closed, rigid vessel* (constant volume), the correct property is $c_v$. However, for liquids and solids, $c_p \approx c_v$, so this is a minor issue.
> (3) Some students apply the steady-flow equation to a batch retort. The retort is a **closed (non-flow) system** — using SFEE is incorrect and loses full credit for the system analysis.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3:**

- [ ] **First Law:** Energy is conserved; $\delta Q = dU + \delta W$ (closed system).
- [ ] **Three forms of First Law:** Cyclic ($\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$), Non-flow ($Q = \Delta U + W$), Steady Flow (SFEE: $\Delta H + \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta v^2 + g\Delta z = Q - W_s$).
- [ ] **Internal energy** = molecular KE + PE of all molecules inside a system.
- [ ] **Enthalpy** = $U + PV$; convenient for constant-$P$ processes.
- [ ] **$C_p - C_v = R$** (per mole) for ideal gases.
- [ ] **For ideal gases:** $\Delta U = m c_v \Delta T$; $\Delta H = m c_p \Delta T$ — both depend *only* on temperature.
- [ ] **For liquids/solids (food systems):** $c_p \approx c_v$ because $\Delta V \approx 0$.
- [ ] **Mean heat capacity** = $\bar{C}_p = \int C_p\,dT / \Delta T$ — used when $C_p$ varies significantly with $T$.
- [ ] **Polygene form:** $C_p = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^3$ for combustion gases and steam.
- [ ] **Throttling:** isenthalpic process, $H_1 = H_2$; pressure drops, no work, no heat.
- [ ] **Turbine:** $W_s = H_1 - H_2$ (work output), adiabatic.
- [ ] **Compressor:** $W_s = H_2 - H_1$ (work input), adiabatic.
- [ ] **Nozzle:** $H_1 + v_1^2/2 = H_2 + v_2^2/2$, adiabatic.
- [ ] **Heat exchanger:** $Q = \Delta H$, no shaft work.
- [ ] **Food-process examples to memorize:**
  - Batch retort → **closed system** (non-flow)
  - HTST pasteurizer → **open system, heat exchanger** ($Q = \Delta H$)
  - Spray dryer nozzle → **open system, nozzle** (KE balance)
  - Flash cooling of milk concentrate → **open system, throttle** ($H_1 = H_2$)
  - Cold-storage refrigeration → **cyclic** (heat engine reversed)
- [ ] **Path functions** (δ): $Q$ and $W$ — depend on the process path.
- [ ] **Point/State functions** (d): $U$, $H$, $T$, $P$, $V$ — depend only on the state.

> **Final KTU Board Tip:** Always begin your solution by **(1)** identifying the system type (cyclic / closed / open), **(2)** writing the appropriate First Law equation, **(3)** listing all assumptions (adiabatic? negligible KE/PE? constant $P$ or $V$?), and **(4)** substituting given data. This structured approach automatically earns 2–3 "methodology" marks even before any computation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
