> Notice that for a **first-order reaction**, the half-life is **independent of the initial concentration** $C_{A0}$. This is the diagnostic test KTU examiners use to identify a first-order reaction from data.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Arrhenius Two-Temperature Form

Starting from the Arrhenius equation at two different temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$k_1 = A\,e^{-E_a/RT_1} \quad \text{and} \quad k_2 = A\,e^{-E_a/RT_2}$$

**Step 1**: Take the natural logarithm of both expressions.

$$\ln k_1 = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R\,T_1} \quad ; \quad \ln k_2 = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R\,T_2}$$

**Step 2**: Subtract the first from the second.

$$\ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = -\frac{E_a}{R\,T_2} + \frac{E_a}{R\,T_1}$$

**Step 3**: Combine.

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 4**: Put over a common denominator.

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 T_2}\right)$$

This is the working formula for KTU numerical problems involving temperature dependence.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-style): Activation Energy from Two-Temperature Data

**Problem**:
The rate constant of a food-quality degradation reaction is $k_1 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ min⁻¹ at $T_1 = 298$ K and $k_2 = 7.0 \times 10^{-3}$ min⁻¹ at $T_2 = 318$ K. Calculate the activation energy $E_a$.

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: List known values.

$$k_1 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{min}^{-1}, \quad T_1 = 298\,\text{K}$$
$$k_2 = 7.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{min}^{-1}, \quad T_2 = 318\,\text{K}$$
$$R = 8.314\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 2**: Apply the two-temperature Arrhenius equation.

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 T_2}\right)$$

**Step 3**: Compute the LHS.

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{7.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.8$$

$$\ln(2.8) = 1.0296$$

**Step 4**: Compute the bracket on the RHS.

$$T_2 - T_1 = 318 - 298 = 20\,\text{K}$$

$$T_1 T_2 = 298 \times 318 = 94\,764\,\text{K}^2$$

$$\frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 T_2} = \frac{20}{94\,764} = 2.111 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5**: Solve for $E_a$.

$$E_a = \frac{R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(T_2 - T_1)/(T_1 T_2)}$$

$$E_a = \frac{8.314 \times 1.0296}{2.111 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a = \frac{8.560}{2.111 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\;E_a \approx 40\,549\,\text{J·mol}^{-1} \approx 40.55\,\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}\;}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: First-Order Conversion Time

**Problem**:
Vitamin C in orange juice degrades by first-order kinetics. Initial concentration $C_{A0} = 50$ mg/100 mL. After 20 days, the concentration drops to 30 mg/100 mL. Find: (a) rate constant $k$, (b) time for 75% conversion.

**Solution**:

**Part (a)**: Use $C_A = C_{A0}\,e^{-k\,t}$.

$$30 = 50\,e^{-k \times 20}$$

$$e^{-20k} = 0.6$$

$$-20k = \ln(0.6) = -0.5108$$

$$\boxed{\;k = 0.02554\,\text{day}^{-1}\;}$$

**Part (b)**: For 75% conversion, $X_A = 0.75$, so $1 - X_A = 0.25$.

$$-\ln(1 - X_A) = k\,t$$

$$-\ln(0.25) = 0.02554\,t$$

$$t = \frac{1.3863}{0.02554}$$

$$\boxed{\;t \approx 54.3\,\text{days}\;}$$

---

## 3.6 Determining Order from Experimental Data — Differential Method

If concentration vs. time data is given, plot $\ln(-r_A)$ vs. $\ln(C_A)$:

$$\ln(-r_A) = \ln k + n\,\ln C_A$$

The **slope = order $n$**, intercept = $\ln k$.

If $\ln(-r_A)$ is independent of $\ln C_A$ (horizontal line), then $n = 0$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip**: If half-life data is given, use the **method of half-lives**:
> - If $t_{1/2}$ is independent of $C_{A0}$ → first order
> - If $t_{1/2} \propto 1/C_{A0}$ → second order
> - If $t_{1/2} \propto 1/C_{A0}^{n-1}$ → nth order
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Tree of Chemical Reactions (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Chemical Reactions]
    rootB[Phase Basis]
    rootC[Molecularity Basis]
    rootD[Order Basis]
    rootE[Reversibility Basis]
    rootF[Mechanism Basis]
    rootG[Thermal Basis]

    rootA --> rootB
    rootA --> rootC
    rootA --> rootD
    rootA --> rootE
    rootA --> rootF
    rootA --> rootG

    rootB --> ph1[Homogeneous]
    rootB --> ph2[Heterogeneous]

    rootC --> m1[Unimolecular]
    rootC --> m2[Bimolecular]
    rootC --> m3[Termolecular]

    rootD --> o0[Zero Order]
    rootD --> o1[First Order]
    rootD --> o2[Second Order]
    rootD --> oF[Fractional Order]

    rootE --> rev1[Reversible]
    rootE --> rev2[Irreversible]

    rootF --> mech1[Elementary]
    rootF --> mech2[Non Elementary]

    rootG --> th1[Exothermic]
    rootG --> th2[Endothermic]
    rootG --> th3[Athermic]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Rate Equation

```mermaid
graph LR
    inpA[Concentration CA] --> concT[Concentration Term fCA]
    inpB[Concentration CB] --> concT
    inpT[Temperature T] --> tempT[Arrhenius Term kT]
    preA[Pre exponential A] --> tempT
    preE[Activation Energy Ea] --> tempT

    concT --> mult[Multiplier]
    tempT --> mult
    mult --> outP[Reaction Rate minus rA]
    outP --> reactor[Reactor Design]
    reactor --> opt1[Yield Optimisation]
    reactor --> opt2[Selectivity Control]
    reactor --> opt3[Energy Efficiency]
```

---

## 4.3 Order vs. Molecularity Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Reaction] --> q1{Known stoichiometry?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Mechanism single-step?}
    q1 -- No --> q3[Determine experimentally]
    q2 -- Yes --> elem[Elementary]
    q2 -- No --> nonelem[Non Elementary]
    elem --> eq1[Order EQUALS Molecularity]
    nonelem --> eq2[Order MAY DIFFER from Molecularity]
    q3 --> eq2
```

---

## 4.4 Process Flow: From Rate Law to Reactor Performance (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    b1[Input: Stoichiometry] --> b2[Identify type of reaction]
    b2 --> b3[Determine order from data]
    b3 --> b4[Apply Arrhenius for temperature]
    b4 --> b5[Integrated rate law]
    b5 --> b6[Reactor material balance]
    b6 --> b7[Design equation for CSTR PFR Batch]
    b7 --> b8[Predict conversion and residence time]
    b8 --> b9[Scale up to plant]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the standard KTU-recommended **problem-solving workflow** for any reactor-design question. Mastering this flow is worth 14 marks in ESE.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (Each carries 3 marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]

**Distinguish between molecularity and order of a chemical reaction. Why can order be fractional but molecularity cannot?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Parameter | Molecularity | Order |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Number of reactant molecules colliding in an elementary step | Sum of exponents in the experimentally determined rate law |
| Source | Stoichiometry of elementary step | Experiment |
| Possible values | Integer (1, 2, 3) only | Any real number (0, 1, ½, 2, …) |

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for source difference, 1 mark for why fractional molecularity is impossible)**

**Reason for the difference (1 mark)**: Molecularity refers to the number of discrete molecules that *must* physically collide — it is meaningless to have "half a molecule" collide. Order, however, is an empirical exponent fitted to concentration data and may be a non-integer due to complex multi-step mechanisms (e.g., $H_2 + Br_2$ has order 1.5 w.r.t. $Br_2$).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 1]

**Differentiate between elementary and non-elementary reactions with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

**Elementary reaction** (1 mark): Occurs in a single step; rate law can be written directly from the stoichiometric equation. Example: $2NO_2 \rightarrow 2NO + O_2$, with $-r_{NO_2} = k\,C_{NO_2}^2$.

**Non-elementary reaction** (1 mark): Proceeds through multiple steps involving intermediates; rate law must be determined experimentally and does not match stoichiometry. Example: $2N_2O_5 \rightarrow 4NO_2 + O_2$ follows first-order kinetics despite 2 molecules of $N_2O_5$ in stoichiometry.

**Key distinguishing point (1 mark)**: In elementary reactions, order equals molecularity; in non-elementary reactions, they generally differ.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (Each carries 14 marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of suitable examples, classify chemical reactions based on **(i)** phase behaviour, **(ii)** order of reaction, **(iii)** molecularity, and **(iv)** reversibility.

**(b) [7 marks)** A food-quality degradation reaction has a rate constant $k_1 = 1.8 \times 10^{-3}$ min⁻¹ at 303 K and $k_2 = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}$ min⁻¹ at 323 K. Calculate the activation energy $E_a$ in kJ/mol.

---

### Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Phase behaviour (2 marks):**
- **Homogeneous** — single phase; example: esterification of acetic acid with ethanol in liquid phase.
- **Heterogeneous** — multiple phases; example: catalytic hydrogenation of oil using solid Ni catalyst and gaseous $H_2$.

**(ii) Order of reaction (2 marks):**
- **Zero order** — rate independent of concentration. Example: $r = k$ (e.g., surface-saturated catalysis).
- **First order** — rate $\propto C_A$. Example: microbial inactivation, radioactive decay.
- **Second order** — rate $\propto C_A^2$ or $C_A C_B$. Example: $2NO_2 \rightarrow 2NO + O_2$.
- **Fractional order** — example: $H_2 + Br_2 \rightarrow 2HBr$, with $r \propto C_{Br_2}^{1/2}$.

**(iii) Molecularity (1.5 marks):**
- **Unimolecular**: $A \rightarrow P$ (e.g., radioactive decay).
- **Bimolecular**: $A + B \rightarrow P$ (e.g., $H_2 + I_2 \rightarrow 2HI$).
- **Termolecular**: $A + B + C \rightarrow P$ (rare; example: $2NO + O_2 \rightarrow 2NO_2$).

**(iv) Reversibility (1.5 marks):**
- **Reversible**: $A \rightleftharpoons P$ — reaction reaches equilibrium (e.g., esterification).
- **Irreversible**: $A \rightarrow P$ — proceeds to completion (e.g., combustion of methane).

---

**Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

Given:
- $k_1 = 1.8 \times 10^{-3}$ min⁻¹ at $T_1 = 303$ K
- $k_2 = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}$ min⁻¹ at $T_2 = 323$ K
- $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹

**Step 1 (1 mark)**: Apply the two-temperature Arrhenius equation:

$$E_a = R \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) \cdot \frac{T_1 T_2}{T_2 - T_1}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks)**: Compute $k_2/k_1$:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.5$$

$$\ln(2.5) = 0.9163$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks)**: Compute $T_1 T_2$ and $T_2 - T_1$:

$$T_1 T_2 = 303 \times 323 = 97\,869\,\text{K}^2$$

$$T_2 - T_1 = 323 - 303 = 20\,\text{K}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks)**: Substitute:

$$E_a = 8.314 \times 0.9163 \times \frac{97\,869}{20}$$

$$E_a = 8.314 \times 0.9163 \times 4893.45$$

$$E_a = 8.314 \times 4483.84$$

$$\boxed{\;E_a \approx 37\,280\,\text{J·mol}^{-1} \approx 37.28\,\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}\;}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks)**: Conclusion — A modest activation energy of ~37 kJ/mol indicates the reaction is mildly temperature-sensitive, consistent with food-quality deterioration kinetics.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, June 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Define the **rate of reaction**. Derive the integrated rate expression for a **first-order reaction** in a constant-volume batch reactor, starting from $-r_A = kC_A$.

**(b) [7 marks]** A milk pasteurization process follows first-order microbial inactivation kinetics with $k = 2.5$ min⁻¹ at 72 °C. If the initial microbial count is $10^6$ CFU/mL, determine:
- (i) the count after 2 minutes,
- (ii) the time required to achieve 99% inactivation (i.e., 2-log reduction).

---

### Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The rate of a chemical reaction is defined as the change in concentration of a species per unit time. For a reactant $A$:

$$-r_A = -\frac{dC_A}{dt} \quad [\text{mol L}^{-1} \text{s}^{-1}]$$

The negative sign denotes disappearance of $A$.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

For a first-order reaction in a constant-volume batch reactor:

$$-r_A = k\,C_A$$

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k\,C_A$$

**Step 1** (1 mark): Rearrange by separating variables.

$$-\frac{dC_A}{C_A} = k\,dt$$

**Step 2** (1 mark): Integrate from $t = 0$, $C_A = C_{A0}$ to $t = t$, $C_A = C_A$.

$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} -\frac{dC_A}{C_A} = \int_{0}^{t} k\,dt$$

**Step 3** (1 mark): Evaluate.

$$-(\ln C_A - \ln C_{A0}) = k\,t$$

**Step 4** (1 mark): Simplify.

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_{A0}}{C_A}\right) = k\,t$$

**Step 5** (1 mark): Final form.

$$\boxed{\;C_A = C_{A0}\,e^{-k\,t}\;}$$

Or equivalently, in terms of conversion $X_A$:

$$-\ln(1 - X_A) = k\,t$$

---

**Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

Given:
- $k = 2.5$ min⁻¹ (first-order)
- $N_0 = 10^6$ CFU/mL

**(i) Count after $t = 2$ min (3.5 marks):**

$$C_A = C_{A0}\,e^{-k\,t}$$

$$C_A = 10^6 \times e^{-2.5 \times 2}$$

$$C_A = 10^6 \times e^{-5}$$

$$e^{-5} = 0.006738$$

$$\boxed{\;C_A \approx 6738\,\text{CFU/mL}\;}$$

**(ii) Time for 99% inactivation (3.5 marks):**

For 99% inactivation, $X_A = 0.99$, so $1 - X_A = 0.01$.

$$-\ln(1 - X_A) = k\,t$$

$$-\ln(0.01) = 2.5\,t$$

$$4.6052 = 2.5\,t$$

$$\boxed{\;t \approx 1.842\,\text{min}\;}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $-r_A$** — students often write $r_A = dC_A/dt$ without the minus sign, losing 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Using °C instead of K** — every temperature in Arrhenius MUST be in Kelvin. Converting 72 °C to 345 K is a compulsory step; missing it costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Conflating order and molecularity** — these are not synonyms. Examiners explicitly look for the distinction in 3-mark questions.
> 4. **Writing rate law for non-elementary reactions from stoichiometry** — this is the #1 trap. Always state *"rate law must be determined experimentally"* for non-elementary reactions.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — if $k$ is in min⁻¹, time must be in minutes. Mixing units (e.g., $k$ in s⁻¹ and $t$ in min) is a 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Not mentioning intermediates in non-elementary reactions** — these are the key markers the examiner scans for.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Chemical Reaction Engineering (CRE)** integrates kinetics, reactor design, and analysis.
- **Homogeneous** reactions have one phase; **heterogeneous** reactions have multiple phases with interfacial reaction.
- **Rate of reaction** is defined as the change in moles (or concentration) per unit time per unit volume: $-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$.
- The **rate equation** has two parts: a **concentration-dependent term** $f(C)$ and a **temperature-dependent term** $k(T)$.
- The **Arrhenius equation** $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ captures the temperature dependence; the **two-temperature form** is essential for KTU numericals.
- **Order** is empirical (sum of exponents in rate law); **Molecularity** is mechanistic (number of colliding molecules).
- Order can be **zero, fractional, or negative**; Molecularity is always a **positive integer (1, 2, or 3)**.
- For **elementary reactions**: order = molecularity, and rate law = stoichiometry.
- For **non-elementary reactions**: order $\neq$ molecularity in general; rate law must be determined experimentally.
- **First-order half-life** is independent of initial concentration: $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k$.
- For **food engineering**: pasteurization, vitamin degradation, and enzyme inactivation are typically first-order; Maillard browning can be zero or fractional order.
- **Universal gas constant** $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ is non-negotiable in Arrhenius problems.
- **Use SI units everywhere**: temperature in K, $E_a$ in J/mol (or kJ/mol), $k$ in s⁻¹ or min⁻¹ as appropriate.
- A reaction with $E_a \approx 40$–$60$ kJ/mol approximately **doubles in rate per 10 K** temperature rise near ambient conditions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactors: Batch Reactor, Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR), Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT302 - FOOD THERMODYNAMICS AND PROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reactors: Batch Reactor, Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR), Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Chemical Reactors in Food Processing

In the food industry, almost every unit operation that involves transformation of raw materials into finished products — brewing, fermentation, dairy processing, pasteurization, enzymatic hydrolysis, hydrogenation of oils, and baking — relies fundamentally on **chemical reactors**. A chemical reactor is a vessel (or system of vessels) in which a controlled chemical or biochemical reaction takes place, converting feed materials (reactants) into desired products.

In Food Process Calculations, we are concerned with three ideal reactor models that form the backbone of all reaction engineering analysis. These three reactors are mathematically tractable, conceptually simple, and provide the basis upon which more complex real reactor behavior is interpreted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 4):**
> The student must be able to **derive, compare, and apply the design equations for a Batch Reactor, a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR), and a Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** for single reactions of various orders, and use these equations to calculate reactor volume, conversion, and residence time under steady-state and unsteady-state conditions.

## 1.2 The Three Ideal Reactor Types — At a Glance

| Reactor Type | Mode of Operation | Mixing Pattern | Typical Food Industry Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Batch Reactor** | Discontinuous / Unsteady-state | Perfect mixing (composition uniform in space, varies with time) | Cheese making, batch fermentation, baking, brewing |
| **CSTR (Mixed Flow)** | Continuous / Steady-state | Perfect mixing (composition uniform in space AND time) | Continuous fermentation, yogurt culture, waste treatment |
| **PFR (Tubular)** | Continuous / Steady-state | No axial mixing (composition varies along length, uniform radially) | Pasteurization in long tubes, continuous sterilization, plug-flow bioreactors |

## 1.3 The Batch Reactor — Intuitive Overview

A **batch reactor** is a closed vessel in which the reactants are charged at the beginning, the contents are mixed thoroughly throughout the reaction period, and the products are removed only when the desired conversion has been achieved. No material enters or leaves the reactor during the reaction.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Pressure Cooker:**
Imagine a pressure cooker on a stove. You put the lentils and water in at the start (initial concentration $C_{A0}$), close the lid (no inflow or outflow), turn on the heat, and the lentils soften over time (concentration $C_A$ decreases with time). You cannot add or remove anything in between. The concentration inside the cooker is uniform at any instant (perfect mixing by boiling and convection), but it changes with time. This is exactly how a batch reactor behaves — *uniform in space, varying with time*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The batch reactor is intrinsically **unsteady-state**. It is the natural choice when the production volume is small, when flexibility is required (the same vessel used for multiple products), or when the reaction is slow.

## 1.4 The Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) — Intuitive Overview

A **CSTR** (also called a mixed flow reactor, MFR, or backmix reactor) is a continuous-flow reactor in which the contents are perfectly and instantaneously mixed by an agitator (impeller). Feed enters continuously at a constant volumetric rate, and product stream leaves continuously at the same rate, so the volume inside the reactor remains constant.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Coffee Mug with Continuous Pour:**
Imagine a mug being continuously filled with hot water from a tap, while the overflow drains out the side. After a brief moment, the water level in the mug stays constant (steady-state). Because the inflow is instantaneously mixed with the mug's contents, the overflow has the **same composition as the contents of the mug at every instant**. So if you wanted the overflow to have a specific conversion, you have to ensure the *inside* of the mug has reached that conversion. This is the defining feature of a CSTR — the **exit composition equals the composition everywhere inside**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The CSTR is **steady-state** with perfect spatial uniformity. Its main disadvantage is that, due to mixing, fresh feed is instantly diluted by partially reacted material, lowering the average reaction rate compared to a PFR.

## 1.5 The Plug Flow Reactor (PFR) — Intuitive Overview

A **Plug Flow Reactor** is a continuous-flow reactor (typically a long tube) in which the fluid flows in *plugs* — each "plug" moves down the tube as a unit, with no axial mixing between adjacent plugs, but with perfect radial mixing. The composition varies continuously along the length of the reactor, but at any axial position, the composition is uniform across the cross-section.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Piston in a Cylinder:**
Think of cars entering a single-lane tunnel. Each car moves forward as a single unit, and no car can overtake another (no axial mixing). The "age" or "conversion" of each car changes as it travels through the tunnel — cars near the entrance have just entered, cars near the exit are about to leave with high conversion. Similarly, in a PFR, each fluid element acts like a tiny batch reactor, traveling along the length and converting reactants as it goes. The composition profile along the tube is essentially the same as the time-profile of a batch reactor, just laid out in space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The PFR is **steady-state** with no axial mixing. For most single reactions, the PFR achieves higher conversion than a CSTR of equal volume, or equivalently, needs less volume than a CSTR to achieve the same conversion.

## 1.6 Fundamental Quantities and Conventions

The following terms are used universally in KTU questions on reactors:

- **$F_{A0}$**: Molar flow rate of reactant A entering the system (kmol/s or mol/s).
- **$F_A$**: Molar flow rate of reactant A leaving the system (kmol/s or mol/s).
- **$C_{A0}$**: Initial (inlet) concentration of A (kmol/m³).
- **$C_A$**: Concentration of A at the point of interest (kmol/m³).
- **$X_A$**: Fractional conversion of A, defined as $X_A = (F_{A0} - F_A)/F_{A0} = 1 - F_A/F_{A0} = 1 - C_A/C_{A0}$ (for constant density systems).
- **$-r_A$**: Rate of disappearance of A per unit volume (kmol/m³·s).
- **$V$**: Reactor volume (m³).
- **$v_0$**: Volumetric flow rate of feed (m³/s).
- **$\tau$ (tau)**: **Space time** = $V/v_0$ (seconds) — average time a fluid element spends in the reactor.
- **$\bar{\tau}$**: **Space time** (alternate symbol often used in Levenspiel).
- **$s$**: **Space velocity** = $v_0/V = 1/\tau$ (s⁻¹).
- **$t$**: Reaction time (for batch) or residence time (for flow reactors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant-Density Assumption (KTU Default):** For the KTU examination, unless explicitly stated otherwise, the liquid-phase systems are assumed to have **constant density** (no volume change on reaction). This makes $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ and $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ directly applicable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Mole Balance Equation

The entire design of all chemical reactors starts from a single universal principle — the **mole balance on reactant A** for a control volume:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} \text{Rate of A} \\ \text{in} \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} \text{Rate of A} \\ \text{out} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \text{Rate of A} \\ \text{generated} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{Rate of A} \\ \text{accumulated} \end{bmatrix} $$

Mathematically, for a generic volume element:

$$ F_{A0} - F_A + \int_V r_A \, dV = \frac{dN_A}{dt} $$

where $r_A$ is the rate of formation of A (negative for disappearance, so $-r_A$ is positive when A is consumed).

The way we apply this single equation to each reactor type produces all the design formulas in this module.

## 2.2 Batch Reactor — Step-by-Step Operating Principle

1. **Charge the reactor** with reactants at $t = 0$, so initial moles $N_{A0} = C_{A0} V_0$.
2. **Close the system** — no inflow or outflow ($F_{A0} = F_A = 0$).
3. **Mix perfectly** so concentration is uniform throughout the volume $V$ (assumed constant for liquid systems).
4. **React** for a time $t$ at controlled temperature and pressure.
5. **Discharge** the product mixture when the desired conversion $X_A$ is reached.

Applying the mole balance to a constant-volume batch reactor:

$$ -\frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A V \quad \Rightarrow \quad -\frac{d(C_A V)}{dt} = r_A V $$

For constant $V$:

$$ -\frac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A = -(-r_A) $$

Separating variables and integrating from $t=0$ (where $C_A = C_{A0}$) to $t = t$ (where $C_A = C_A$):

$$ t = \int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} \frac{-dC_A}{-r_A} = \int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{C_{A0} \, dX_A}{-r_A} $$

The volume required for a desired conversion is then found using the production rate (how many batches per unit time, called the *cycle time*).

## 2.3 CSTR — Step-by-Step Operating Principle

1. **Feed enters** at a constant volumetric rate $v_0$ and molar rate $F_{A0} = v_0 C_{A0}$.
2. **Perfect, instantaneous mixing** ensures the concentration everywhere inside = concentration at the exit.
3. **Reactants are consumed** at a rate $-r_A$ (evaluated at the exit concentration $C_A$).
4. **Effluent leaves** at the same volumetric rate $v_0$, with molar rate $F_A = v_0 C_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$.

Applying the steady-state mole balance (accumulation = 0):

$$ F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{A0} X_A = -r_A V $$

Rearranging:

$$ V_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{F_{A0} X_A}{-r_A} = \frac{v_0 C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A} $$

In terms of space time:

$$ \tau_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{V}{v_0} = \frac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** In the CSTR formula, $-r_A$ is evaluated at the **exit** concentration $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$, NOT at the inlet. This is the source of maximum confusion in board exams.

## 2.4 PFR — Step-by-Step Operating Principle

1. **Feed enters** at $z=0$ with molar rate $F_{A0}$ and concentration $C_{A0}$.
2. **Flow as a plug** — no axial mixing, perfect radial mixing.
3. Consider a **differential slice** of volume $dV$ at axial position $z$.
4. **Reactants disappear** at rate $-r_A$ within the slice.
5. **Effluent leaves** at $z = L$ with molar rate $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_{A,\text{final}})$.

Applying the mole balance to the differential element $dV$ at steady state:

$$ F_A \big\vert_z - F_A \big\vert_{z+dz} + r_A \, dV = 0 $$

$$ -dF_A = -r_A \, dV \quad \Rightarrow \quad dF_A = r_A \, dV $$

Expressing in terms of conversion: $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$, so $dF_A = -F_{A0} \, dX_A$:

$$ F_{A0} \, dX_A = -r_A \, dV $$

Integrating from inlet ($X_A = 0$ at $V = 0$) to outlet ($X_A = X_{A,\text{final}}$ at $V = V$):

$$ V_{\text{PFR}} = F_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_{A,\text{final}}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A} = v_0 C_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_{A,\text{final}}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A} $$

In terms of space time:

$$ \tau_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{V}{v_0} = C_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_{A,\text{final}}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight:** The PFR design equation is an **integral** (the rate changes along the length), whereas the CSTR design equation is a single-point **algebraic** evaluation. For any positive-order reaction, $\tau_{\text{PFR}} \leq \tau_{\text{CSTR}}$ for the same conversion.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE ULTIMATE KTU REACTOR DESIGN FORMULA TABLE — Memorize This**

### 2.5.1 Master Design Equations

| Reactor Type | Design Equation (Volume form) | Design Equation (Space time form) | Form Type |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Batch** | $V = v_0 \displaystyle\int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{C_{A0} \, dX_A}{-r_A}$ | $t = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{C_{A0} \, dX_A}{-r_A}$ | Integral |
| **CSTR** | $V = \dfrac{F_{A0} X_A}{-r_A \big\vert_{\text{exit}}}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A \big\vert_{\text{exit}}}$ | Algebraic |
| **PFR** | $V = F_{A0} \displaystyle\int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | $\tau = C_{A0} \displaystyle\int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | Integral |

### 2.5.2 Specialization to First-Order Reaction $A \to \text{Products}$, $-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$

| Reactor | $\tau$ or $t$ Equation | Volume for given $X_A$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Batch** | $t = \dfrac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)$ | $V = v_0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)$ |
| **CSTR** | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k} \cdot \dfrac{X_A}{1 - X_A}$ | $V = v_0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{k} \cdot \dfrac{X_A}{1 - X_A}$ |
| **PFR** | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)$ | $V = v_0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation:** For first-order reactions, $\tau_{\text{Batch}} = \tau_{\text{PFR}}$ and the CSTR requires a *larger* volume. This is a classic KTU viva question.

### 2.5.3 Specialization to Second-Order Reaction $-r_A = k C_A^2 = k C_{A0}^2 (1 - X_A)^2$

| Reactor | $\tau$ or $t$ Equation |
| :---: | :---: |
| **Batch** | $t = \dfrac{1}{k C_{A0}} \cdot \dfrac{X_A}{1 - X_A}$ |
| **CSTR** | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k C_{A0}} \cdot \dfrac{X_A}{(1 - X_A)^2}$ |
| **PFR** | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k C_{A0}} \cdot \dfrac{X_A}{1 - X_A}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation:** For second-order reactions, $\tau_{\text{Batch}} = \tau_{\text{PFR}}$ still holds, and CSTR again needs a larger volume for the same conversion.

### 2.5.4 Zero-Order Reaction $-r_A = k$

| Reactor | $\tau$ or $t$ Equation |
| :---: | :---: |
| **Batch** | $t = C_{A0} X_A / k$ |
| **CSTR** | $\tau = C_{A0} X_A / k$ |
| **PFR** | $\tau = C_{A0} X_A / k$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case:** For zero-order reactions, all three reactors give the **same** $\tau$ for the same conversion, because rate is independent of concentration.

### 2.5.5 Useful Auxiliary Formulas

| Quantity | Formula | Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Conversion** | $X_A = 1 - \dfrac{C_A}{C_{A0}}$ | For constant-density systems |
| **Space time** | $\tau = V / v_0$ | Average residence time |
| **Space velocity** | $s = v_0 / V = 1/\tau$ | Reciprocal of space time |
| **Volumetric feed rate** | $v_0 = F_{A0} / C_{A0}$ | For single-reactant systems |
| **Molar feed rate** | $F_{A0} = C_{A0} v_0$ | For liquid systems |
| **CSTR / PFR volume ratio** | $\dfrac{V_{\text{CSTR}}}{V_{\text{PFR}}} = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{X_A}{(1 - X_A)^n}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{n-1}\right)\left[(1 - X_A)^{1-n} - 1\right]}$ | Generic $n$-th order, $n \neq 1$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In food industry design, these three reactor models are used to:
- **Scale up** laboratory reaction kinetics data to plant-scale equipment.
- **Compare alternative reactor configurations** to choose the most economic one (CSTR is cheaper to build; PFR is more efficient).
- **Determine residence time** for thermal processes such as pasteurization, ensuring pathogen kill while preserving nutrients.
- **Design bioreactors** for enzyme-catalyzed reactions, fermentation, and hydrolysis.
- **Troubleshoot existing reactors** by comparing actual performance with the ideal model.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Batch Reactor Design Equation (Constant Volume)

We start from the **general mole balance equation** applied to a batch reactor.

**Step 1:** The general mole balance on reactant A over a control volume is:

$$ F_{A0} - F_A + G_A = \frac{dN_A}{dt} $$

where $G_A = \int_V r_A \, dV$ is the total rate of generation of A in the volume.

**Step 2:** Apply to a batch reactor: there is no inflow or outflow, so $F_{A0} = 0$ and $F_A = 0$. The reaction is assumed uniform throughout the volume (perfect mixing), so $r_A$ is constant in space:

$$ 0 - 0 + r_A V = \frac{dN_A}{dt} $$

Rearranging (with $r_A = -(-r_A)$, the rate of disappearance being the positive quantity $-r_A$):

$$ -\frac{dN_A}{dt} = -r_A V $$

**Step 3:** Define $N_A = C_A V$. For a **constant volume** system (constant density, no significant volume change on mixing or reaction), $V$ is constant. Differentiating:

$$ -\frac{d(C_A V)}{dt} = -V \frac{dC_A}{dt} = -r_A V $$

Cancelling $V$:

$$ -\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -r_A $$

**Step 4:** Convert from concentration form to conversion form. Since $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$, we have $dC_A = -C_{A0} \, dX_A$. Also, $X_A = 0$ at $t = 0$ and $X_A = X_{A,\text{final}}$ at $t = t$. Substituting:

$$ -\frac{(-C_{A0} \, dX_A)}{dt} = -r_A \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{A0} \frac{dX_A}{dt} = -r_A $$

**Step 5:** Separate variables and integrate:

$$ t = \int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{C_{A0} \, dX_A}{-r_A} $$

This is the **batch reactor design equation** — it tells us the time required to achieve a given conversion $X_A$ for any rate law $-r_A = f(C_A, T)$.

**Step 6 — Specialize to first-order:** $-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$:

$$ t = \int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{C_{A0} \, dX_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)} = \frac{1}{k} \int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A} $$

$$ t = \frac{1}{k} \big[-\ln(1 - X_A)\big]_{0}^{X_A} = \frac{1}{k} \left[-\ln(1 - X_A) - 0\right] $$

$$ \boxed{\,t = \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)\,} $$

**Step 7 — Specialize to second-order:** $-r_A = k C_A^2 = k C_{A0}^2 (1 - X_A)^2$:

$$ t = \int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{C_{A0} \, dX_A}{k C_{A0}^2 (1 - X_A)^2} = \frac{1}{k C_{A0}} \int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{(1 - X_A)^2} $$

Using $\int (1 - X_A)^{-2} dX_A = (1 - X_A)^{-1}$:

$$ t = \frac{1}{k C_{A0}} \left[\frac{1}{1 - X_A} - 1\right] = \frac{1}{k C_{A0}} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A} $$

$$ \boxed{\,t = \frac{1}{k C_{A0}} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A}\,} $$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CSTR Design Equation (Steady-State)

**Step 1:** The general steady-state mole balance on A (no accumulation):

$$ F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = 0 $$

**Step 2:** Express in terms of conversion. $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$, so $F_{A0} - F_A = F_{A0} X_A$. Also $r_A = -(-r_A)$:

$$ F_{A0} X_A - (-r_A) V = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{A0} X_A = -r_A V $$

**Step 3:** Rearranging for volume:

$$ V_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{F_{A0} X_A}{-r_A} $$

**Step 4:** Since $F_{A0} = v_0 C_{A0}$ and $\tau = V/v_0$:

$$ \tau_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Step 5:** In a CSTR, due to perfect mixing, the rate $-r_A$ is constant throughout the reactor and is evaluated at the **exit conditions**, i.e., at $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$.

**Step 6 — Specialize to first-order:** $-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$:

$$ \tau = \frac{C_{A0} X_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)} = \frac{X_A}{k(1 - X_A)} $$

$$ \boxed{\,\tau_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A}\,} $$

**Step 7 — Specialize to second-order:** $-r_A = k C_A^2 = k C_{A0}^2 (1 - X_A)^2$:

$$ \tau = \frac{C_{A0} X_A}{k C_{A0}^2 (1 - X_A)^2} = \frac{X_A}{k C_{A0} (1 - X_A)^2} $$

$$ \boxed{\,\tau_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{1}{k C_{A0}} \cdot \frac{X_A}{(1 - X_A)^2}\,} $$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PFR Design Equation (Steady-State)

**Step 1:** Consider a differential element of the PFR of volume $dV$ at axial position $z$. Apply a steady-state mole balance on A:

$$ F_A \big\vert_{z} - F_A \big\vert_{z+dz} + r_A \, dV = 0 $$

**Step 2:** Using a Taylor expansion (for small $dz$):

$$ F_A \big\vert_{z+dz} \approx F_A \big\vert_{z} + \frac{dF_A}{dz} dz $$

Substituting back:

$$ F_A - \left(F_A + \frac{dF_A}{dz} dz\right) + r_A \, dV = 0 $$

$$ -\frac{dF_A}{dz} dz + r_A \, dV = 0 $$

**Step 3:** Since $dV = A_c \, dz$ (cross-sectional area times length), and $dF_A/dz$ is the gradient:

$$ -dF_A + r_A \, dV = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad dF_A = r_A \, dV $$

Note: $r_A$ is negative for disappearance, so we write:

$$ -dF_A = -r_A \, dV \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{A0} \, dX_A = -r_A \, dV $$

(using $dF_A = -F_{A0} dX_A$)

**Step 4:** Integrating from inlet ($V = 0, X_A = 0$) to outlet ($V = V, X_A = X_{A,\text{final}}$):

$$ V_{\text{PFR}} = F_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_{A,\text{final}}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A} $$

**Step 5:** Dividing both sides by $v_0$ to get space time:

$$ \tau_{\text{PFR}} = C_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A} $$

**Step 6 — Specialize to first-order:** $-r_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$:

$$ \tau = C_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)} = \frac{1}{k} \left[-\ln(1 - X_A)\right]_{0}^{X_A} $$

$$ \boxed{\,\tau_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)\,} $$

**Step 7 — Specialize to second-order:** $-r_A = k C_{A0}^2 (1 - X_A)^2$:

$$ \tau = C_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{k C_{A0}^2 (1 - X_A)^2} = \frac{1}{k C_{A0}} \left[\frac{1}{1 - X_A} - 1\right] $$

$$ \boxed{\,\tau_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{1}{k C_{A0}} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A}\,} $$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Volume Ratios for the Same Conversion

For a first-order reaction, the volume (or space time) ratio between a CSTR and a PFR at the same conversion $X_A$ is:

$$ \frac{V_{\text{CSTR}}}{V_{\text{PFR}}} = \frac{\dfrac{X_A}{1 - X_A}}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)} $$

This ratio is **always greater than 1** (except at $X_A = 0$ where it is 1, and as $X_A \to 1$ where it diverges). This is a frequently asked KTU 2-mark question.

| $X_A$ | $V_{\text{CSTR}} / V_{\text{PFR}}$ (1st order) | Interpretation |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0.50 | 1.442 | CSTR needs 44% more volume |
| 0.80 | 2.011 | CSTR needs ~2× the volume |
| 0.90 | 2.558 | CSTR needs 2.5× the volume |
| 0.95 | 3.153 | CSTR needs 3.15× the volume |
| 0.99 | 4.65 | CSTR needs 4.65× the volume |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** For high conversion, the PFR is dramatically more efficient than the CSTR. This is why continuous pasteurizers and plug-flow fermenters are preferred in food industry practice for high-conversion processes.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A first-order liquid-phase reaction $A \to B$ takes place in a CSTR and a PFR of equal volume $V = 2 \, \text{m}^3$. The volumetric flow rate is $v_0 = 0.5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{min}$, the inlet concentration is $C_{A0} = 4 \, \text{kmol}/\text{m}^3$, and the rate constant is $k = 0.3 \, \text{min}^{-1}$. Calculate the exit conversion in each reactor.

**Solution for the CSTR:**

We use the CSTR design equation: $\tau = (1/k) \cdot X_A/(1 - X_A)$.

Compute $\tau = V/v_0 = 2 / 0.5 = 4 \, \text{min}$.

Substitute: $4 = (1/0.3) \cdot X_A/(1 - X_A) \Rightarrow 1.2 = X_A/(1 - X_A)$.

Solve: $1.2(1 - X_A) = X_A \Rightarrow 1.2 - 1.2 X_A = X_A \Rightarrow 1.2 = 2.2 X_A \Rightarrow X_A = 0.545$.

**Solution for the PFR:**

We use the PFR design equation: $\tau = (1/k) \ln[1/(1 - X_A)]$.

$4 = (1/0.3) \ln[1/(1 - X_A)] \Rightarrow 1.2 = \ln[1/(1 - X_A)] \Rightarrow 1/(1 - X_A) = e^{1.2} = 3.32$.

Solve: $1 - X_A = 0.301 \Rightarrow X_A = 0.699$.

**Conclusion:** The PFR achieves a higher conversion (69.9%) than the CSTR (54.5%) for the same volume and feed conditions, illustrating the inherent advantage of plug flow over mixed flow for positive-order kinetics.

## 3.6 Series Reactor Configurations

### 3.6.1 CSTRs in Series ($N$ equal-size CSTRs)

For $N$ equal-volume CSTRs in series, each operating at steady state with the same volumetric flow rate $v_0$:

$$ \tau_i = \frac{V_i}{v_0} = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{N \, v_0} = \frac{\tau_{\text{total}}}{N} $$

For a first-order reaction, the overall conversion $X_{A,N}$ is given by:

$$ \frac{C_{A,N}}{C_{A0}} = \frac{1}{(1 + k \tau_i)^N} = \frac{1}{(1 + k \tau_{\text{total}}/N)^N} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Theorem:** As $N \to \infty$, the cascade of CSTRs behaves like a PFR. The volume of an infinite number of small CSTRs equals the volume of a single PFR for the same conversion and feed.

### 3.6.2 PFRs in Series

Multiple PFRs in series are mathematically equivalent to a single PFR of total volume equal to the sum of individual volumes (since the design equation is additive):

$$ V_{\text{total, PFR}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i $$

### 3.6.3 Mixed PFR + CSTR Combinations

For a series of one PFR followed by one CSTR (or vice versa), the conversion in each stage is computed independently using the appropriate design equation, with the exit of the upstream reactor being the inlet of the downstream reactor.
